### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Forces in a Fluid**

- ✅ **Two fundamental force categories:** Body (volume-based) and Surface (area-based) — know the distinction cold.
- ✅ **Body force formula:** $\vec{F}_b = \rho \vec{g} \, dV$ — the only one in this course is gravity.
- ✅ **Surface force decomposition:** $\sigma_{ij} = -p \delta_{ij} + \tau_{ij}$ — pressure is normal, viscous is tangential.
- ✅ **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \, (du/dy)$ — applicable **only to Newtonian fluids** (air and water qualify).
- ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure law:** $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ — linear in depth for incompressible fluid.
- ✅ **Archimedes' principle:** $F_B = \rho_f g V$ — upward; equals weight of displaced fluid.
- ✅ **Floating equilibrium:** $V_{submerged} = m / \rho_{fluid}$.
- ✅ **Total aerodynamic force integral:** $\vec{F}_{aero} = \oint_S (-p \hat{n} + \vec{\tau}_{visc}) dA$ — this is the master equation.
- ✅ **Drag-Lift decomposition:** Decompose $\vec{F}_{aero}$ along and perpendicular to $V_\infty$.
- ✅ **Standard air properties:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$ at sea-level standard conditions.
- ✅ **Non-dimensionalization:** $C_F = F / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_{ref})$ — used for $C_D$, $C_L$, $C_S$ throughout the course.
- ✅ **Always draw a free-body diagram** of the fluid element in derivations — KTU examiners reward this.
- ✅ **Dimensional homogeneity check** is a free 1-mark saver in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressibility and Incompressibility
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compressibility and Incompressibility

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

**Compressibility** is defined as the fractional change in the volume (or equivalently, the density) of a fluid element per unit change in pressure applied to it. Mathematically, the isentropic compressibility coefficient of a fluid is expressed as:

$$\tau \equiv \frac{1}{\rho}\left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial p}\right)_{s}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density, $p$ is the thermodynamic pressure, and the subscript $s$ denotes that the process is isentropic (constant entropy). The **inverse of compressibility** is known as the **bulk modulus of elasticity**, $K = \rho \, a^{2}$, where $a$ is the local acoustic (sound) speed.

**Incompressibility** is the limiting case where the density of a fluid element remains effectively constant during its motion, regardless of the pressure variations it experiences. In this regime, $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} \approx 0$ and consequently the compressibility coefficient $\tau \to 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A flow is termed **incompressible** when the density variation within the flow field is less than **5%** of the free-stream density ($\Delta\rho / \rho_{\infty} < 0.05$). This practically corresponds to a free-stream Mach number $M_{\infty} < \mathbf{0.3}$. Above this threshold, compressibility effects must be explicitly resolved.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw & Syringe" Model

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a straw versus drinking water through the same straw.

- **Water through the straw:** You can suck rapidly, push and pull, and the water barely "cares" — it rushes in and out with the same density. This is **incompressible** flow behavior: the fluid does not store energy by being squashed; it simply moves.

- **Milkshake through the straw:** If you try to suck it too fast, the milkshake gets "stuck" and you feel back-pressure. If you squeeze the straw from outside, the milkshake visibly deforms in density. This is **compressible** behavior: the fluid's own volume resists rapid changes.

In the same way, **air at low vehicle speeds** (e.g., a city car at 60 km/h) behaves like the water — it flows smoothly with negligible density change. **Air at high vehicle speeds** (e.g., a Formula-1 car at 320 km/h, or a supersonic jet) behaves like the milkshake — the density changes are significant, and shock waves may form.

## 1.3 The Role of Mach Number

The single most important non-dimensional parameter governing compressibility is the **Mach Number**, $M$:

$$M \equiv \frac{V}{a}$$

where $V$ is the local flow velocity and $a$ is the local speed of sound. For standard sea-level conditions ($T = 288.15\,\text{K}$), the speed of sound is approximately **$\mathbf{a = 340.29\,\text{m/s}}$**, which corresponds to roughly **$\mathbf{1225\,\text{km/h}}$**.

| Flow Regime | Mach Number Range | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Incompressible** | $M < 0.3$ | Density variation $< 5\%$; Bernoulli valid directly |
| **Subsonic** | $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ | Density changes small but noticeable |
| **Transonic** | $0.8 \le M \le 1.2$ | Mixed subsonic/supersonic pockets; shock formation begins |
| **Supersonic** | $1.2 < M \le 5.0$ | Shock waves present; entropy rises across shocks |
| **Hypersonic** | $M > 5.0$ | Strong shock-layer heating; real-gas effects |

> [!NOTE]
> For the vast majority of **road vehicle aerodynamics** (passenger cars, trucks, buses), the freestream Mach number is well below 0.3, so the flow is treated as **incompressible**. Compressibility becomes critical only for **racing vehicles** (F1, Le Mans prototypes) and **high-speed trains / aircraft**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Density Variation Matters — The Governing Equations

The behavior of a compressible fluid is governed by the full set of **Navier–Stokes equations** coupled with the **continuity equation**, the **momentum equations**, and the **energy equation**. The key is the **continuity equation**, which for a compressible, unsteady, three-dimensional flow takes the form:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

When we apply the incompressibility assumption $\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$, this equation **collapses** to the much simpler divergence-free condition:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

This dramatic simplification is precisely why aerodynamicists treat low-speed flows as incompressible — it allows the use of potential flow theory, panel methods, and the **Bernoulli equation** in its classical form.

## 2.2 The Compressibility Cascade — Why It Matters Step by Step

1. **Step 1 — Pressure Variations Drive Density Variations:** By the isentropic relation $p/\rho^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, any change in static pressure produces a corresponding change in density. The proportionality factor is $\gamma$ (ratio of specific heats, $\gamma_{\text{air}} = 1.4$).

2. **Step 2 — Density Variations Couple to Velocity via Continuity:** Because mass must be conserved, when density drops locally (e.g., over the suction peak of a wing), velocity must rise to keep the mass flux $\rho V$ constant.

3. **Step 3 — Velocity Variations Feed Back into Pressure:** Faster flow produces lower static pressure (Bernoulli effect), which further reduces density — a feedback loop.

4. **Step 4 — At Critical Mach Number, Local Sonic Conditions Are Reached:** Even though the freestream may be subsonic, local acceleration over a curved surface (e.g., the roof of a sports car) may push $M_{\text{local}} = 1$, triggering **local transonic shock formation**.

5. **Step 5 — Beyond $M_{\infty} \approx 0.85$, Wave Drag Becomes Significant:** Shock-induced total pressure losses translate into a new drag component called **wave drag**, $C_{D,w}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for all compressibility-related problem-solving in OEAUT723. Memorize these expressions; they appear in nearly every Part B question on this module.

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mach Number | $M = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | $V$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s |
| 2 | Speed of Sound (Ideal Gas) | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $\gamma=1.4$, $R=287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$, $T$ in K |
| 3 | Isentropic Compressibility | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\rho}\left(\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial p}\right)_{s} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma p}$ | $\tau$ in $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Bulk Modulus | $K = \rho a^{2} = \gamma p$ | $K$ in Pa |
| 5 | Isentropic Process Relation | $\dfrac{p}{\rho^{\gamma}} = \text{const}$ | — |
| 6 | Critical Pressure Ratio (Choked Flow) | $\left(\dfrac{p^{*}}{p_{0}}\right) = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | For $\gamma=1.4$, ratio $\approx 0.528$ |
| 7 | Area-Mach Relation | $\dfrac{A}{A^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\!\left[\!\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\!\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)\!\right]^{\!\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | Subsonic/supersonic branch |
| 8 | Stagnation Temperature | $T_{0} = T\!\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)$ | $T_{0}$ in K |
| 9 | Stagnation Pressure | $p_{0} = p\!\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | $p_{0}$ in Pa |
| 10 | Prandtl-Glauert Correction | $C_{p,\text{comp}} = \dfrac{C_{p,\text{incomp}}}{\sqrt{1-M_{\infty}^{2}}}$ | Subsonic compressibility correction |
| 11 | Critical Mach Number | $M_{\text{cr}} \approx M_{\infty} - \dfrac{\Delta C_{p,\text{max}}}{2\,\gamma\, M_{\infty}^{3}}$ (Kuchemann) | Approximation for $M_{\text{cr}}$ |
| 12 | Density Ratio | $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \left(1+\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-1/(\gamma-1)}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:** In road-vehicle aerodynamics, the **Prandtl–Glauert correction** (Row 10) is used to correct wind-tunnel pressure coefficients measured at low Reynolds number toward true free-flight conditions at high subsonic speeds. The **area-Mach relation** (Row 7) governs the design of intake and exhaust ducts in racing vehicles.

## 2.4 The Physical Meaning of Incompressibility — A Sanity Check

Even "incompressible" fluids (like water) are technically compressible — water has a bulk modulus of about **2.2 GPa**. The reason we treat them as incompressible is that the pressures encountered in typical flows are far too small to produce measurable density changes. Air, with $K \approx 101\,\text{kPa}$ at sea level, is roughly **22,000 times more compressible** than water, yet at $M < 0.3$ we still safely treat it as incompressible for most engineering purposes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speed of Sound from First Principles

We begin with the **momentum equation** for an inviscid, adiabatic fluid in one dimension:

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -\rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

Apply a small perturbation: $p \rightarrow p + dp$, $\rho \rightarrow \rho + d\rho$, $V \rightarrow V + dV$. The linearized one-dimensional continuity equation for a sound wave is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 0$$

For an isentropic (reversible, adiabatic) process, the pressure depends only on density:

$$dp = \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_{s} d\rho$$

Combining these three relations and eliminating the velocity perturbation yields the classical wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{a^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$$

where the wave-propagation speed is identified as:

$$\boxed{\,a = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_{s}}\,}$$

For a calorically perfect ideal gas, the isentropic relation $p = \rho R T$ combined with $p/\rho^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ gives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_{s} = \gamma \frac{p}{\rho} = \gamma R T$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}\,}$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The speed of sound depends only on the *temperature* (not the pressure) of the gas. This is why, for example, the speed of sound is lower on a cold winter day at the same altitude, and why supersonic aircraft are limited by temperature, not pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Density Variation with Mach Number

Starting from the isentropic relation $p_{0}/p = (1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2})^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ and the ideal gas law $p/\rho = RT$, together with $p_{0}/\rho_{0} = RT_{0}$, we write:

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \frac{p}{p_{0}} \cdot \frac{T_{0}}{T}$$

Substituting the isentropic expressions:

$$\frac{p}{p_{0}} = \left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

$$\frac{T}{T_{0}} = \left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{1}$$

Combining the exponents:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-1/(\gamma-1)}\,}$$

For $\gamma = 1.4$, the exponent becomes $-1/0.4 = -2.5$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The "5% Density Rule" for Incompressibility

We require $\Delta\rho / \rho_{\infty} \le 0.05$. At a typical flow acceleration of $M_{\infty} = 0.3$:

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \left(1 + \frac{1.4-1}{2}(0.3)^{2}\right)^{-2.5} = (1 + 0.018)^{-2.5}$$

$$= (1.018)^{-2.5} \approx 0.9556$$

So $\Delta\rho/\rho_{0} \approx 4.44\%$, which is below the 5% threshold. At $M = 0.5$:

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \left(1 + 0.2 \cdot 0.25\right)^{-2.5} = (1.05)^{-2.5} \approx 0.882$$

So $\Delta\rho/\rho_{0} \approx 11.8\%$, which exceeds 5%. **Conclusion:** The Mach 0.3 cutoff is well-justified.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Prandtl–Glauert Compressibility Correction

For small perturbations in a 2D subsonic compressible flow, the linearized potential equation is:

$$(1 - M_{\infty}^{2})\,\phi_{xx} + \phi_{yy} = 0$$

Substituting $\xi = x \cdot \beta$, where $\beta = \sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^{2}}$, recovers Laplace's equation for an incompressible potential $\tilde{\phi}$:

$$\tilde{\phi}_{\xi\xi} + \tilde{\phi}_{yy} = 0$$

The pressure coefficient in compressible flow is related to the incompressible solution by:

$$C_{p,\text{comp}} = \frac{C_{p,\text{incomp}}}{\sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^{2}}}$$

This is the **Prandtl–Glauert rule**, valid for $0.3 < M_{\infty} < 0.7$ and for small perturbation bodies (thin airfoils, slender vehicle bodies). It blows up as $M_{\infty} \to 1$, reflecting the singular onset of transonic shock formation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Speed of Sound at Altitude

The temperature variation in the standard atmosphere below 11 km (troposphere) is:

$$T = T_{0} - L \cdot h$$

with $T_{0} = 288.15\,\text{K}$ and $L = 0.0065\,\text{K/m}$. Therefore:

$$a(h) = \sqrt{\gamma R (T_{0} - Lh)}$$

| Altitude $h$ (m) | Temperature $T$ (K) | Speed of Sound $a$ (m/s) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 288.15 | 340.29 |
| 1000 | 281.65 | 336.43 |
| 5000 | 255.65 | 319.49 |
| 11000 | 216.65 | 295.07 |

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction in $a$ with altitude means that the **critical Mach number for compressibility onset is reached at lower true airspeeds** for high-altitude flight — a key consideration for high-altitude long-endurance (HALE) UAVs but irrelevant for terrestrial vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Regime Decision Diagram

The following block diagram maps the logical decision tree that an aerodynamicist follows when classifying a flow as compressible or incompressible. It links the freestream Mach number to the appropriate governing equations and solution methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["START - Identify Flow Problem"] --> B0["Compute Freestream Velocity V_infty"]
    B0 --> C0["Compute Local Sound Speed a_infty from a = sqrt gamma R T_infty"]
    C0 --> D0["Calculate Mach Number M_infty = V_infty divided by a_infty"]
    D0 --> E0{"Is M_infty less than 0.3?"}
    E0 -- YES --> F0["INCOMPRESSIBLE REGIME"]
    E0 -- NO --> G0["COMPRESSIBLE REGIME"]
    F0 --> F1["Use Continuity eq: div V equals 0"]
    F1 --> F2["Use Bernoulli directly"]
    F2 --> F3["Solution via Panel Methods or CFD Incompressible Solver"]
    G0 --> G1{"Is M_infty less than 0.8?"}
    G1 -- YES --> H0["SUBSONIC COMPRESSIBLE"]
    H0 --> H1["Apply Prandtl Glauert Correction"]
    H1 --> H2["Solve Transonic Small Disturbance Equation"]
    G1 -- NO --> I0{"Is M_infty between 0.8 and 1.2?"}
    I0 -- YES --> J0["TRANSONIC - SHOCKS PRESENT"]
    J0 --> J1["Apply Euler Equations with Shock Capturing"]
    I0 -- NO --> K0["SUPERSONIC OR HYPERSONIC"]
    K0 --> K1["Apply Rankine Hugoniot Jump Conditions"]
    K1 --> K2["Use Method of Characteristics or Supersonic Panel Methods"]
```

## 4.2 Compressibility Coupling Block Architecture

The diagram below illustrates how the four fundamental conservation equations **couple** through the thermodynamic state variables in a compressible flow. In the incompressible limit, all couplings except the kinematic ones collapse.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THERMO["THERMODYNAMIC BLOCK"]
        T1["Ideal Gas Law p equals rho R T"]
        T2["Isentropic Relation p over rho to the gamma equals const"]
        T3["Equation of State"]
    end
    subgraph CONS["CONSERVATION BLOCK"]
        C1["Continuity: d rho by dt plus div rho V equals 0"]
        C2["Momentum: rho DV by Dt equals minus grad p plus mu Laplacian V"]
        C3["Energy: rho Dh by Dt equals d p by d t plus Phi dissipation"]
    end
    subgraph SOLVE["SOLUTION STRATEGY BLOCK"]
        S1["Incompressible: decouple via div V equals 0"]
        S2["Compressible subsonic: coupled iteration on rho p T"]
        S3["Compressible supersonic: explicit time marching with shocks"]
    end
    T3 --> C1
    T3 --> C2
    T3 --> C3
    C1 --> S1
    C1 --> S2
    C1 --> S3
    C2 --> S1
    C2 --> S2
    C2 --> S3
    C3 --> S1
    C3 --> S2
    C3 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Vehicle Speed vs Mach Number — Real-World Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pedestrian 1.4 m per s"] --> M0["M approximately 0.004"]
    B["City Car 60 km per h"] --> M1["M approximately 0.049"]
    C["Highway Car 120 km per h"] --> M2["M approximately 0.098"]
    D["Sports Car 250 km per h"] --> M3["M approximately 0.204"]
    E["F1 Car 360 km per h"] --> M4["M approximately 0.294"]
    F["TGV Train 320 km per h"] --> M5["M approximately 0.261"]
    G["Concorde 2179 km per h"] --> M6["M approximately 1.70"]
    H["SR-71 Blackbird 3529 km per h"] --> M7["M approximately 2.85"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that even an **F1 car** at full speed operates just *below* the incompressibility threshold ($M \approx 0.29$). This is why most road-vehicle aerodynamic research uses incompressible solvers — but it is also why **subtle compressibility corrections** are now being researched for next-generation hypercars (e.g., Bugatti Chiron Super Sport 300+, Hennessey Venom F5).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define compressibility. State the criterion used to classify a flow as incompressible in vehicle aerodynamics.**

**Model Answer:**

Compressibility is defined as the fractional change in density of a fluid per unit change in pressure, characterized by the coefficient $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\rho}\left(\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial p}\right)_{s}$.

A flow is classified as **incompressible** when the density variation in the flow field is less than **5%** of the freestream density. This corresponds to a freestream Mach number $M_{\infty} < \mathbf{0.3}$. Below this threshold, the compressibility correction factor $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^{2}}}$ deviates from unity by less than 2%, justifying the incompressibility assumption.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for 5% rule, 1 mark for M < 0.3 statement.)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between static temperature and stagnation temperature. Why is stagnation temperature constant in adiabatic flow?**

**Model Answer:**

The **static temperature** $T$ is the thermodynamic temperature of the fluid in its local rest frame (i.e., measured by a thermometer moving with the fluid).

The **stagnation (or total) temperature** $T_{0}$ is the temperature the fluid would attain if brought to rest isentropically. For an ideal gas:

$$T_{0} = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^{2}\right)$$

The stagnation temperature remains **constant in an adiabatic flow with no shaft work** because the **steady-flow energy equation (SFEE)** reduces to $h_{0} = h + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2} = \text{const}$, and since $h = c_{p}T$, the stagnation temperature is invariant along a streamline in the absence of heat addition and friction.

**(3 marks: 1.5 marks for definitions + equation, 1.5 marks for SFEE-based justification.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]`
**(a)** Starting from the isentropic relation $p/\rho^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, derive the expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aircraft cruises at $M_{\infty} = 0.78$ at an altitude where the ambient temperature is $T_{\infty} = 250\,\text{K}$. The maximum local Mach number on the wing's upper surface reaches $M_{\text{local}} = 0.92$. Calculate: **(i)** the local static temperature and static pressure, given $p_{\infty} = 75\,\text{kPa}$; **(ii)** whether shock formation is expected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

We begin with the definition of the speed of sound as the speed of propagation of an infinitesimal pressure disturbance in a fluid:

$$a = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_{s}}$$

For an isentropic process, $p = C \rho^{\gamma}$, where $C = p_{\text{ref}}/\rho_{\text{ref}}^{\gamma}$ is a constant.

Differentiating with respect to $\rho$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_{s} = \gamma C \rho^{\gamma - 1} = \gamma \frac{C\rho^{\gamma}}{\rho} = \gamma \frac{p}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma \frac{p}{\rho}}$$

For an ideal gas, $p = \rho R T$, so $p/\rho = RT$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}\,}$$

**Mark Distribution:** Definition of $a$ (2 marks), isentropic relation (2 marks), substitution of ideal gas law (2 marks), final boxed result (1 mark).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $M_{\infty} = 0.78$, $T_{\infty} = 250\,\text{K}$, $p_{\infty} = 75\,\text{kPa}$, $M_{\text{local}} = 0.92$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the local static temperature using the energy equation:**

Since stagnation temperature is conserved (adiabatic, no work):

$$T_{0} = T_{\infty}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M_{\infty}^{2}\right)$$

$$= 250 \times \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.78^{2}\right) = 250 \times 1.1217 = 280.42\,\text{K}$$

The local static temperature is:

$$T_{\text{local}} = \frac{T_{0}}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M_{\text{local}}^{2}} = \frac{280.42}{1 + 0.2 \times 0.92^{2}}$$

$$= \frac{280.42}{1.16928} = 239.83\,\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the local static pressure using the isentropic pressure ratio:**

$$p_{\text{local}} = p_{\infty}\left(\frac{T_{\text{local}}}{T_{\infty}}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$$

$$= 75 \times \left(\frac{239.83}{250}\right)^{3.5} = 75 \times (0.95932)^{3.5}$$

Calculating: $(0.95932)^{3.5} \approx 0.8539$. Therefore:

$$p_{\text{local}} \approx 64.04\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Assess shock formation:**

Since $M_{\text{local}} = 0.92 > 1.0$ is **not** satisfied, but $M_{\text{local}}$ is in the **transonic range** ($0.8 \le M \le 1.2$), and given that freestream Mach is 0.78, there is **high probability of normal shock formation** on the upper surface if the local Mach reaches unity. The local Mach number 0.92 indicates the flow is **just subsonic** locally, but is close enough to unity that any further acceleration (e.g., due to a flap deflection or angle-of-attack increase) will trigger a shock.

**Conclusion:** **Yes, shock formation is highly likely.** The wing is operating in the transonic regime, and the upper-surface Mach number being 0.92 means only an additional ~9% velocity increase is needed to reach sonic conditions and form a shock wave.

**Mark Distribution:** Stagnation temperature (2 marks), local temperature (2 marks), local pressure (2 marks), shock assessment (1 mark).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the Mach number. With a neat sketch, describe the five flow regimes classified by Mach number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A high-speed passenger car travels at $V = 300\,\text{km/h}$ through still air at sea-level standard conditions ($T_{\infty} = 288.15\,\text{K}$, $p_{\infty} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$). Determine: **(i)** the freestream Mach number; **(ii)** the density ratio $\rho/\rho_{0}$; **(iii)** whether compressibility effects can be neglected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Mach number** $M = V/a$ is the ratio of the flow velocity to the local speed of sound. It quantifies how fast a body moves relative to the speed at which pressure disturbances (sound waves) propagate through the fluid.

**Physical Significance:**

- It governs the **direction of propagation** of pressure disturbances. For $M < 1$, disturbances can travel upstream against the flow (subsonic regime). For $M > 1$, disturbances are confined within a Mach cone (supersonic regime).
- It determines whether the flow can be modeled as incompressible ($M < 0.3$) or requires full compressible treatment.
- It controls the formation of shock waves and the magnitude of wave drag.

**The Five Flow Regimes (with sketch description):**

| Regime | Mach Range | Sketch Description |
|---|---|---|
| Incompressible | $M < 0.3$ | Disturbances spread spherically upstream |
| Subsonic | $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ | Disturbances still propagate upstream, density changes small |
| Transonic | $0.8 \le M \le 1.2$ | Local supersonic pockets; bow shock forms blunt bodies; mixed regions |
| Supersonic | $1.2 < M \le 5.0$ | Disturbances confined in Mach cone; sharp oblique shocks on wedges |
| Hypersonic | $M > 5.0$ | Strong detached bow shock; shock layer thin; real-gas effects |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition and physical meaning (3 marks), five regimes with sketch description (4 marks).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert velocity and compute the speed of sound:**

$$V = 300\,\text{km/h} = \frac{300}{3.6} = 83.33\,\text{m/s}$$

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T_{\infty}} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288.15} = \sqrt{115{,}835} \approx 340.34\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the freestream Mach number:**

$$M_{\infty} = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{83.33}{340.34} \approx 0.245$$

**Step 3 — Compute the density ratio:**

$$\frac{\rho}{\rho_{0}} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M_{\infty}^{2}\right)^{-1/(\gamma-1)} = \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.245^{2}\right)^{-2.5}$$

$$= (1 + 0.01201)^{-2.5} = (1.01201)^{-2.5} \approx 0.9702$$

Therefore $\Delta\rho/\rho_{0} \approx 1 - 0.9702 = 0.0298$, i.e., **2.98%**.

**Step 4 — Assess compressibility:**

Since $\Delta\rho/\rho_{0} \approx 2.98\% < 5\%$ and $M_{\infty} = 0.245 < 0.3$, **compressibility effects can be safely neglected**. The flow can be modeled using the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations, Bernoulli's equation, and classical low-speed aerodynamics.

**Mark Distribution:** Velocity conversion (1 mark), sound speed (2 marks), Mach number (1 mark), density ratio (2 marks), assessment (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287$:** A surprising number of students confuse the ratio of specific heats $\gamma$ with the gas constant $R$. Remember: $\gamma$ is dimensionless; $R$ has units of J/(kg·K).
>
> 2. **Using $M < 0.3$ without mentioning the 5% rule:** Simply stating "$M < 0.3$ for incompressible flow" without the underlying $\Delta\rho < 5\%$ justification is incomplete. KTU examiners expect both.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the stagnation-temperature conservation argument:** When solving compressible flow problems, many students compute $T_{0}$ but fail to state *why* it is conserved (adiabatic, no work, steady-state). This loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Applying Prandtl–Glauert outside its validity range:** The PG correction is valid for $0.3 < M_{\infty} < 0.7$ and small perturbations only. Do NOT apply it at $M > 0.8$ or for blunt bodies.
>
> 5. **Units in $a = \sqrt{\gamma RT}$:** Always verify that the final answer for $a$ is in m/s. Common mistake: plugging in $T$ in °C instead of K, giving an erroneous speed of sound.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compressibility & Incompressibility**

- **Definition:** Compressibility = fractional density change per unit pressure change; characterized by $\tau = (\partial \rho / \partial p)_{s} / \rho$.
- **Incompressibility Criterion:** $M_{\infty} < 0.3$ AND $\Delta\rho/\rho_{\infty} < 5\%$.
- **Master Formula:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — temperature (not pressure) governs sound speed.
- **Mach Number:** $M = V/a$ — the single most important non-dimensional parameter in compressible flow.
- **Continuity Equation:**
  - Compressible: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
  - Incompressible: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$
- **Isentropic Master Relations** (must memorize):
  - $\dfrac{T_{0}}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^{2}$
  - $\dfrac{p_{0}}{p} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$
  - $\dfrac{\rho_{0}}{\rho} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$
- **Prandtl–Glauert Correction:** $C_{p,\text{comp}} = C_{p,\text{incomp}}/\sqrt{1 - M_{\infty}^{2}}$ — for thin, small-perturbation bodies at $0.3 < M < 0.7$.
- **Five Regimes:** Incompressible ($<0.3$), Subsonic ($0.3$–$0.8$), Transonic ($0.8$–$1.2$), Supersonic ($1.2$–$5.0$), Hypersonic ($>5.0$).
- **Energy Conservation:** $T_{0} = $ const along a streamline in adiabatic, no-work flow — the foundation of all compressible-flow analyses.
- **Real-World Relevance:** Most road vehicles operate at $M < 0.3$; racing vehicles and aircraft require compressible-flow treatment.
- **Numerical Benchmark:** $a_{\text{sea level}} \approx 340.3\,\text{m/s} \approx 1225\,\text{km/h}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reynolds Number (Re)** is a fundamental dimensionless quantity in fluid mechanics that quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces within a fluid subjected to relative internal motion due to varying fluid velocities. Mathematically, it represents the relative importance of these two force types and serves as a critical predictor of flow regime—whether the flow will be **laminar**, **transitional**, or **turbulent**.

In the specific context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics**, the Reynolds number characterizes the airflow over a vehicle's body, governing the behavior of the boundary layer, the onset of flow separation, the magnitude of pressure and viscous drag, and the overall aerodynamic performance of road and race vehicles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEAUT723 – Module 1):** Reynolds number is introduced as a foundational similarity parameter. Mastery of this concept is non-negotiable for solving scaling problems between full-scale vehicles and wind-tunnel models.

The Reynolds number can be expressed in three equivalent forms depending on the available fluid properties:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot L}{\mu} = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}
$$

where the symbols denote standard fluid and flow quantities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a long stick through still water. If you push it **slowly**, the water glides past the stick in smooth, organized layers—like marbles rolling in parallel tracks. The "stickiness" (viscosity) of the water dominates over the momentum (inertia) of the flow. This is **low Reynolds number** behavior.

Now push the stick **very fast**. The water no longer flows in neat layers—it churns, swirls, and forms chaotic eddies. The momentum of the water now overwhelms viscosity. This is **high Reynolds number** behavior.

**Vehicles travel at extremely high Reynolds numbers** (typically $Re \approx 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$), meaning inertia dominates overwhelmingly over viscosity. This is why:

- Streamlining is far more effective than surface polishing at reducing drag on a road car.
- Aerodynamic devices (spoilers, diffusers, vortex generators) work by manipulating inertial, not viscous, effects.
- Wind-tunnel tests must be conducted at the same Reynolds number as the real vehicle to produce meaningful results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Insight:** Reynolds number is not a property of the fluid alone, nor of the body alone—it is a property of the **interaction** between the fluid, the body, and the flow velocity. Change any one, and Re changes.

## 1.3 Physical Significance at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values in Vehicle Aerodynamics:**
>
> | Parameter | Typical Road Vehicle | Race Car (F1 / LMP) |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: |
> | Velocity ($V$) | 20 – 40 m/s | 60 – 90 m/s |
> | Characteristic Length ($L$) | 2 – 5 m | 4 – 5 m |
> | Reynolds Number ($Re$) | $10^{6}$ – $10^{7}$ | $10^{6}$ – $10^{7}$ |
> | Flow Regime | Turbulent (with laminar pockets) | Turbulent |

The characteristic length $L$ for a ground vehicle is conventionally taken as the **wheelbase length** or, more commonly in automotive practice, the **vehicle's streamwise length** (front bumper to rear bumper). For bluff bodies like trucks and buses, $L$ is sometimes taken as the **height or width** depending on the phenomenon being studied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Identifying the Two Competing Forces

When a fluid flows past a body, two fundamental force families compete:

1. **Inertial Forces ($F_i$):** These arise from the fluid's mass and acceleration. They tend to keep fluid parcels moving in their original direction, resisting changes to the flow. Inertial forces scale with **$\rho V^{2} L^{2}$**.

2. **Viscous Forces ($F_v$):** These arise from internal friction between fluid layers sliding past one another. They tend to damp motion and promote orderly, layered flow. Viscous forces scale with **$\mu V L$**.

### Step 2 — Forming the Dimensionless Ratio

Taking the ratio eliminates all dimensions (kg, m, s) and yields a pure number that compares the two effects directly:

$$
\frac{F_{i}}{F_{v}} \sim \frac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
$$

This is the **Reynolds Number**.

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation of the Ratio

- **$Re \ll 1$:** Viscous forces dominate → flow is highly damped, smooth, and creeping. Seen in micro-fluidics, lubrication films, and insect flight at small scales.
- **$Re \sim 1$:** Inertial and viscous forces are comparable → delicate balance; rarely encountered in practical vehicle flows.
- **$Re \gg 1$:** Inertial forces dominate → flow becomes unstable, eddies form, and turbulence develops. This is the **operational regime of every road and racing vehicle**.

### Step 4 — The Three Equivalent Forms

| Form | Equation | When to Use |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Density-based | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | When dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is tabulated |
| Kinematic-viscosity form | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | When kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is given |
| Mass-flow form | $Re = \dfrac{\dot{m} L}{\mu A}$ | For internal flows (ducts, pipes) |

where $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

### Step 5 — Critical Reynolds Number and Flow Regime Boundaries

For external flow over a flat plate or streamlined body, there exists a **critical Reynolds number** ($Re_{crit}$) at which the laminar boundary layer transitions to turbulent. For typical automotive geometries:

- Smooth body, clean conditions: $Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$
- Real road vehicle (with surface roughness, protuberances): $Re_{crit}$ drops significantly

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (dynamic form) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (kinematic form) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity definition | $m^{2}/s$ |
| 4 | $Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ | Critical Re for flat-plate transition | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Re_{model}}{Re_{proto}} = 1$ | Dynamic similarity condition | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{V_{m} L_{m}}{\nu_{m}} = \dfrac{V_{p} L_{p}}{\nu_{p}}$ | Wind-tunnel scaling equation | — |
| 7 | $V \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ | Inverse velocity–length scaling at fixed Re | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** Always keep velocity in **m/s** and length in **meters** to avoid conversion errors. Always convert km/h → m/s by dividing by **3.6**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reynolds number is the **single most-used similarity parameter** in vehicle aerodynamic design. Its practical applications include:

1. **Wind-Tunnel Testing:** Since full-scale testing is prohibitively expensive, scale models (typically 1:4 or 1:5) are used. To make the test data valid for the full-scale vehicle, the test Reynolds number must match that of the prototype—this is **dynamic similarity**.
2. **CFD Validation:** Computational results must be verified against experimental data taken at matched Re values.
3. **Drag Coefficient Scaling:** $C_D$ is a function of $Re$. Failing to match $Re$ between model and prototype leads to misleading drag predictions.
4. **Boundary-Layer Tripping:** Engineers deliberately add surface roughness (tape strips, dimples) to force the boundary layer turbulent at low wind-tunnel Re, preventing laminar-separation artifacts that don't exist on the real car.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number from First Principles

### Derivation A — Force Ratio Approach (Primary Definition)

**Starting point:** The Reynolds number is defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow.

**Step 1:** Write the dimensional scaling of each force.

Inertial force scales as mass × acceleration, where mass scales as $\rho L^{3}$ and acceleration scales as $V / t$. Since the characteristic time is $t \sim L / V$ (time for fluid to traverse the body), acceleration $\sim V^{2} / L$. Therefore:

$$
F_{i} \sim \rho L^{3} \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{L} = \rho V^{2} L^{2}
$$

Viscous force scales as shear stress × area, where shear stress scales as $\mu \cdot V / L$ (Newton's law of viscosity) and area scales as $L^{2}$:

$$
F_{v} \sim \mu \cdot \frac{V}{L} \cdot L^{2} = \mu V L
$$

**Step 2:** Form the ratio.

$$
Re \equiv \frac{F_{i}}{F_{v}} = \frac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify by canceling common terms.

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
$$

This completes the derivation in the dynamic-viscosity form.

**Step 4:** Convert to kinematic form by substituting $\nu = \mu / \rho$, i.e., $\mu = \rho \nu$:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\rho \nu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}
$$

**Final consolidated result:**

$$
\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}}
$$

### Derivation B — Dimensional Analysis Approach (Verification)

**Step 1:** Identify governing variables. The inertial force per unit volume $F_{i}/V_{vol}$ depends on density $\rho$, velocity $V$, characteristic length $L$, and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.

$$
\frac{F_{i}}{L^{3}} = f(\rho, V, L, \mu)
$$

**Step 2:** List the dimensions of each variable in the **M-L-T** system.

| Variable | Dimension |
| :---: | :---: |
| $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| $L$ | $L$ |
| $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |

**Step 3:** By the Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem, with 4 variables and 3 primary dimensions, we expect **one** dimensionless group.

**Step 4:** Assume the group takes the form $\rho^{a} V^{b} L^{c} \mu^{d}$ and require dimensionless. Setting up dimensional homogeneity:

$$
M^{0} L^{0} T^{0} = (M L^{-3})^{a} (L T^{-1})^{b} (L)^{c} (M L^{-1} T^{-1})^{d}
$$

**Step 5:** Equate exponents.

For $M$: $\quad a + d = 0 \implies d = -a$

For $T$: $\quad -b - d = 0 \implies b = -d = a$

For $L$: $\quad -3a + b + c - d = 0 \implies -3a + a + c + a = 0 \implies c = a$

**Step 6:** Choose $a = 1$. Then $b = 1$, $c = 1$, $d = -1$.

$$
\Pi = \rho^{1} V^{1} L^{1} \mu^{-1} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re
$$

This independently confirms the same expression.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (Standard Examination Type)

### Numerical Example 1 — Direct Calculation

**Problem:** A sedan of length $L = 4.5 \, \text{m}$ travels at $V = 108 \, \text{km/h}$ through air at standard conditions ($\rho = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$). Calculate the Reynolds number.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
V = \frac{108}{3.6} = 30 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{1.225 \times 30 \times 4.5}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
1.225 \times 30 = 36.75
$$

$$
36.75 \times 4.5 = 165.375
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by denominator:**

$$
Re = \frac{165.375}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.137 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{Re \approx 9.14 \times 10^{6}}
$$

This value comfortably exceeds the critical Reynolds number, confirming fully turbulent flow over the vehicle—typical for real-world road cars.

### Numerical Example 2 — Wind-Tunnel Scaling

**Problem:** A 1:5 scale model of a bus (prototype length $L_{p} = 12 \, \text{m}$, prototype speed $V_{p} = 90 \, \text{km/h}$) is tested in a wind tunnel using air at the same conditions as the prototype. Find the required wind-tunnel speed for dynamic similarity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model dimensions:**

$$
L_{m} = \frac{L_{p}}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Prototype velocity in SI units:**

$$
V_{p} = \frac{90}{3.6} = 25 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic similarity condition** (same Re, same fluid $\nu$):

$$
\frac{V_{m} L_{m}}{\nu} = \frac{V_{p} L_{p}}{\nu} \implies V_{m} L_{m} = V_{p} L_{p}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{m}$:**

$$
V_{m} = \frac{V_{p} L_{p}}{L_{m}} = \frac{25 \times 12}{2.4}
$$

$$
V_{m} = \frac{300}{2.4} = 125 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to km/h:**

$$
V_{m} = 125 \times 3.6 = 450 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{V_{m} = 125 \text{ m/s} = 450 \text{ km/h}}
$$

This is why **full-scale wind tunnels (or pressurized tunnels)** are sometimes needed for large-vehicle testing—at 1:5 scale, the required test velocity is impractically high for conventional tunnels.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Regime | Re Range | Flow Character | Vehicle Implication |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Creeping | $Re < 1$ | Viscous-dominated, smooth | Not applicable to vehicles |
| Laminar | $Re < 5 \times 10^{5}$ | Smooth, layered | Subsonic model-aircraft regime |
| Transitional | $5 \times 10^{5} < Re < 10^{6}$ | Mixed, intermittent | Model cars in small tunnels |
| Turbulent | $Re > 10^{6}$ | Chaotic, mixing, eddies | **All real road vehicles** |
| Fully Turbulent | $Re > 10^{7}$ | Highly chaotic, attached | Race cars at top speed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Factors Governing Reynolds Number

This block-level functional diagram maps the three primary variables that determine the Reynolds number for a vehicle in atmospheric flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[REYNOLDS NUMBER Re] --> B[Fluid Property Group]
    A --> C[Flow Kinematic Group]
    A --> D[Geometric Group]

    B --> B1[Density rho kg per cubic m]
    B --> B2[Dynamic viscosity mu Ns per sq m]
    B --> B3[Kinematic viscosity nu sq m per s]

    C --> C1[Free stream velocity V m per s]
    C --> C2[Reference frame and ground effect]

    D --> D1[Characteristic length L m]
    D --> D2[Wheelbase or streamwise length]
    D --> D3[Vehicle height or width for bluff bodies]

    A --> E[Resulting Flow Regime]
    E --> E1[Laminar when Re below 5e5]
    E --> E2[Transitional between 5e5 and 1e6]
    E --> E3[Turbulent when Re above 1e6]

    E --> F[Vehicle Aerodynamic Consequences]
    F --> F1[Boundary layer state]
    F --> F2[Pressure drag magnitude]
    F --> F3[Viscous drag magnitude]
    F --> F4[Separation onset location]
    F --> F5[Cd value for given geometry]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Wind-Tunnel Dynamic Similarity Workflow

This block diagram illustrates the engineering decision tree used to match Reynolds numbers between a prototype vehicle and its scale model in a wind tunnel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Prototype Vehicle Specification] --> P2[Identify Lp and Vp]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Rep using VL by nu]

    M1[Choose Geometric Scale Ratio n] --> M2[Compute model length Lm equals Lp by n]
    M2 --> M3{Choose test fluid}

    M3 -->|Same air| M4[nu model equals nu proto]
    M3 -->|Different gas| M5[Use nu of test gas]

    M4 --> M6[Apply Re match Vm Lm by nu m equals Vp Lp by nu p]
    M5 --> M6

    M6 --> M7[Solve for Vm required]
    M7 --> M8{Vm within tunnel capability}

    M8 -->|Yes| M9[Conduct test at Vm]
    M8 -->|No| M10[Reduce scale ratio OR pressurize tunnel OR use alternative fluid]

    M9 --> M11[Validate Cd and Cl match]
    M10 --> M3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reynolds Number Effect on Drag Coefficient

This block diagram summarizes how the drag coefficient of a streamlined automotive body varies qualitatively with Reynolds number.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[Re less than 1e4] --> S1[High Cd due to viscous dominance]
    R2[Re between 1e4 and 1e5] --> S2[Cd decreasing rapidly with Re]
    R3[Re between 1e5 and 1e6] --> S3[Drag crisis region boundary layer transitions]
    R4[Re between 1e6 and 1e7] --> S4[Cd plateau typical vehicle regime]
    R5[Re greater than 1e7] --> S5[Cd approximately constant Reynolds number independence]

    S4 --> V1[Modern sedan at highway speed]
    S5 --> V2[Race car at top speed]
    S3 --> V1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Diagram Note:** Because Reynolds number governs a continuum of physical flow phenomena, a functional block architecture is more rigorous than any single schematic. The diagrams above should be used alongside the formulas in Section 2 for full conceptual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Define Reynolds number. Explain its physical significance in vehicle aerodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Reynolds number $Re$ is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}
$$

where $\rho$ is fluid density, $V$ is free-stream velocity, $L$ is characteristic length, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity. **(1 mark)**

**Physical Significance:** It predicts the flow regime—laminar, transitional, or turbulent—and quantifies the relative dominance of inertial over viscous effects. In vehicle aerodynamics, $Re$ governs boundary-layer behavior, separation onset, drag coefficient, and is the key similarity parameter for wind-tunnel testing of scaled vehicle models. Real road vehicles operate at $Re \approx 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$, firmly in the turbulent regime. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Distinguish between laminar and turbulent flow on the basis of Reynolds number.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reynolds number | $Re < Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ | $Re > Re_{crit}$ |
| Velocity profile | Smooth, parabolic, ordered | Chaotic, fluctuating, mixing |
| Fluid motion | Layers slide parallel | Eddies and vortices present |
| Energy dissipation | Low | High |
| Vehicle relevance | Not encountered on real cars | Dominant regime for all road vehicles |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for relevance)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part B carries 14 marks and typically contains two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Attempt any ONE full question from the choice provided.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for Reynolds number starting from the ratio of inertial to viscous forces. A car of streamwise length $L = 4 \, \text{m}$ moves at $V = 144 \, \text{km/h}$. The air properties are $\rho = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$ and $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$. Compute the Reynolds number. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the force expressions:**

Inertial force: $F_i \sim \rho V^{2} L^{2}$ **(1 mark)**

Viscous force: $F_v \sim \mu V L$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Form the ratio and simplify:**

$$
Re = \frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Convert given data to SI units:**

$$
V = \frac{144}{3.6} = 40 \text{ m/s}, \quad L = 4 \text{ m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute:**

$$
Re = \frac{1.2 \times 40 \times 4}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{192}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
Re = 1.067 \times 10^{7} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{7}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{Re \approx 1.07 \times 10^{7}}$ — indicating fully turbulent flow.

---

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain how Reynolds number governs the boundary-layer behavior over a vehicle body. Discuss the concept of **Reynolds number independence** as it applies to road-vehicle drag. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part 1 — Boundary-Layer Behavior:** When air flows over a vehicle, a thin boundary layer forms adjacent to the body surface. At the front of the vehicle (stagnation region), the boundary layer is initially laminar due to favorable pressure gradient. As the flow progresses downstream, depending on the local Reynolds number and surface conditions, it undergoes transition to turbulent state at $Re_{x,crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ (based on distance $x$ from leading edge). The turbulent boundary layer is more energetic, resists separation longer, and reattaches more readily than a laminar layer. **(2 marks)**

**Part 2 — Flow Regimes Sketch Description:**

- **Low Re** ($< 5 \times 10^{5}$): Thin laminar layer, early separation, large wake, high pressure drag.
- **Transitional Re** ($\sim 10^{6}$): Mixed layer, separation location shifts aft, drag decreases.
- **High Re** ($> 10^{6}$): Fully turbulent layer, late separation, smaller wake, lower drag.

**(2 marks)**

**Part 3 — Reynolds Number Independence:** For bluff-body automotive shapes operating above $Re \approx 10^{6}$, the drag coefficient $C_D$ becomes essentially **independent of further increases in Re**. This is called the **Reynolds number independence regime**. It is the most important reason why sub-scale wind-tunnel tests of cars yield valid drag data—provided $Re$ is high enough to be in this plateau. **(2 marks)**

**Conclusion:** For typical road cars at highway speeds, $Re \gg 10^{6}$ and $C_D$ is approximately constant. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** A 1:4 scale model of a sedan is tested in a wind tunnel. The prototype length is $L_p = 4.8 \, \text{m}$ and prototype cruise speed is $V_p = 120 \, \text{km/h}$. If the test is conducted in air at the same conditions as the prototype ($\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$), determine the required wind-tunnel velocity for dynamic similarity. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert prototype velocity to SI:**

$$
V_p = \frac{120}{3.6} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Identify scale ratio and compute model length:**

Scale ratio = $1:4 \Rightarrow L_m = L_p / 4 = 4.8 / 4 = 1.2 \, \text{m}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Write dynamic similarity condition:**

$$
\frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p}
$$

Since $\nu_m = \nu_p$ (same fluid):

$$
V_m L_m = V_p L_p
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_m$:**

$$
V_m = \frac{V_p L_p}{L_m} = \frac{33.33 \times 4.8}{1.2}
$$

$$
V_m = \frac{160}{1.2} = 133.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Convert to km/h (optional presentation):**

$$
V_m = 133.33 \times 3.6 \approx 480 \text{ km/h}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{V_m = 133.33 \text{ m/s} \approx 480 \text{ km/h}}$

**Examiner's Note:** Such a high velocity is unrealistic in conventional atmospheric tunnels, which is why full-scale or pressurized tunnels are used for production-vehicle validation.

---

**(b)** Explain the principle of **dynamic similarity** in aerodynamic testing. Discuss why matching Reynolds number alone is not always sufficient for full-scale vehicle testing and what additional considerations arise. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part 1 — Dynamic Similarity Principle:** Two flows are dynamically similar when all dimensionless governing parameters (Re, Froude number, Mach number, etc.) are equal between model and prototype. For vehicle aerodynamics, the Reynolds number is the dominant similarity parameter because inertia and viscosity are the primary competing forces. When $Re_m = Re_p$, the velocity and pressure field patterns around the model are geometrically similar to those around the prototype. **(2 marks)**

**Part 2 — Why Re Alone Is Insufficient:** Real vehicle flows involve additional physical effects:

- **Compressibility:** At high speeds (above ~0.3 Mach), air density variations matter, requiring **Mach number matching** as well. This is critical for race cars approaching 100 m/s. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Ground Boundary Layer:** Real vehicles operate near the ground, where the moving road surface and stationary air interact through a complex boundary layer. Wind-tunnel testing must use a **moving ground belt** or **boundary-layer suction** to simulate this effect. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Reynolds Number Independence Plateau:** Below $Re \approx 10^{6}$, $C_D$ varies steeply with Re, so testing a small model at low Re gives unreliable results. This is why **boundary-layer tripping** is used. **(1 mark)**
- **Blockage Effects:** In a confined tunnel, the model displaces air and artificially accelerates the flow. Blockage should be kept below ~5% of tunnel cross-section. **(1 mark)**

**Part 3 — Conclusion:** Hence, a complete aerodynamic test program demands Re matching, ground simulation, blockage correction, and sometimes Mach matching. **(0 marks — implied)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s. Always remember $V(\text{m/s}) = V(\text{km/h}) / 3.6$. This single error loses 1 mark per problem.
> 2. **Confusing Characteristic Length:** Students often write $L$ = vehicle height or width without justification. For external vehicle flow, **streamwise length** is the standard choice unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Missing the "Dimensionless" Check:** The Reynolds number has **no units**. If your answer carries units, you have made an error.
> 4. **Forgetting Dynamic Similarity Condition:** In scaling problems, students compute $V_m$ without writing $Re_m = Re_p$ first. Examiners award marks for stating the principle, not just plugging numbers.
> 5. **Ignoring Kinematic vs Dynamic Viscosity:** When a problem gives $\nu$, use $Re = VL/\nu$. When it gives $\mu$, use $Re = \rho VL/\mu$. Mixing them up gives wrong numerical answers.
> 6. **Skipping the Boundary-Layer Discussion:** A purely numerical answer without conceptual explanation (flow regime, transition, Re-independence) will not earn full marks in Part B sub-questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — REYNOLDS NUMBER**

- **Definition:** $Re$ is the ratio of inertial to viscous forces in a fluid flow. It is dimensionless. **(Must memorize)**
- **Two equivalent forms:** $Re = \rho V L / \mu = V L / \nu$. **(Both forms required)**
- **Characteristic length $L$ for vehicles:** Streamwise length (front to rear bumper). **(Frequently tested)**
- **Critical Reynolds number for transition:** $\approx 5 \times 10^{5}$ on a smooth flat plate; lower on real vehicles due to roughness. **(Key transition point)**
- **Road-vehicle regime:** $Re \approx 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$ — fully turbulent. **(Always quote)**
- **Dynamic similarity:** $Re_m = Re_p$ is mandatory for valid wind-tunnel model tests. **(Central concept)**
- **Scaling law (same fluid):** $V_m / V_p = L_p / L_m$. **(Quick formula for exams)**
- **Reynolds Number Independence:** Above $Re \approx 10^{6}$, $C_D$ is approximately constant — explains why sub-scale tests work. **(Conceptual gold)**
- **Dimensionless answer check:** If your final $Re$ has any unit, you have made an error.
- **Standard air values:** $\rho = 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$, $\nu = 1.48 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. **(Default if not given)**
- **Velocity unit conversion:** $1 \, \text{km/h} = 1/3.6 \, \text{m/s}$. **(Step 1 of every numerical)**
- **Practical implication:** Streamlining beats surface smoothness for drag reduction because vehicles are inertia-dominated, not viscosity-dominated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mach Number
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mach Number

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics** and compressible fluid flow, the **Mach Number** (denoted as $M$) is formally defined as the **dimensionless ratio of the local flow velocity of a fluid to the local speed of sound in that same medium**:

$$M = \frac{V}{a}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Local flow velocity (in m/s)
- $a$ = Local speed of sound in the fluid medium (in m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Mach Number is the *governing* similarity parameter in high-speed compressible flow. Two geometrically identical bodies moving at the same Mach Number (in the same gas) will exhibit **aerodynamically similar** flow fields, regardless of their absolute physical size or absolute speed.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Boat and the Wave"

Imagine you are rowing a small boat on the surface of a calm pond. The waves your boat generates travel outward at a certain speed (analogous to the speed of sound, $a$).

- **Slow rowing (Subsonic, $M < 1$):** The boat moves slower than the waves it creates. The waves race ahead of the boat and dissipate upstream. Information about the boat's presence reaches everywhere.
- **Racing speed (Sonic, $M = 1$):** The boat moves exactly at the wave speed. Waves pile up at the bow — analogous to a **normal shock wave**.
- **High-speed powerboat (Supersonic, $M > 1$):** The boat outruns its own waves. The waves can only spread out behind and to the sides in a V-shaped pattern — the famous **Mach Cone**.

In aerodynamics, the "boat" is the vehicle (or the airflow), and the "waves" are the **pressure disturbances** propagating at the speed of sound.

### 1.3 The Speed of Sound — Foundation Parameter

The Mach number cannot exist without knowing the speed of sound $a$. For a perfect gas undergoing an **isentropic (adiabatic + reversible) process**, Newton's derivation yields:

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = Ratio of specific heats ($C_p / C_v$) — for air, **$\gamma = 1.4$**
- $R$ = Specific gas constant — for air, **$R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$**
- $T$ = Absolute static temperature of the gas (in Kelvin, K)

> [!NOTE]
> At standard sea-level conditions ($T = 288.15 \text{ K}$, i.e., $15^\circ\text{C}$), the speed of sound in air is **$a \approx 340.29 \text{ m/s}$** (≈ **1225 km/h**).

### 1.4 Mach Number Regimes — The Five Flow Regimes

The behavior of the airflow around a vehicle changes *dramatically* depending on the Mach number. KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to classify the regime of a given flow.

| Regime | Mach Number Range | Physical Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Incompressible / Low-Subsonic** | $M < 0.3$ | Density variation $< 5\%$; treated as incompressible. **Valid regime for most road vehicles.** |
| **Subsonic** | $0.3 \le M < 0.8$ | Compressibility becomes noticeable; local supersonic pockets may form. |
| **Transonic** | $0.8 \le M \le 1.2$ | Both subsonic and supersonic regions coexist; **shock waves** form. |
| **Supersonic** | $1.2 < M < 5.0$ | Entire flow field is supersonic; oblique shocks and expansion fans dominate. |
| **Hypersonic** | $M \ge 5.0$ | Shock layer is very thin; real-gas effects (dissociation, ionization) appear. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Is Mach Number the Most Important Parameter?

The Mach number represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **elastic (compressibility) forces** in the flow. When $M$ is small, the fluid has enough time to "communicate" pressure disturbances upstream, so it behaves as if it were incompressible. As $M \to 1$, this communication becomes impossible across certain regions, and **discontinuities (shock waves)** must form to satisfy conservation laws.

### 2.2 Critical Mach Number vs. Freestream Mach Number

This is a **frequently tested distinction** in KTU board exams:

- **Freestream Mach Number ($M_\infty$):** The Mach number of the undisturbed flow far away from the body.
- **Critical Mach Number ($M_{cr}$):** The *freestream* Mach number at which the **local Mach number first reaches 1.0** somewhere on the body surface (typically at the point of maximum thickness).

For example, on an airfoil, $M_{cr} < M_\infty$ because flow accelerates over the upper surface. Local velocity $V_{local} > V_\infty$, so local Mach number $M_{local} > M_\infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Relevance:** For road vehicles (cars, trucks, buses), the freestream Mach number is typically $M_\infty \approx 0.05$ to $0.30$ (at 60 km/h to 360 km/h). Even at high racing speeds, **true compressibility effects are negligible**. However, **local Mach numbers** in narrow gaps (radiator inlets, wheel wells, wing-tip vortices) can spike, causing micro-shocks in extreme cases.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = V / a$ | Definition of Mach number | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound (Newton-Laplace eqn.) | m/s; $T$ in K |
| 3 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T_S}$ | $T_S$ = stagnation (total) temperature | Relates static & stagnation |
| 4 | $M_{cr} < M_\infty$ | Critical < Freestream Mach | Always true on curved surfaces |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$ | Stagnation temperature ratio | Isentropic flow |
| 6 | $\dfrac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Stagnation pressure ratio | Isentropic flow |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ | Stagnation density ratio | Isentropic flow |
| 8 | $\mu = M_{local} / M_\infty$ | Velocity coefficient ratio | Used in Prandtl-Glauert |
| 9 | $C_{p,compressible} = \dfrac{C_{p,incompressible}}{\sqrt{\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert}}$ | **Prandtl-Glauert Rule** | Subsonic compressibility correction |
| 10 | $\sin \mu = 1/M$ | **Mach Angle** (supersonic) | $\mu$ is the Mach angle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Prandtl-Glauert Singularity:** The denominator becomes **zero** at $M_\infty = 1$. The formula breaks down at transonic flow. Do not use it for $M > 0.7$ or for $M = 1$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Designing supersonic jets, rockets, and re-entry vehicles requires accurate $M$ estimation to place shock-free inlets and assess thermal loading.
- **High-Speed Rail & Hyperloop:** Even though they operate in "low-subsonic" regimes, designers use $M$ to estimate drag divergence and tunnel pressure waves (the *piston effect* in tunnels).
- **Road Vehicle Aerodynamics:** Although $M$ itself is small, the **Prandtl-Glauert correction** is conceptually introduced so students understand that at high enough $M_\infty$ the *form* of the pressure distribution would change — justifying why modern cars are tested in wind tunnels with proper pressure transducers rather than relying solely on incompressible theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Speed of Sound ($a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$)

This is a **standard KTU 14-mark derivation question**. Below is the complete, exhaustive derivation suitable for full marks.

**Starting Point:** A small pressure disturbance (sound wave) propagates as a weak compression wave. We model the wavefront as a thin control volume moving with the wave at speed $a$.

**Step 1 — Control Volume Setup:**
Consider a sound wave traveling through a stationary fluid at speed $a$. Transform to a reference frame moving with the wave. In this frame, the fluid far upstream approaches the wave at speed $a$ and leaves downstream at speed $a + da$.

**Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity):**
Mass flux into the wave = Mass flux out of the wave.

$$\rho \, a = (\rho + d\rho)(a + da)$$

Expanding the right-hand side (neglecting the second-order term $d\rho \cdot da$):

$$\rho \, a = \rho \, a + \rho \, da + a \, d\rho$$

Canceling $\rho a$ from both sides:

$$0 = \rho \, da + a \, d\rho$$

Dividing throughout by $\rho \, a$:

$$\frac{da}{a} = -\frac{d\rho}{\rho} \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Momentum:**
The net force on the control volume equals the rate of change of momentum flux.

$$P - (P + dP) = \rho \, a \left[(a + da) - a\right]$$

The left side is the net pressure force; the right side is the momentum flux change.

$$-dP = \rho \, a \, da$$

Dividing by $da$:

$$\frac{dP}{da} = -\rho \, a \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

> *Note:* Equation B carries a negative sign because as velocity increases ($+da$), pressure must decrease ($-dP$) — Bernoulli's principle.

**Step 4 — Combine Equations A and B:**
From Eq. A: $\dfrac{da}{a} = -\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$, so $da = -\dfrac{a}{\rho}d\rho$.

Substituting into Eq. B:

$$\frac{dP}{da} = \frac{dP}{-\frac{a}{\rho}d\rho} = -\frac{\rho}{a}\frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

Equating the two forms of Eq. B:

$$-\rho \, a = -\frac{\rho}{a}\frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

Cancel $-\rho$ from both sides:

$$a = \frac{1}{a}\frac{dP}{d\rho}$$

Multiply both sides by $a$:

$$a^2 = \frac{dP}{d\rho} \quad \text{...(Eq. C)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Isentropic Process Assumption:**
For a sound wave (a weak, rapid disturbance), the process is **reversible and adiabatic** (isentropic). For a perfect gas, the isentropic relation is:

$$\frac{P}{\rho^\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating implicitly with respect to $\rho$:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \gamma \frac{P}{\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law:**
From the ideal gas equation: $P = \rho R T$, so $\dfrac{P}{\rho} = R T$.

**Step 7 — Combine Final Equations:**
Substituting Steps 5 and 6 into Eq. C:

$$a^2 = \gamma \cdot R T$$

Taking the positive square root (speed of sound is positive):

$$\boxed{a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Example for Air at Sea Level:**

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288.15}$$

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 288.15}$$

$$a = \sqrt{115{,}747.5}$$

$$a \approx 340.29 \text{ m/s} \approx 1225 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** This matches the accepted standard value of the speed of sound in dry air at $15^\circ$C. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Prandtl-Glauert Compressibility Correction (Analytical Matrix)

The Prandtl-Glauert rule allows engineers to convert **incompressible** pressure coefficients (which are easy to compute via potential flow) into **compressible** coefficients for subsonic flow.

**Starting Governing Equation (Linearized Compressible Potential Flow):**

$$\left(1 - M_\infty^2\right)\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

where $\phi$ is the velocity potential.

**Step 1 — Apply Affine Transformation:**
Define new stretched coordinates $x^* = x\sqrt{\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert}$ and $y^* = y$, $z^* = z$.

Substituting, the equation becomes Laplace's equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^{*2}} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^{*2}} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^{*2}} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recognize Geometric Equivalence:**
A body of half-thickness $t$ in the stretched coordinates has half-thickness $t / \sqrt{\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert}$ in real coordinates. Thus, the **compressible subsonic flow** around the actual body is mathematically equivalent to the **incompressible flow** around an *affinely stretched* (thicker) body.

**Step 3 — Use the Linearized Pressure Coefficient:**
$$C_p = -\frac{2 u}{V_\infty}$$

where $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$.

**Step 4 — Combine via Affine Stretching:**
Since $\partial/\partial x = \partial/\partial x^* \cdot (1/\sqrt{\vert 1-M_\infty^2 \vert})$, the velocity scales inversely. The ratio of compressible to incompressible $C_p$ is:

$$\frac{C_{p,\text{compressible}}}{C_{p,\text{incompressible}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{C_p = \frac{C_{p,0}}{\sqrt{\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical:** The absolute value bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ must be shown in the answer. The Prandtl-Glauert rule is valid for **subsonic** flow only ($M_\infty < 1$).

---

### 3.3 Mach Angle Derivation (Supersonic Flow)

When flow is supersonic, pressure disturbances cannot propagate upstream. They can only spread laterally, forming a **Mach cone** behind the source.

**Setup:** A point source moves at velocity $V > a$. At time $t = 0$, the source is at point $O$. After time $t$, the source has moved to point $S$, a distance $Vt$ from $O$.

Meanwhile, the pressure disturbance emitted at $t = 0$ has spread as a spherical wave of radius $at$.

**Geometric Relationship:** The disturbance wavefront forms the boundary of a cone whose half-angle is the **Mach angle** $\mu$.

From the geometry of a right triangle:

$$\sin \mu = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{at}{Vt} = \frac{a}{V} = \frac{1}{M}$$

$$\boxed{\sin \mu = \frac{1}{M}}$$

**Verification:** As $M \to 1$, $\sin \mu \to 1$, so $\mu \to 90^\circ$ (no cone — spherical wave). As $M \to \infty$, $\mu \to 0^\circ$ (very narrow cone). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mach Number Classification Engine

The following Mermaid block maps the **decision logic** for classifying any flow scenario based on its Mach number — useful for both conceptual understanding and for solving KTU numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Velocity V and Temperature T] --> B[Compute speed of sound: a = sqrt gamma R T]
    B --> C[Compute Mach number: M = V divided by a]
    C --> D{M less than 0.3}
    D -- YES --> E[Regime: INCOMPRESSIBLE]
    D -- NO --> F{M less than 0.8}
    F -- YES --> G[Regime: SUBSONIC]
    F -- NO --> H{M between 0.8 and 1.2}
    H -- YES --> I[Regime: TRANSONIC with Shock Waves]
    H -- NO --> J{M between 1.2 and 5.0}
    J -- YES --> K[Regime: SUPERSONIC Mach Cone forms]
    J -- NO --> L[Regime: HYPERSONIC Real gas effects]
    E --> M[Output: Use Incompressible Bernoulli]
    G --> N[Output: Use Prandtl-Glauert Correction]
    I --> O[Output: Use Transonic Small Disturbance Theory]
    K --> P[Output: Use Oblique Shock Relations]
    L --> Q[Output: Use Hypersonic Correlation Methods]
```

### 4.2 Mapped Relationships Between Key Entities

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[Freestream Velocity V] -->|divided by| M[Mach Number M]
    T[Static Temperature T] -->|used in| A[Speed of Sound a]
    R[Gas Constant R] -->|used in| A
    GAMMA[Specific Heat Ratio gamma] -->|used in| A
    A -->|divided into| M
    M -->|governs| REG[Flow Regime Selection]
    M -->|input to| PG[Prandtl-Glauert Correction]
    M -->|defines| MA[Mach Angle mu]
    REG --> AA[Aerodynamic Method Selection]
    PG --> CP[Pressure Coefficient Cp]
    MA --> SHOCK[Mach Cone Geometry]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Mach Cone Formation

```
                       Supersonic Vehicle Path (V > a)
                                  ↓
                                  ↓
                                  ↓
                  . . . . . . . . . . . . .
              . '                       ' .
            . '                           ' .
          . '    ← DISTURBANCE WAVEFRONT    ' .
        . '     ← (spreading at speed a)      ' .
      . '                                       ' .
    . '                                           ' .
   <————————— SPREADING SPHERICAL WAVE ————————————>
   
   |←——— V·t ———→|  (vehicle traveled distance V·t in time t)
   |←—— a·t ——→|   (wave radius in same time t)
   
   Half-angle of the cone: μ = arcsin(1/M)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The vehicle travels from top-left to bottom-right at supersonic speed $V$. The leading spherical wavefront (the oldest pressure signal) is far ahead and behind. All subsequent wavefronts are nested inside it, forming the visible **Mach cone** with half-angle $\mu$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define Mach Number. List the five flow regimes based on Mach number with their respective ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

The Mach Number is defined as the ratio of the local flow velocity to the local speed of sound in the fluid:

$$M = \frac{V}{a}$$

The five flow regimes are:

1. **Subsonic ($M < 0.8$):** Flow everywhere below sonic; compressibility begins to matter.
2. **Transonic ($0.8 \le M \le 1.2$):** Mixed subsonic and supersonic regions; shock waves form.
3. **Supersonic ($1.2 < M < 5.0$):** Entire flow field is supersonic; shocks and expansion fans dominate.
4. **Hypersonic ($M \ge 5.0$):** Very thin shock layer; real-gas effects appear.
5. **Incompressible ($M < 0.3$):** Density variations $< 5\%$; treated as incompressible (special case of subsonic).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing regimes with correct ranges = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Freestream Mach Number ($M_\infty$) and Critical Mach Number ($M_{cr}$). Why is $M_{cr} < M_\infty$ for a curved body?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Freestream Mach Number ($M_\infty$):** The Mach number of the undisturbed flow far upstream of the body.
- **Critical Mach Number ($M_{cr}$):** The value of $M_\infty$ at which the **local** Mach number first reaches unity ($M_{local} = 1$) somewhere on the body surface.

For a curved (e.g., airfoil-shaped or bluff) body, the flow **accelerates** over the curved surface. By Bernoulli's principle, as velocity increases, pressure decreases, and the local Mach number exceeds $M_\infty$. Therefore, the surface point reaches $M = 1$ *before* the freestream does, giving $M_{cr} < M_\infty$.

*(2 marks for definitions + 1 mark for physical reasoning = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Module-level internal choice. Either Q(A) or Q(B) appears.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **speed of sound** in a perfect gas, starting from the principles of conservation of mass and momentum. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an aircraft flying at $V = 850$ km/h at an altitude where the ambient temperature is $-50^\circ$C and $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K):
  - (i) Calculate the freestream Mach number. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) An airfoil has an incompressible pressure coefficient $C_{p,0} = -1.2$ at its suction peak. Using Prandtl-Glauert, calculate the compressible $C_p$. **(4 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key checkpoints the examiner awards marks for:

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Statement of isentropic process assumption | 1 |
| 2 | Continuity equation derivation (Eq. A) | 1.5 |
| 3 | Momentum equation derivation (Eq. B) | 1.5 |
| 4 | Combination yielding $a^2 = dP/d\rho$ | 1 |
| 5 | Isentropic relation $dP/d\rho = \gamma P/\rho$ | 1 |
| 6 | Final substitution yielding $a = \sqrt{\gamma RT}$ | 1 |

**(b) (i) Mach Number Calculation (3 marks):**

Convert temperature to Kelvin:
$$T = -50 + 273.15 = 223.15 \text{ K}$$

Speed of sound:
$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 223.15}$$

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 223.15} = \sqrt{89{,}659.6} \approx 299.43 \text{ m/s}$$

Convert velocity:
$$V = 850 \text{ km/h} = \frac{850}{3.6} = 236.11 \text{ m/s}$$

Mach number:
$$M_\infty = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{236.11}{299.43} \approx 0.789$$

$$M_\infty \approx 0.789 \quad \text{(Subsonic regime)}$$

**(b) (ii) Prandtl-Glauert Correction (4 marks):**

$$\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert = \vert 1 - (0.789)^2 \vert = \vert 1 - 0.623 \vert = 0.377$$

$$C_p = \frac{C_{p,0}}{\sqrt{\vert 1 - M_\infty^2 \vert}} = \frac{-1.2}{\sqrt{0.377}} = \frac{-1.2}{0.614} \approx -1.954$$

$$\boxed{C_p \approx -1.954}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The negative pressure peak becomes *more negative* due to compressibility — suction intensifies by ~63% compared to incompressible prediction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the concept of the **Mach Cone**. Derive the relation $\sin\mu = 1/M$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supersonic cruise missile travels at $M = 2.5$ at an altitude where $T = -20^\circ$C.
  - (i) Find the actual speed of the missile in m/s. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) Calculate the Mach angle $\mu$. **(4 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Mach Cone Derivation (7 marks):**

See **Section 3.3** for the full derivation. Award breakdown:

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Schematic of Mach cone (see Section 4.3) | 2 |
| 2 | Definition of Mach angle $\mu$ | 1 |
| 3 | Geometric setup with distance $Vt$ and $at$ | 2 |
| 4 | Final relation $\sin\mu = 1/M$ | 2 |

**(b) (i) Missile Speed (3 marks):**

$$T = -20 + 273.15 = 253.15 \text{ K}$$
