## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Boundary-Layer Momentum Integral Equation (Von Kármán)

The **Von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation** is the cornerstone analytical tool for predicting boundary-layer behavior under non-zero pressure gradients. It relates the wall shear stress to the external pressure gradient and is essential for analyzing separation.

**Starting Point — Conservation of Momentum in a Control Volume**

Consider a control volume bounded by the body surface (from $x=0$ to $x$), the outer edge of the boundary layer, a vertical inflow plane at $x$, and a vertical outflow plane at $x+dx$. Apply the integral momentum equation in the $x$-direction.

The momentum flux entering the control volume at $x$ is:

$$\dot{M}_{\text{in}} = \rho \int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy$$

The momentum flux leaving at $x + dx$ is:

$$\dot{M}_{\text{out}} = \rho \int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy + \frac{d}{dx}\left[\rho \int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy\right] dx$$

The pressure forces on the vertical faces cancel because the static pressure at the boundary-layer edge equals the local external pressure $p(x)$. The net pressure force on the body surface (acting in the $+x$ direction) is:

$$F_{p,\text{wall}} = -p(x) \, \delta$$

The shear force on the wall (acting in the $-x$ direction) is:

$$F_{\tau} = -\tau_w \, dx$$

The pressure force on the top of the control volume at $x + dx$ (acting in $+x$) is:

$$F_{p,\text{top}} = p(x+dx) \, \delta$$

**Sum of Forces in the x-direction:**

$$\sum F_x = -\tau_w \, dx + \left[p(x+dx) - p(x)\right]\delta$$

**Rate of Change of Momentum:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\rho \int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy\right] dx$$

Setting Force = Rate of Change of Momentum:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\rho \int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy\right] dx = -\tau_w \, dx + \frac{dp}{dx}\delta \, dx$$

Divide through by $dx$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\rho \int_0^{\delta} u^2 \, dy\right] = -\tau_w + \frac{dp}{dx}\delta$$

**Introduce Momentum Thickness** $\theta = \int_0^\delta \dfrac{u}{U}\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U}\right) dy$:

After substituting $u/U$ ratios and simplifying, we obtain the classical **Von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}}$$

where $H = \delta^*/\theta$ is the shape factor.

**Why This Equation Matters for Separation:**

If the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ is driven to zero (the separation condition), the equation becomes:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -(2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$$

For separation to occur, the right-hand side must be positive (since $\theta$ is always increasing), which requires $\frac{dU}{dx} < 0$. Through Bernoulli's principle, a deceleration of the free stream ($\frac{dU}{dx} < 0$) corresponds to an **adverse pressure gradient** ($\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$). This rigorously confirms that **APG is the necessary condition for separation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Local Skin Friction on a Flat Plate (Blasius Solution)

For the special case of a **zero pressure gradient** flat plate ($\frac{dp}{dx} = 0$, so $U = \text{const}$), the Von Kármán equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

For the Blasius laminar solution, the momentum thickness is:

$$\theta = \frac{0.664 \, x}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = 0.664 \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}} - \frac{x}{2\text{Re}_x^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{U}{\nu} \right]$$

Using $\text{Re}_x = Ux/\nu$, simplify:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{0.332}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}$$

Therefore, the **local skin friction coefficient** is:

$$\boxed{c_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho U^2} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Condition for Incipient Separation

We seek the condition under which $\tau_w = 0$. Starting from the boundary-layer momentum equation (the differential form):

$$\rho u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \rho v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

Apply the no-slip condition $u = v = 0$ at the wall ($y = 0$):

$$\mu \left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}\right)_{y=0} = \frac{dp}{dx}$$

At separation, $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$, which means the velocity profile has an **inflection point at the wall**:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}\right)_{y=0} = 0$$

Combining the two results, the **critical adverse pressure gradient** for incipient separation is:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{dp}{dx}\right)_{\text{crit}} = 0}$$

This counter-intuitive result reveals that any non-zero adverse pressure gradient will eventually drive the wall shear to zero and cause separation. In practice, of course, a finite APG is required to overcome the stabilizing effect of the turbulent mixing in a real turbulent boundary layer.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Estimating Drag from Separated Base Pressure

For a bluff body (e.g., a flat-faced truck trailer), the wake base pressure coefficient is empirically found to be approximately constant: $C_{p,b} \approx -0.4$.

The pressure drag on the rear face of area $A_b$ is:

$$F_D = (p_\infty - p_b) A_b = \left[ \frac{1}{2}\rho U^2 (1 - C_{p,b}) \right] A_b$$

The corresponding drag coefficient is:

$$\boxed{C_D = \left(1 - C_{p,b}\right) \frac{A_b}{A_{\text{ref}}}}$$

For $C_{p,b} = -0.4$ and $A_b = A_{\text{ref}}$ (a square-back truck), $C_D \approx 1.4 \times 1.0 = $ **a huge drag coefficient** — confirming why trucks and bluff vehicles are so aerodynamically inefficient without boat-tails.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Style Problem:** A passenger car travels at $V = 30 \; \text{m/s}$ through air at standard sea-level conditions. The rear window has length $L = 1.2 \; \text{m}$. Estimate the local Reynolds number at the trailing edge of the rear window and determine whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent. Compute the local skin friction coefficient.

**Step 1 — Compute the local Reynolds number:**

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{V L}{\nu} = \frac{30 \times 1.2}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\text{Re}_L = \frac{36}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.466 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with critical Reynolds number:**

Since $\text{Re}_L = 2.466 \times 10^{6} \gg \text{Re}_{cr} = 5 \times 10^{5}$, the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** at the trailing edge.

**Step 3 — Compute turbulent local skin friction coefficient:**

$$c_f = \frac{0.0592}{\text{Re}_L^{1/5}} = \frac{0.0592}{(2.466 \times 10^{6})^{0.2}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$(2.466 \times 10^{6})^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(2.466 \times 10^6)} = e^{0.2 \times 14.717} = e^{2.943} \approx 18.97$$

Therefore:

$$c_f = \frac{0.0592}{18.97} \approx 3.12 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:** Despite the high vehicle speed, the local skin friction coefficient at the rear window is only about $0.3\%$ — meaning surface friction contributes very little to total drag. Most of the drag comes from pressure drag caused by the **separated wake**, reinforcing the importance of flow-separation control in vehicle aerodynamics.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`**
**Define the phenomenon of boundary-layer flow separation. State the mathematical condition that identifies the separation point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Boundary-layer flow separation is the phenomenon in which the fluid streamlines detach from a solid surface because the kinetic energy of the boundary layer is insufficient to overcome an adverse pressure gradient. Beyond the separation point, the flow reverses near the wall and forms a turbulent, low-pressure wake region.

The mathematical condition at the separation point is that the wall shear stress vanishes and its sign reverses immediately downstream:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} < 0 \text{ downstream}$$

This identifies the separation point as the location where the wall-normal velocity gradient at the wall is zero, beyond which it becomes negative (flow reversal).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`**
**Distinguish between a favorable pressure gradient and an adverse pressure gradient. How does each affect boundary-layer separation on a road vehicle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Favorable Pressure Gradient | Adverse Pressure Gradient |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of $dp/dx$ | Negative ($\frac{dp}{dx} < 0$) | Positive ($\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$) |
| Free-stream behavior | Accelerates ($dU/dx > 0$) | Decelerates ($dU/dx < 0$) |
| Effect on boundary layer | Adds momentum; suppresses separation | Removes momentum; promotes separation |
| Typical vehicle location | Front nose, hood, windshield | Rear roof, backlight, underbody |
| Velocity profile shape | Fuller, $H$ decreasing | Skewed, $H$ increasing |

On a passenger car, FPG regions (front) maintain attached flow, while APG regions (rear) are the primary locations where separation occurs and the dominant pressure drag is generated. Vehicle designers therefore focus on shaping the rear end to either eliminate the APG (fastback) or delay separation as far aft as possible (boat-tail).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts: Part (a) for 7 marks and Part (b) for 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question A]**

**(a)** Derive the Von Kármán momentum integral equation for a two-dimensional boundary layer along a flat surface. Starting from the integral momentum equation for a control volume, clearly define the displacement thickness $\delta^*$ and the momentum thickness $\theta$, and show how the equation reduces to the form:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$$

Use this result to explain mathematically why an adverse pressure gradient is a necessary condition for boundary-layer separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sedan of frontal area $A = 2.2 \; \text{m}^2$ travels at $V = 90 \; \text{km/h}$ through standard air ($\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$). The rear base pressure coefficient is $C_{p,b} = -0.38$. The rear face area is $A_b = 1.4 \; \text{m}^2$. Compute:
- (i) The drag force attributable solely to the rear base pressure.
- (ii) The percentage reduction in drag if a boat-tail device raises the base pressure coefficient to $C_{p,b} = -0.20$.
- (iii) Comment qualitatively on where else on the vehicle separation occurs and how it could be controlled. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation and Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Control Volume Definition:** Consider a 2-D control volume of unit depth, bounded below by the wall (from $x = 0$ to $x$), above by the boundary-layer edge $y = \delta$, and laterally by vertical lines at $x$ and $x + dx$. Apply the integral momentum equation in the $x$-direction:

$$\sum F_x = \frac{d}{dx} \int_0^\delta \rho u^2 \, dy \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Force Inventory:**
- Pressure force on the wall: $-p(x)\,\delta$ (acting in $+x$ direction, since pressure pushes the wall in $+x$)
- Pressure force on the top of the CV at $x+dx$: $+p(x+dx)\,\delta$
- Shear force on the wall: $-\tau_w \, dx$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\rho \int_0^\delta u^2 \, dy\right] = -\tau_w + \frac{dp}{dx}\delta$$

**Step 4 — Express Momentum Flux Using $\theta$ and $H$:**

By substituting $u/U$ non-dimensional profiles and applying the definitions of $\delta^*$ and $\theta$, the equation becomes:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$$

**Step 5 — Separation Condition:** At separation, $\tau_w = 0$, so:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -(2 + H)\frac{\theta}{U}\frac{dU}{dx}$$

Since $\theta > 0$ and $(2 + H) > 0$, the right-hand side must be positive, requiring $\frac{dU}{dx} < 0$. By Bernoulli, $p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 = \text{const}$ implies $\frac{dp}{dx} = -\rho U \frac{dU}{dx} > 0$. Hence, an **adverse pressure gradient is necessary for separation**. **(7/7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem and Qualitative Discussion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:**
$$V = 90 \; \text{km/h} = 25 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute dynamic pressure:**
$$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 25^2 = 382.81 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure difference between base and freestream:**
$$\Delta p = q_\infty (1 - C_{p,b}) = 382.81 \times (1 - (-0.38)) = 382.81 \times 1.38 = 528.28 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — (i) Drag force from base pressure:**
$$F_D = \Delta p \cdot A_b = 528.28 \times 1.4 = 739.6 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Drag with boat-tail:**
$$\Delta p_{\text{new}} = 382.81 \times (1 - (-0.20)) = 382.81 \times 1.20 = 459.4 \; \text{Pa}$$
$$F_{D,\text{new}} = 459.4 \times 1.4 = 643.2 \; \text{N}$$

Percentage reduction:
$$\%\text{reduction} = \frac{739.6 - 643.2}{739.6} \times 100\% = 13.0\%$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Qualitative comment:** Separation also occurs at the A-pillars, side mirrors, wheel arches, and the underbody. These can be controlled by streamlined pillar cross-sections, aero-mirrors, fender liners, and flat underbody panels with a rear diffuser, respectively. **(7/7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question B]**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labelled diagram, describe the development of a turbulent boundary layer along the upper surface of a passenger car from the windshield header to the rear deck. Identify on the diagram: the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$, the boundary-layer thickness $\delta$, the location of maximum velocity, the separation point, and the wake region. Explain the role of Reynolds number in determining whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent at the rear of the car. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sports car is being designed with two candidate rear-end geometries:
- **Candidate 1:** Notchback with a steeply raked backlight (separation occurs at $x_s = 0.5 \; \text{m}$ from the roof trailing edge).
- **Candidate 2:** Fastback with a smooth taper (separation occurs at $x_s = 1.1 \; \text{m}$ from the roof trailing edge).
For each candidate, compute the local Reynolds number at the separation point given a design speed of $V = 50 \; \text{m/s}$ and $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. State and justify which candidate will have the lower pressure drag, and explain two passive flow-control techniques that the designer could apply to push the separation point further rearward. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Description and Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The student should draw a labelled side-view of a car showing:
1. Free-stream velocity $U_\infty$ approaching horizontally from the left.
2. Boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ growing along the hood, roof, and rear window.
3. Maximum velocity occurring at the roof apex (where the streamlines are most compressed).
4. Separation point marked at the upper edge of the rear window where the streamlines lift off.
5. Recirculating wake drawn as a rotating eddy pattern behind the rear of the car.
6. Pressure gradient arrow showing $dp/dx > 0$ over the rear glass (adverse).

The student should note that for $V \approx 25 \; \text{m/s}$ and a roof length of $L \approx 2 \; \text{m}$, $\text{Re}_L = VL/\nu \approx 3.4 \times 10^6$, which is well above $\text{Re}_{cr} \approx 5 \times 10^5$. Therefore the boundary layer at the rear is **fully turbulent**, providing more resistance to separation than a laminar layer but still separating in strong APG regions. **(7/7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison and Justification (7 marks):**

**Candidate 1:**
$$\text{Re}_{x,1} = \frac{V \cdot x_s}{\nu} = \frac{50 \times 0.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.67 \times 10^6$$

**Candidate 2:**
$$\text{Re}_{x,2} = \frac{V \cdot x_s}{\nu} = \frac{50 \times 1.1}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.67 \times 10^6$$

**Justification:** Candidate 2 (fastback) has the separation point farther downstream, which means:
- The wake is smaller in streamwise extent.
- The base pressure is higher (less negative $C_{p,b}$), so pressure drag is lower.
- The larger $\text{Re}_{x,2}$ also means a slightly thicker but more energetic turbulent boundary layer that resists separation longer.

Therefore, **Candidate 2 (fastback) will have the lower pressure drag**.

**Two passive flow-control techniques to push separation rearward:**

1. **Boat-tail tapering** — A smooth, gradual contraction of the rear body (typically 8–12° included angle) reduces the magnitude of the adverse pressure gradient by smoothly recovering pressure toward the base.
2. **Vortex generators** — Small, angled tabs or fins placed on the roof upstream of the separation point energize the boundary layer by generating streamwise vortices, increasing near-wall momentum and delaying separation by 5–15 % of body length.

**(Other acceptable answers: rear spoiler used as a controlled separation trip, rear diffuser at the underbody, dimpled or ribbed surface textures.)** **(7/7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OEAUT723 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "separation point" with "stagnation point."** The stagnation point is at the nose of the vehicle (front, where $V = 0$). The separation point is at the rear (where the boundary layer lifts off). Examiners specifically deduct marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing the separation criterion as just "$\tau_w = 0$."** The complete condition is $\tau_w = 0$ **AND** the wall-normal gradient reverses sign downstream. A one-line answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units.** Every formula must be accompanied by SI units. A naked "Re = $10^6$" without specifying that it is dimensionless is flagged.
> 4. **Mixing up displacement and momentum thickness in the Shape Factor $H$.** $H = \delta^*/\theta$, never $H = \theta/\delta^*$. This is a 2-mark killer.
> 5. **In derivations, skipping the "no-slip" justification.** Every time the wall boundary condition $u = v = 0$ at $y = 0$ is used, students must explicitly state it.
> 6. **Not labelling Mermaid/sketch diagrams.** A diagram without arrow for free-stream velocity, separation point, and wake region loses up to 3 marks.
> 7. **Stating "APG causes separation" without the qualifier "is necessary for."** Strictly, APG is *necessary but not sufficient* — the magnitude of APG must exceed a critical value.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flow Separation (Module 1)**

- [x] **Definition:** Flow separation occurs when a boundary layer detaches from a solid surface due to an adverse pressure gradient.
- [x] **Separation Criterion:** $\tau_w = 0$ AND $\partial u/\partial y \vert_{y=0} < 0$ downstream.
- [x] **APG is necessary (not sufficient)** for separation; $dp/dx > 0$.
- [x] **Bernoulli link:** $dp/dx > 0 \iff dU/dx < 0 \iff$ boundary-layer deceleration.
- [x] **Von Kármán Equation:** $\dfrac{\tau_w}{\rho U^2} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} + (2+H)\dfrac{\theta}{U}\dfrac{dU}{dx}$.
- [x] **Shape Factor:** $H = \delta^*/\theta \ge 1$, $H \to \infty$ at separation.
- [x] **Blasius laminar:** $c_f = 0.664/\sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$.
- [x] **Turbulent:** $c_f = 0.0592/\text{Re}_x^{1/5}$.
- [x] **Turbulent boundary layers resist separation** better than laminar because of enhanced near-wall momentum.
- [x] **Drag Force:** $F_D = 0.5 \rho U^2 C_D A$.
- [x] **Critical Reynolds number:** $\text{Re}_{cr} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ for natural transition on a flat plate.
- [x] **Vehicle hotspots for separation:** rear roof, backlight, A-pillars, side mirrors, wheel arches, underbody.
- [x] **Flow-control techniques:** fastback profile, boat-tail, vortex generators, rear spoiler, diffuser, fender liners, wheel covers.
- [x] **Empirical base pressure:** $C_{p,b} \approx -0.3$ to $-0.5$ for bluff bodies.
- [x] **Drag reduction magnitude:** Boat-tails cut truck drag by 5–15 %; modern EVs achieve $C_D \approx 0.20$ via rear-end separation control.
- [x] **Memory hook:** **APG → Stall → Wake → Drag**. Memorize this four-step chain.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** Always quote the separation criterion **in full**, never abbreviated.
- [x] **Dimensionless:** Reynolds number, $C_D$, $C_p$, $c_f$, $H$ — all are dimensionless; state explicitly in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Streamlines, Streak Lines and Path Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Streamlines, Streak Lines and Path Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In vehicle aerodynamics and fluid kinematics, the visualization and mathematical description of fluid motion is critical. Since fluids are continuous media composed of countless particles in motion, we use three classical "lines" to describe and visualize their behavior: **Streamlines**, **Streak Lines**, and **Path Lines**.

### 1.1 Streamline (SL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Streamline** is an *imaginary curve* drawn through a flowing fluid such that the **tangent at every point on the curve is aligned with the instantaneous velocity vector** of the fluid particle at that point, at a *fixed instant of time*.

**Key Properties:**
- A streamline represents an **instantaneous snapshot** of the flow field.
- Fluid particles move **along** the streamline (they do not cross it).
- For a **steady flow**, streamlines, path lines, and streak lines are *identical*.
- In **unsteady flow**, they are *fundamentally different* and must be treated separately.

### 1.2 Path Line (Particle Trajectory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Path Line** is the actual *trajectory* traced by a **single fluid particle** as it moves through space over a period of time.

**Key Properties:**
- It tracks **one specific particle** from the past to the present.
- It is the fluid equivalent of a "GPS breadcrumb trail" for a particle.
- It depends on the *history* of the particle's motion.

### 1.3 Streak Line (Filament Line)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Streak Line** is the locus of all fluid particles that have *previously* passed through (or are currently passing through) a **given fixed point in space** (typically a point of dye injection).

**Key Properties:**
- It is a "**photograph of dye injection**" with a long exposure.
- Used in **experimental flow visualization** (e.g., smoke in a wind tunnel).
- In steady flow, streak lines coincide with streamlines and path lines.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Three Perspectives of a River:**
>
> - **Streamline** ≈ A *still photograph* of a flowing river, with arrows showing the direction of water at each visible point at that moment. No individual droplet is tracked.
> - **Path Line** ≈ Tying a small cork to a GPS tracker and letting it float downstream. The line drawn is the cork's actual route.
> - **Streak Line** ≈ Pouring a single drop of green dye into the river at a fixed spot, and then taking a long-exposure photo. The green line you see contains *all* the dye molecules that have ever passed through that spot.

In a **vehicle aerodynamics context**, the airflow over a car body or wing is typically *steady* at low speeds (constant velocity), so engineers often treat streamlines and path lines as equivalent. However, during **transient maneuvers** (overtaking, gust encounters, cornering), the flow becomes unsteady, and the distinction becomes academically and practically important.

![Conceptual Distinction Overview]

| Line Type | Time Frame | Tracks | Visualization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Streamline** | Instantaneous ($t = t_0$) | Direction of *all* particles at one instant | Still photo with arrows |
| **Path Line** | Time interval ($t_0$ to $t_1$) | One specific particle | GPS trail of a cork |
| **Streak Line** | Time interval ($t_0$ to $t_1$) | All particles through fixed point | Long-exposure photo of dye |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Description of Flow

Let the velocity field of a 3-D unsteady flow be given by the vector function:

$$
\mathbf{V}(x,y,z,t) = u(x,y,z,t)\,\hat{i} + v(x,y,z,t)\,\hat{j} + w(x,y,z,t)\,\hat{k}
$$

where:
- $u, v, w$ are the **Cartesian components** of velocity.
- $t$ is the time variable.
- $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are the **unit vectors** along the $x, y, z$ axes respectively.

### 2.2 Streamline Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The streamline at time $t_0$ is defined such that the **velocity vector is tangent to the curve** at every point:

$$
\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w} \quad \text{(at a fixed instant } t = t_0\text{)}
$$

**Why this form?**
- A tangent vector to a curve is proportional to $(dx, dy, dz)$.
- The velocity vector at that point is $(u, v, w)$.
- For them to be parallel, their cross product must vanish, leading to the ratios above.
- This is solved at **constant time** $t_0$.

### 2.3 Path Line Equation

The path line is obtained by integrating the velocity components over time:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = u(x,y,z,t), \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = v(x,y,z,t), \quad \frac{dz}{dt} = w(x,y,z,t)
$$

Integrating from the initial position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ at $t = t_0$:

$$
x = x_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} u\,d\tau, \quad y = y_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} v\,d\tau, \quad z = z_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} w\,d\tau
$$

### 2.4 Streak Line Equation

The streak line is the locus of all particles that passed through a fixed point $(x_p, y_p, z_p)$ at *different* times. If a particle passed through at time $\tau$, its current position is given by the path-line integral:

$$
x_s(x_p,y_p,z_p,t) = x_p + \int_{\tau}^{t} u\,dt', \quad y_s = y_p + \int_{\tau}^{t} v\,dt', \quad z_s = z_p + \int_{\tau}^{t} w\,dt'
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Steady Flow ($\partial/\partial t = 0$):**
> Streamlines $\equiv$ Path Lines $\equiv$ Streak Lines.
> This identity is the **single most-tested fact** in KTU university exams on this topic.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Conditions | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity Field (3-D) | $\mathbf{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | General 3-D flow | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | Streamline Equation | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | Fixed time $t = t_0$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Streamline (2-D) | $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$ | Fixed time $t = t_0$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | Path Line (2-D) | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = dt$ | Variable time $t$ | $\text{s}$ |
| 5 | Path Line Position | $x = x_0 + \int u\,dt$, $y = y_0 + \int v\,dt$ | Integrated over $t$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | Streak Line Locus | $x_s(\tau) = x_p + \int_{\tau}^{t} u\,dt$ | Varying $\tau$ (injection time) | $\text{m}$ |
| 7 | Steady Flow Identity | Streamline $\equiv$ Path Line $\equiv$ Streak Line | $\partial \mathbf{V}/\partial t = 0$ | — |
| 8 | Stream Function (2-D) | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Incompressible, 2-D | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | Tangent Vector | $\mathbf{T} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}}{\vert \mathbf{V} \vert}$ | Unit tangent along streamline | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility in Vehicle Aerodynamics:**
> - **Streamlines** are used in **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** post-processing to visualize flow topology around cars, wings, and diffusers.
> - **Path Lines** are used in **particle-tracking simulations** (e.g., soot from exhaust, water spray from tires, dust around a moving vehicle).
> - **Streak Lines** are used in **wind-tunnel smoke visualization** to qualitatively assess flow separation and wake structures behind vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Streamline Equation in 2-D

**Given:** A 2-D velocity field $\mathbf{V} = u(x,y,t)\,\hat{i} + v(x,y,t)\,\hat{j}$.

**To Prove:** The differential form of the streamline is $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$.

**Proof (Exhaustive):**

**Step 1:** Consider a small element $ds$ along the streamline at point $P(x,y)$ at fixed time $t_0$. The tangent vector to the streamline at $P$ is:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \frac{d\mathbf{r}}{ds} = \frac{dx}{ds}\hat{i} + \frac{dy}{ds}\hat{j}
$$

**Step 2:** The velocity vector at the same point $P$ is:

$$
\mathbf{V} = u(x,y,t_0)\,\hat{i} + v(x,y,t_0)\,\hat{j}
$$

**Step 3:** By definition of a streamline, $\mathbf{T}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ are **collinear (parallel)**. Therefore, their cross product must vanish:

$$
\mathbf{T} \times \mathbf{V} = 0
$$

In 2-D, this is equivalent to the proportional ratios:

$$
\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = ds
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiplying the first two ratios:

$$
u\,dy = v\,dx
$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging to standard textbook form:

$$
u\,dy - v\,dx = 0
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The same logic extends to 3-D using the triple ratio $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$, with the constraint that time is held **constant** while taking the differentials.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence of Streamlines, Path Lines, and Streak Lines in Steady Flow

**Given:** $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$, $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0$, $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Streamline:**
At $t = t_0$, the streamline passes through $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ and satisfies $\dfrac{dx}{u(x_0,y_0,z_0)} = \dfrac{dy}{v(x_0,y_0,z_0)} = \dfrac{dz}{w(x_0,y_0,z_0)}$.

**Step 2 — Path Line:**
Path line passes through $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ at $t = t_0$. Since flow is steady, $u, v, w$ are functions of position *only*:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = u(x), \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = v(y), \quad \frac{dz}{dt} = w(z)
$$

Eliminating $dt$ between equations:

$$
\frac{dx}{u(x)} = \frac{dy}{v(y)} = \frac{dz}{w(z)} = dt
$$

This is the *same differential equation* as the streamline. Hence, **path line $\equiv$ streamline** in steady flow.

**Step 3 — Streak Line:**
For a particle that passed through the fixed point $(x_p, y_p, z_p)$ at time $\tau$, its current position is:

$$
x_s = x_p + \int_{\tau}^{t} u(x_s, t')\,dt'
$$

Since $u$ does not depend on time, the integral path is *time-independent*. The locus traced by varying $\tau$ is again the streamline. Hence, **streak line $\equiv$ streamline** in steady flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concluding Identity:** In a steady flow, the three lines are *mathematically identical* because the velocity field is **time-invariant**, removing the distinction between "current snapshot", "particle history", and "dye trail".

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison & Use Cases

| Property | Streamline | Path Line | Streak Line |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time dependence** | Fixed $t$ (snapshot) | Varies over interval | Varies over interval |
| **Number of particles** | All particles in the field at instant | One specific particle | All particles through fixed point |
| **Governing ODE** | $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$ | $dx/u = dy/v = dt$ | Integral of path line over $\tau$ |
| **Tangent at any point** | $\mathbf{V}(x,y,z,t_0)$ | $d\mathbf{r}/dt$ at that point | Velocity at that instant |
| **Steady flow identity** | $\equiv$ Path $\equiv$ Streak | $\equiv$ Stream $\equiv$ Streak | $\equiv$ Stream $\equiv$ Path |
| **Experimental method** | Dye injection + stroboscope | Hydrogen-bubble tracing | Smoke or dye injection |
| **Computational method** | Plot $\mathbf{V}$ at fixed $t$ | Particle tracking (Lagrangian) | Continuous injection of massless particles |
| **Vehicle aero use** | Wake topology, CFD plots | Soot/dust trajectory | Wind tunnel smoke visualization |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Streamline Through a Velocity Field

**Problem:** For the 2-D velocity field $u = 1 + 2x$, $v = 1 - 2y$ (steady flow), find the streamline passing through the origin $(0, 0)$.

**Solution:**

The streamline equation is:

$$
u\,dy - v\,dx = 0
$$

Substituting the velocity components:

$$
(1 + 2x)\,dy - (1 - 2y)\,dx = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
dy + 2x\,dy - dx + 2y\,dx = 0
$$

Grouping $dx$ and $dy$ terms:

$$
(dy - dx) + 2(x\,dy + y\,dx) = 0
$$

Note that $x\,dy + y\,dx = d(xy)$. So:

$$
(dy - dx) + 2\,d(xy) = 0
$$

Integrating (treating as exact differential):

$$
y - x + 2xy = C
$$

Applying the boundary condition that the streamline passes through $(0, 0)$:

$$
0 - 0 + 2(0)(0) = C \Rightarrow C = 0
$$

**Final Streamline Equation:**

$$
y - x + 2xy = 0
$$

This is a family of curves through the origin representing the streamlines of the given velocity field. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Relationship Between the Three Line Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flow Field V x y z t] --> B{Steady or Unsteady?}
    B -- Steady --> C[Identity Holds]
    B -- Unsteady --> D[Three Distinct Lines]
    C --> E[Streamline = Path Line = Streak Line]
    D --> F1[Streamline: Snapshot at t0]
    D --> F2[Path Line: Particle History]
    D --> F3[Streak Line: Dye Trail through fixed point]
    F1 --> G1[Use: CFD post-processing, wake topology]
    F2 --> G2[Use: Particle tracking, Lagrangian methods]
    F3 --> G3[Use: Wind tunnel visualization, smoke tests]
    E --> H[Vehicle Aero: Constant cruise, no gusts]
    D --> I[Vehicle Aero: Transient maneuvers, gusts, overtaking]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above maps the **decision logic** an engineer follows when selecting the appropriate flow visualization technique based on whether the flow is steady or unsteady — a key skill for vehicle aerodynamicists dealing with both constant-speed highway cruising (steady) and cornering/gust encounters (unsteady).

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Visualization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Define Velocity Field] --> P2[Choose Line Type]
    P2 --> P3[Streamline Mode]
    P2 --> P4[Path Line Mode]
    P2 --> P5[Streak Line Mode]
    P3 --> P4A[Fix time t0]
    P4A --> P4B[Integrate u dy minus v dx equals 0]
    P4B --> P4C[Plot curve]
    P4 --> P5A[Select particle P0]
    P5A --> P5B[Integrate dx/dt equals u over t0 to t]
    P5B --> P5C[Plot trajectory]
    P5 --> P6A[Select injection point P]
    P6A --> P6B[Sweep injection time tau]
    P6B --> P6C[Plot locus of all tagged particles]
    P4C --> OUT[Flow Visualization Output]
    P5C --> OUT
    P6C --> OUT
    OUT --> APP[Vehicle Aero Application: Wake Study, Drag Analysis, Separation Detection]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Representation — Flow Past a Cylinder (Block Form)

For a physical fluid flow past a circular cylinder (a classical vehicle-aero analogue for car A-pillars, wheels, and antennae), the three line types differ in unsteady flow. Since Mermaid cannot easily render physical flow patterns, the following block diagram conveys the *information flow*:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph upstream[Upstream Region]
        UP1[Uniform Velocity U infinity]
    end
    subgraph around[Around Cylinder Body]
        AB1[Stagnation Point at theta equals 0]
        AB2[Acceleration on Upper Surface]
        AB3[Acceleration on Lower Surface]
        AB4[Deceleration in Wake]
    end
    subgraph downstream[Downstream Wake Region]
        DW1[Velocity Deficit Region]
        DW2[Recirculation Zone if unsteady]
    end
    upstream --> around
    around --> downstream
    AB1 -.->|Streamline Branches| AB2
    AB1 -.->|Streamline Branches| AB3
    AB2 --> AB4
    AB3 --> AB4
    AB4 --> DW1
    DW1 --> DW2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for Vehicle Aerodynamics:**
> A car body is a *bluff body* similar to the cylinder above. The **stagnation point** is where the front bumper/nose meets the oncoming air, the **acceleration zones** are over the hood and roof, and the **wake region** is the turbulent low-pressure zone behind the trunk that largely determines **pressure drag** (a key contributor to the vehicle's $C_D$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between a streamline, a path line, and a streak line in fluid flow. Under what condition are they identical?

**Model Answer:**

A **streamline** is a curve whose tangent at any point coincides with the instantaneous velocity direction at that point, drawn at a fixed instant of time. A **path line** is the actual trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over a period of time. A **streak line** is the locus of all fluid particles that have passed through a given fixed point in space at some prior time (commonly visualized by continuous dye injection).

**Condition of identity:** When the flow is **steady** (i.e., $\partial \mathbf{V}/\partial t = 0$), all three lines are mathematically identical. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** The velocity field in a flow is given by $\mathbf{V} = (2x)\,\hat{i} + (3y)\,\hat{j}$. Write the equation of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 1)$.

**Model Answer:**

Streamline equation (2-D, at fixed $t$):

$$
u\,dy - v\,dx = 0
$$

Substituting $u = 2x$ and $v = 3y$:

$$
2x\,dy - 3y\,dx = 0
$$

Separating variables:

$$
2x\,dy = 3y\,dx \Rightarrow \frac{2\,dy}{y} = \frac{3\,dx}{x}
$$

Integrating:

$$
2\ln y = 3\ln x + \ln C \Rightarrow \ln(y^2) = \ln(Cx^3)
$$

$$
y^2 = C x^3
$$

Applying the point $(1, 1)$: $1^2 = C(1)^3 \Rightarrow C = 1$.

**Streamline equation:** $\boxed{y^2 = x^3}$ (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, ESE Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the differential equation of a streamline in a 3-D unsteady flow field. Explain why the time variable is held constant during this derivation.

**(b) (7 Marks)** For a 2-D velocity field $\mathbf{V} = (x + y)\,\hat{i} + (x - y)\,\hat{j}$, determine:
   (i) The equation of the streamline passing through $(1, 0)$.
   (ii) The path line of a particle that starts at $(1, 0)$ at $t = 0$.
   (iii) Comment on whether the flow is steady or unsteady.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Streamline Equation (3-D):**

Let the velocity field be:

$$
\mathbf{V}(x,y,z,t) = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k}
$$

At an instant $t_0$, the streamline is a curve passing through point $P$ such that its tangent is parallel to $\mathbf{V}$. Let the position vector along the streamline be $\mathbf{r}(s) = x(s)\hat{i} + y(s)\hat{j} + z(s)\hat{k}$, where $s$ is the arc length. The unit tangent vector is:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \frac{d\mathbf{r}}{ds} = \frac{dx}{ds}\hat{i} + \frac{dy}{ds}\hat{j} + \frac{dz}{ds}\hat{k}
$$

For the streamline, $\mathbf{T} \parallel \mathbf{V}$:

$$
\frac{dx/ds}{u} = \frac{dy/ds}{v} = \frac{dz/ds}{w} = \lambda
$$

Eliminating $ds$ and the parameter $\lambda$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}}
$$

**Why time is constant:** A streamline is an **instantaneous geometric construct**. It does not represent any particle's motion over time; it is simply the *direction field* of velocities at one frozen moment. Differentiating with respect to time would describe a *path*, not a streamline. (7 marks)

---

**(b) (i) Streamline through (1, 0):**

Streamline equation (2-D): $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$

Substituting $u = x + y$ and $v = x - y$:

$$
(x+y)\,dy - (x-y)\,dx = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
x\,dy + y\,dy - x\,dx + y\,dx = 0
$$

Grouping:

$$
(x\,dy + y\,dx) + (y\,dy - x\,dx) = 0
$$

Recognizing $x\,dy + y\,dx = d(xy)$ and $y\,dy - x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}d(y^2) - \frac{1}{2}d(x^2)$:

$$
d(xy) + \frac{1}{2}d(y^2 - x^2) = 0
$$

Integrating:

$$
xy + \frac{1}{2}(y^2 - x^2) = C \Rightarrow 2xy + y^2 - x^2 = K
$$

Applying $(1, 0)$: $2(1)(0) + 0 - 1 = K \Rightarrow K = -1$.

**Streamline equation:** $\boxed{y^2 + 2xy - x^2 = -1}$ (2 marks)

---

**(b) (ii) Path line starting at (1, 0) at t = 0:**

Path line equations (note: time is variable):

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = x + y, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = x - y
$$

We solve this system of ODEs. Adding the two equations:

$$
\frac{d(x+y)}{dt} = (x+y) + (x-y) = 2x
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\frac{d(x-y)}{dt} = (x+y) - (x-y) = 2y
$$

Let $A = x + y$ and $B = x - y$. Then:

$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = 2x = A + B, \quad \frac{dB}{dt} = 2y = A - B
$$

Taking the second derivative of $A$:

$$
\frac{d^2 A}{dt^2} = \frac{dA}{dt} + \frac{dB}{dt} = (A+B) + (A-B) = 2A
$$

So $\dfrac{d^2 A}{dt^2} - 2A = 0$, with solution $A(t) = C_1 e^{\sqrt{2}\,t} + C_2 e^{-\sqrt{2}\,t}$.

Initial conditions at $t = 0$, $(x,y) = (1,0)$: $A(0) = 1$, $B(0) = 1$.

Also, $\dfrac{dA}{dt}\big|_{t=0} = A(0) + B(0) = 2$.

Solving for constants:

$$
A(0) = C_1 + C_2 = 1
$$
$$
\frac{dA}{dt}\bigg|_0 = \sqrt{2}(C_1 - C_2) = 2 \Rightarrow C_1 - C_2 = \sqrt{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_1 = \frac{1 + \sqrt{2}}{2}, \quad C_2 = \frac{1 - \sqrt{2}}{2}
$$

Similarly, $B$ satisfies $\dfrac{d^2 B}{dt^2} = 2B$ with the same characteristic roots, leading to:

$$
x(t) = \frac{A + B}{2}, \quad y(t) = \frac{A - B}{2}
$$

**Path line expressions:**

$$
\boxed{x(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[(1+\sqrt{2})\,e^{\sqrt{2}\,t} + (1-\sqrt{2})\,e^{-\sqrt{2}\,t}\right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[(1-\sqrt{2})\,e^{\sqrt{2}\,t} - (1-\sqrt{2})\,e^{-\sqrt{2}\,t}\right]}
$$

(3 marks)

---

**(b) (iii) Steady or Unsteady?**

Inspecting $\mathbf{V} = (x+y)\hat{i} + (x-y)\hat{j}$, the components depend **only on spatial coordinates** $(x, y)$, not on time $t$. Therefore $\partial \mathbf{V}/\partial t = 0$ and the flow is **steady**.

In a steady flow, streamlines, path lines, and streak lines **coincide**. (2 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a) (7 Marks)** Define streak line. Show with a neat sketch how a streak line is generated experimentally in a wind tunnel. How does it differ from a path line if the flow is unsteady?

**(b) (7 Marks)** A fluid particle moves in a flow field with velocity components $u = 1 + t$ and $v = 2$, with $x(0) = 0$, $y(0) = 0$. Find:
   (i) The path line equation $y(x)$.
   (ii) The streamline equation at $t = 2$ seconds.
   (iii) The streak line equation formed by dye injected continuously from the origin starting at $t = 0$, observed at $t = 3$ s.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Streak Line Definition and Distinction:**

**Definition (3 marks):** A **streak line** is the locus of *all* fluid particles that have passed through a *given fixed point* in the flow at some prior time. It is the curve traced by a continuous stream of dye injected at that point.

**Experimental Generation (2 marks):** In a wind tunnel, a thin stream of **smoke or colored dye** is continuously released from a fine needle/nozzle at a fixed location in the test section. A camera (or stroboscopic light) captures the resulting colored filament. The shape of this filament is the **streak line** at that instant.

**Difference from Path Line in Unsteady Flow (2 marks):**
- A **path line** tracks *one* particle over time.
- A **streak line** tracks *many* particles that all passed through the *same* point at *different* times.
- In **steady flow** they are identical, but in **unsteady flow** they diverge because different particles experience *different* velocity histories as they traverse the time-varying field.

---

**(b) (i) Path line $y(x)$:**

From $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = u = 1 + t$ with $x(0) = 0$:

$$
x(t) = \int_0^t (1 + t')\,dt' = t + \frac{t^2}{2}
$$

From $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = v = 2$ with $y(0) = 0$:

$$
y(t) = 2t
$$

Eliminating $t$: $t = y/2$, substitute:

$$
x = \frac{y}{2} + \frac{(y/2)^2}{2} = \frac{y}{2} + \frac{y^2}{8}
$$

**Path line equation:** $\boxed{x = \dfrac{y}{2} + \dfrac{y^2}{8}}$ (2 marks)

---

**(b) (ii) Streamline at $t = 2$ s:**

At $t = t_0 = 2$ s, the velocity field is $u = 1 + 2 = 3$ and $v = 2$ (constants, since they are independent of $x$ and $y$).

Streamline equation: $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0 \Rightarrow 3\,dy = 2\,dx$

Integrating:

$$
3y = 2x + C_1
$$

For a streamline through origin at this instant, $C_1 = 0$.

**Streamline at $t = 2$ s:** $\boxed{3y = 2x}$ (2 marks)

---

**(b) (iii) Streak line at $t = 3$ s:**

A particle injected at time $\tau$ (where $0 \le \tau \le 3$) at the origin will be at:

$$
x(\tau, 3) = \int_{\tau}^{3} (1 + t')\,dt' = (3 - \tau) + \frac{1}{2}(9 - \tau^2)
$$

$$
x(\tau) = 3 - \tau + \frac{9}{2} - \frac{\tau^2}{2} = \frac{15}{2} - \tau - \frac{\tau^2}{2}
$$

$$
y(\tau, 3) = \int_{\tau}^{3} 2\,dt' = 2(3 - \tau) = 6 - 2\tau
$$

Eliminating $\tau$: from the $y$-equation, $\tau = (6 - y)/2 = 3 - y/2$. Substituting into the $x$-equation:

$$
x = \frac{15}{2} - \left(3 - \frac{y}{2}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(3 - \frac{y}{2}\right)^2
$$

$$
x = \frac{15}{2} - 3 + \frac{y}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\left(9 - 3y + \frac{y^2}{4}\right)
$$

$$
x = \frac{9}{2} + \frac{y}{2} - \frac{9}{2} + \frac{3y}{2} - \frac{y^2}{8}
$$

$$
x = 2y - \frac{y^2}{8}
$$

**Streak line at $t = 3$ s:** $\boxed{x = 2y - \dfrac{y^2}{8}}$ (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to hold $t$ constant for streamlines:** A student who writes $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = dt$ for a streamline *loses 1 mark immediately*. The correct form is $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$ (no $dt$). The $dt$ belongs to the **path line** only.
>
> 2. **Mixing up path-line and streak-line integrals:** A path line has a *fixed* lower limit ($t_0$) and varying upper limit $t$. A streak line has a *varying* injection time $\tau$ as the lower limit and a fixed observation time $t$ as the upper limit. Confusing these leads to a wrong locus shape. *Lose up to 3 marks.*
>
> 3. **Assuming streamline = path line without checking steadiness:** Always state explicitly whether $\partial \mathbf{V}/\partial t = 0$ before invoking the identity. Examiners deduct for unverified assumptions. *Lose 1 mark.*
>
> 4. **Sign error in $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$:** A common slip is writing $v\,dy - u\,dx = 0$. Memorize the correct ordering: the $x$-component of velocity $u$ multiplies $dy$, and the $y$-component $v$ multiplies $dx$. *Lose 1–2 marks.*
>
> 5. **Omitting the "no-flow-across" property of streamlines:** When asked "what does a streamline represent physically", failing to mention that *no fluid crosses a streamline* costs marks. This is a key physical insight.
>
> 6. **Algebraic errors in streak-line elimination:** The elimination of $\tau$ between two parametric equations is a frequent source of errors. *Always double-check the algebra at least once.*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **streamline** is an *instantaneous* curve tangent to the velocity field at a fixed time $t_0$. Its 2-D differential form is $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$.
- A **path line** is the *trajectory of a single particle* over time, given by integrating $\dfrac{d\mathbf{r}}{dt} = \mathbf{V}$ from an initial position.
- A **streak line** is the *locus of all particles* that have passed through a *fixed injection point* at any earlier time, visualized by continuous dye injection.
- In **steady flow** ($\partial \mathbf{V}/\partial t = 0$), **streamlines = path lines = streak lines** — this is the most-tested single fact on this topic.
- In **unsteady flow**, the three lines are *distinct*, and the path/streak line integral form depends explicitly on time.
- Streamlines are used in **CFD post-processing** to visualize wake topology and identify **flow separation** behind vehicles.
- Path lines are used in **Lagrangian particle tracking** (e.g., dust, spray, exhaust soot dispersion around moving cars).
- Streak lines are used in **experimental wind-tunnel smoke visualization** for qualitative assessment of flow patterns.
- The *tangent* to a streamline is the unit vector $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{V} / \vert \mathbf{V} \vert$, where $\vert \mathbf{V} \vert = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$.
- For **2-D incompressible flow**, streamlines can be obtained from the **stream function** $\psi(x,y)$ via $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$.
- **Vehicle aerodynamicists** rely on the streamline identity in steady cruise conditions but must invoke unsteady analysis during **transient maneuvers** (braking, cornering, overtaking, gust encounters).
- Remember to **always state the time parameter** ($t_0$ for streamlines, $t$ for path lines, $\tau$ for streak lines) in your derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Visualization Techniques - Types and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Visualization Techniques - Types and Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flow Visualization** is the experimental technique of rendering an otherwise invisible fluid flow field (air, water, or any working fluid) into a perceivable form — visual, photographic, or digital — so that streamlines, vortices, separation zones, shock waves, and boundary-layer transitions can be observed, recorded, and quantitatively analyzed. In the context of **Vehicle Aerodynamics (OEAUT723)**, flow visualization is the bridge between theoretical/aerodynamic predictions and physical reality, allowing engineers to verify the behavior of airflow over a road vehicle, race car, or aircraft under controlled test conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Flow Visualization falls under Module 1 of OEAUT723 — *Vehicle Aerodynamics*. It directly supports **CO1** (Understand fundamental aerodynamic principles and flow behavior) and **CO2** (Apply experimental methods to analyze flow over vehicles). It carries an indicative weightage of **2 to 3 marks** in Part A and frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-part in Part B.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing beside a perfectly clear, still swimming pool at night. You cannot see the water currents. Now imagine sprinkling a thin layer of biodegradable confetti on the surface. The instant you disturb the water, the confetti traces the current patterns, swirling into eddies, accelerating around obstacles, and lingering in stagnation zones. 

**Flow visualization does exactly this for air.** Air is colorless and usually transparent, so engineers must introduce "tracers" — whether they are smoke particles, tufts of yarn, oil streaks, or laser-illuminated aerosols — to make the flow patterns visible. 

- **Smoke** in a wind tunnel is like the confetti in the pool.
- **Tufts** taped on a car body are like grass blades bent by the wind, instantly showing direction and reversed flow (separation).
- **Schlieren imaging** is like a heat-haze photograph, but made ultra-precise to capture the density gradients around a moving object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Constants in Flow Visualization:**
> - **Refractive index of air (n ≈ 1.000293)** at standard sea-level conditions
> - **Density of air (ρ = 1.225 kg/m³)** at ISA standard conditions
> - **Dynamic viscosity (μ = 1.789 × 10⁻⁵ N·s/m²)** at 15 °C
> - **Speed of sound in air (a = 340.3 m/s)** at 15 °C

### 1.3 Why Flow Visualization Matters in Vehicle Aerodynamics

| Engineering Decision | Visualization Method Used | Insight Gained |
|---|---|---|
| Drag reduction of a sedan | Smoke or fog in a wind tunnel | Location and size of the wake behind the rear window |
| Spoiler/wing design of an F1 car | Yarn tufts on body and wing | Attachment, separation, and reattachment zones |
| Cooling airflow through a radiator | Oil-film or fluorescent paint | Streamline patterns on inlet ducts |
| Underbody diffuser design | Surface flow visualization (naphthalene) | Boundary-layer transition and separation on the floor |
| Transonic flow over a wing-mirror | Schlieren photography | Shock-wave position and strength |
| CFD code validation | PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) | Full 2D/3D velocity field comparison |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Flow Visualization Techniques

Flow visualization methods are classified by the **physical property** they exploit to render the flow visible. The taxonomy below is structured exactly as required for the KTU 2024 theory paper.

#### A. Surface-Flow Visualization Methods
These techniques require a **solid wall** (vehicle body) where the flow leaves a trace.

1. **Yarn Tufts**
   - Small tufts of yarn glued or taped onto the model surface.
   - Each tuft aligns with the local flow direction.
   - **Indicator of:** attachment, separation, and reversal.
   - **Application:** Race-car bodywork, wing surfaces, diffuser ramps.
   - **Limitation:** Qualitative only; no quantitative velocity data.

2. **Oil-Film Technique (Oil-Streak Method)**
   - A mixture of pigmented oil (e.g., titanium dioxide in kerosene) is applied as a thin film.
   - Under surface shear, the oil streaks align with the near-wall streamlines.
   - **Indicator of:** boundary-layer direction, separation lines, and attachment lines.
   - **Application:** Aircraft wings, road-vehicle underbodies.

3. **Sublimation Technique (Naphthalene Method)**
   - A coating of naphthalene sublimates faster in high-shear regions.
   - Visualizes shear-stress distribution as a non-uniform thickness pattern.
   - **Indicator of:** boundary-layer transition (laminar → turbulent).
   - **Application:** High-speed wind-tunnel models, transonic tests.

4. **Soot/Chalk Deposit Method**
   - Fine carbon (soot) or chalk powder is dusted on the surface.
   - The flow sweeps the powder along, leaving clear paths.
   - **Indicator of:** qualitative flow direction.

5. **Pressure-Sensitive Paint (PSP)**
   - A luminescent coating whose emission intensity is inversely proportional to local oxygen partial pressure.
   - Excited by UV light, captured by a CCD camera.
   - **Indicator of:** full-field **pressure distribution**.
   - **Application:** Aircraft wings, F1 front wings.

#### B. Streamline / Pathline Visualization Methods
These introduce a tracer into the **freestream** to visualize the bulk flow.

1. **Smoke Generators**
   - Smoke is produced by vaporizing oil, kerosene, or glycol.
   - Particle size ≈ **0.5 to 5 µm** to faithfully follow the flow.
   - **Application:** Automotive wind tunnels, low-speed aerodynamic tests.

2. **Fog / Vapor Screen**
   - A fine mist of water droplets or glycol is generated and illuminated by a light sheet.
   - The droplets scatter light and trace streamlines.
   - **Application:** Full-vehicle wind-tunnel tests, V/STOL aircraft studies.

3. **Helium-Bubble Technique**
   - Soap bubbles filled with helium (density ≈ 0.18 kg/m³) are released upstream.
   - Bubbles are neutrally buoyant in air and follow streamlines faithfully.

#### C. Optical Methods (Density-Gradient Visualization)
These do **not** introduce tracers; they exploit the variation of the **refractive index** of air with density.

1. **Shadowgraph Method**
   - Simplest optical method. A point light source casts shadows of density gradients.
   - Captures the **second spatial derivative** of density: $\nabla^2 \rho$.
   - **Best for:** shock waves, plumes, convection cells.
   - **Limitation:** Lower sensitivity than Schlieren.

2. **Schlieren Method**
   - Uses a knife-edge or color filter at the focal plane of a parabolic mirror.
   - Captures the **first spatial derivative** of density: $\partial \rho / \partial x$.
   - Two configurations: **single-pass (Toepler)** and **double-pass (folded)**.
   - **Application:** Supersonic flow, F1 rear-wing vortex visualization, transonic wing testing.

3. **Interferometry (Mach-Zehnder / Michelson)**
   - Splits a coherent laser beam into two paths — one through the flow, one as a reference.
   - Recombined beams produce **fringe patterns**.
   - Captures the **density itself**: $\rho(x,y)$.
   - **Application:** High-precision density measurement, plasma flows.

4. **Holographic Interferometry**
   - Records the full 3D wavefront; multiple holograms are compared to detect density changes.
   - **Application:** Transonic and supersonic flow, automotive engine in-cylinder studies.

#### D. Quantitative Velocity Field Methods

1. **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)**
   - A thin laser sheet (typically Nd:YAG, 532 nm) illuminates seed particles.
   - Two consecutive images are captured and cross-correlated to obtain displacement vectors.
   - **Output:** full 2D or 3D velocity field.
   - **Application:** CFD validation, race-car wake characterization.

2. **Laser Doppler Velocimetry (LDV)**
   - Two laser beams intersect at a small probe volume.
   - Particles crossing the volume scatter light, producing a Doppler frequency shift.
   - **Output:** pointwise velocity with extreme accuracy.

3. **Particle Tracking Velocimetry (PTV)**
   - Tracks individual particles across multiple frames; ideal for low-density seeding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list and briefly explain flow visualization techniques," students must classify them by the underlying physical principle (surface, tracer, optical, quantitative). A bare list without classification is treated as incomplete and attracts a partial-mark deduction.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table collects every governing equation you must memorize for the OEAUT723 theory paper. All symbols, units, and limits are listed for quick revision.

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n - 1 = \frac{\rho}{\rho_s} \cdot C$ | Gladstone–Dale relation (refractive index vs density) | $n$ dimensionless, $C$ = 0.000226 m³/kg for air | Schlieren, Interferometry |
| 2 | $\Delta y = f \cdot \tan(\varepsilon) \cdot L \cdot \frac{1}{n_0} \frac{dn}{dx} \cdot \Delta x$ | Schlieren ray deflection (Toepler, simplified) | $\Delta y$ = screen displacement (m) | Schlieren |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = L \int \frac{1}{n} \frac{\partial n}{\partial y} \, dy$ | Schlieren total angular deflection (line integral) | $\varepsilon$ in radians | Schlieren |
| 4 | $I(x) = I_0 \exp\left( -\alpha \int_0^L \rho(x,y) \, dy \right)$ | Beer–Lambert law (shadow intensity) | $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient | Shadowgraph |
| 5 | $\text{Fringe shift} = \frac{L}{\lambda} (n - n_0)$ | Interferometry fringe equation | $L$ = path length, $\lambda$ = wavelength | Interferometry |
| 6 | $U = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | PIV velocity from cross-correlation | $\Delta x$ in m, $\Delta t$ in s | PIV |
| 7 | $v = \frac{f_D \cdot \lambda}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$ | LDV velocity from Doppler shift | $f_D$ in Hz | LDV |
| 8 | $M = \frac{V}{a}$ | Mach number (decides which method applies) | $M < 0.3$ tracer methods, $M > 0.8$ optical | All methods |
| 9 | $\tau_w = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0}$ | Wall shear stress (basis of sublimation & oil film) | $\tau_w$ in N/m² | Surface flow |
| 10 | $C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2}$ | Pressure coefficient (linked to PSP output) | dimensionless | PSP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Avoidance Note:** The vertical bar character `|` is forbidden inside the table because it breaks the markdown parser. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX for absolute values. The formulas above already use safe notation.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry** (F1, EV manufacturers): Tuft and PIV studies cut thousands of CFD-validation hours by quickly identifying wake asymmetry and floor-separation bubbles.
- **Aviation industry**: Schlieren and PSP reveal shock-boundary-layer interaction on transonic wings — a problem that pure CFD still struggles to resolve near the wing root.
- **Motorsport aerodynamics**: The FIA uses standardized smoke-rake and tuft-grid tests to enforce technical regulations on race cars.
- **CFD Validation**: Every commercial CFD code (ANSYS Fluent, STAR-CCM+, OpenFOAM) is benchmarked against PIV/LDV data from the **Oklahoma State University** and **Calspan/UB** wind-tunnel databases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides rigorous derivations of the most examiner-relevant relations. Every algebraic step is fully expanded; nothing is abbreviated.

### 3.1 Derivation: Gladstone–Dale Relation (Foundation of Schlieren & Interferometry)

The refractive index of a gas depends on its density. For air (and most common gases) under the conditions of aerodynamic testing, this relationship is linear.

**Step 1: Empirical starting point.**
For a gas at constant temperature and composition, experiments show that

$$
n - 1 \;\propto\; \rho
$$

**Step 2: Introduce the Gladstone–Dale constant.**
A proportionality constant $C$ converts the proportionality into equality. By definition, $C$ is the Gladstone–Dale constant.

$$
n - 1 = C \cdot \rho
$$

**Step 3: Normalize using a reference state.**
Divide both sides by the reference density $\rho_0$ (typically at 1 atm, 288.15 K) to obtain the more commonly tabulated form.

$$
n - 1 \;=\; \frac{\rho}{\rho_0} \cdot C \rho_0
$$

**Step 4: Combine the constants.**
Define a single new constant $C_0 = C \rho_0$. The textbook form is

$$
\boxed{\,n - 1 \;=\; C_0 \,\frac{\rho}{\rho_0}\,}
$$

For air at $\lambda = 589.3$ nm (sodium D-line), $C_0 = 2.93 \times 10^{-4}$, and at standard conditions $n \approx 1.000293$.

**Step 5: Differentiate to relate $\nabla n$ to $\nabla \rho$.**
Differentiating both sides with respect to a spatial coordinate $x$,

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial x} \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\rho_0} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}
$$

This derivative is the fundamental input to the Schlieren equation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Schlieren Ray-Deflection Equation (Toepler Setup)

Consider a point source of light $S$, two parabolic mirrors of focal length $f$, separated by distance $2f$, and a knife-edge placed at the focal point of the second mirror. A test section of width $L$ is at distance $f$ from the second mirror.

**Step 1: Geometry of a single ray.**
A light ray entering the test section parallel to the optical axis is refracted by the density gradient. The angle of refraction $\varepsilon$ is, from Snell's law applied differentially,

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \int_{0}^{L} \frac{1}{n}\frac{\partial n}{\partial y}\,dy
$$

**Step 2: Use the Gladstone–Dale relation to convert $\partial n$ to $\partial \rho$.**
Substituting the derivative from Step 5 of §3.1,

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\rho_0 \, n} \int_{0}^{L} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y}\,dy
$$

**Step 3: Convert integral to a finite difference across the test section.**
For small regions, $\int (\partial \rho/\partial y)\,dy \approx \Delta \rho$, so

$$
\varepsilon \;\approx\; \frac{C_0}{\rho_0 \, n} \Delta \rho
$$

**Step 4: Convert angular deflection to screen displacement.**
A ray deflected by $\varepsilon$ will, on reaching the screen at the focal plane of the second mirror, be displaced by

$$
\Delta y \;=\; f \tan(\varepsilon)
$$

For small angles, $\tan \varepsilon \approx \varepsilon$, hence

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta y \;=\; f \,\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{f \,C_0}{\rho_0 \, n} \Delta \rho\,}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**
The screen displacement $\Delta y$ — and therefore the brightness variation in the Schlieren image — is **linearly proportional to the density difference $\Delta \rho$** along the test section. This linearity is what makes the Schlieren method an ideal **first-derivative density probe**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Fringe Equation for Mach–Zehnder Interferometer

**Step 1: Optical path difference.**
The optical path length through the test section of physical length $L$ and refractive index $n$ is $nL$. The reference path has length $n_0 L$ in the same geometric length. The optical path difference (OPD) is

$$
\text{OPD} \;=\; (n - n_0)\,L
$$

**Step 2: Convert OPD to phase difference.**
The phase difference is

$$
\Delta \phi \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \, \text{OPD} \;=\; \frac{2\pi L (n - n_0)}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3: Count the fringe shifts.**
Each whole wavelength of OPD corresponds to **one fringe shift**. The number of fringe shifts $N$ is

$$
N \;=\; \frac{\text{OPD}}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{L(n - n_0)}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Gladstone–Dale relation.**

$$
\boxed{\,N \;=\; \frac{L \,C_0}{\lambda \,\rho_0}\, (\rho - \rho_0)\,}
$$

**Step 5: Use the isentropic relation to convert $\rho$ to pressure.**
For low-speed flows, $p/p_0 = (\rho/\rho_0)^\gamma$ where $\gamma = 1.4$ for air. For small density changes, the linearized form is

$$
\frac{\rho - \rho_0}{\rho_0} \;\approx\; \frac{p - p_0}{\gamma p_0}
$$

Substituting yields

$$
N \;=\; \frac{L \,C_0}{\lambda \,\gamma p_0}\,(p - p_0)
$$

This final form directly connects the **counted fringe shifts** to the **pressure change** — a relation that is fundamental in transonic wind-tunnel testing of wings and rotors.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

The single most important decision a vehicle-aerodynamics engineer must make is: *Which visualization method suits my test?* The matrix below organizes the choice by flow regime and measurement goal.

| Flow Regime | Mach Range | Surface vs Freestream | Best Method | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-speed sedan | $M < 0.15$ | Surface | Tufts + Oil-film | Tracers are unnecessary; surface methods are cheap and fast |
| Low-speed sedan | $M < 0.15$ | Freestream | Smoke / PIV | Quantitative wake data for drag studies |
| F1 aerodynamics | $0.2 \leq M \leq 0.4$ | Both | Tufts + Schlieren | Schlieren captures vortex cores; tufts show separation |
| Transonic wing | $0.7 \leq M \leq 1.3$ | Both | Schlieren + PSP | Optical method essential to see shocks; PSP gives pressure |
| Supersonic vehicle | $M > 1.5$ | Both | Shadowgraph + Interferometry | Schlieren saturates; interferometry gives accurate density |
| Hypersonic / Re-entry | $M > 5$ | Both | Holographic interferometry | Need 3D density field |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Validation Logic:** A KTU Part-B answer that fails to justify *why* a particular method is chosen — referencing the Mach range, the geometry (surface vs freestream), and the qualitative vs quantitative requirement — will be capped at half marks on that sub-part.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Schlieren Sensitivity)

**Problem.** A Schlieren system with $f = 1.5$ m, $L = 0.4$ m, and a knife-edge cut-off of 50% (i.e., half the image is blocked) is used to view a flow whose density varies by $\Delta \rho = 0.05$ kg/m³. Calculate the angular deflection of the ray and the screen displacement. (Use $C_0 = 2.93 \times 10^{-4}$, $n \approx 1.000293$, $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³.)

**Solution.**

**Step 1: Compute angular deflection.**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{C_0}{\rho_0 \, n}\,\Delta \rho
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{2.93 \times 10^{-4}}{1.225 \times 1.000293} \times 0.05
$$

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{2.93 \times 10^{-4}}{1.22536} \times 0.05
$$

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; 2.391 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\varepsilon \;=\; 1.196 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad}\,}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to degrees for cross-check.**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{deg}} \;=\; 1.196 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \;=\; 6.85 \times 10^{-4} \text{ degrees}
$$

**Step 3: Compute screen displacement.**

$$
\Delta y \;=\; f \tan \varepsilon \;\approx\; f \varepsilon
$$

$$
\Delta y \;=\; 1.5 \times 1.196 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 1.794 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta y \;=\; 17.94 \text{ µm}\,}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret.**
Even a small density difference of 0.05 kg/m³ (a 4 % change from standard) produces a 17.9 µm displacement on the screen — easily resolved by a modern camera sensor. The method is highly sensitive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The four mermaid diagrams below summarize the classification, optical architecture, decision logic, and PIV workflow. Every node identifier is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword collisions.

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Flow Visualization Techniques

```mermaid
graph TD

    B3 --> C3A[Shadowgraph]
```
