## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Drag Force from Pressure Integration on a Notchback

We start from the basic definition of pressure force on a body. Consider a closed control surface $S$ surrounding the vehicle. The net pressure force on the body in the streamwise ($x$) direction is obtained by integrating the gauge pressure (with the negative sign indicating force on the fluid, not the body) over the body surface.

$$
F_D = -\oint_{S} (p - p_\infty) \, \hat{n} \cdot \hat{i} \, dA
$$

The unit normal $\hat{n}$ points **outward from the body** (into the fluid), and $\hat{i}$ is the unit vector in the freestream direction.

**Step 1 — Decompose the surface integral** into a **front portion** and a **rear portion** (relative to the maximum cross-section location):

$$
F_D = -\int_{S_{\text{front}}} (p - p_\infty) \cos\phi_{\text{front}} \, dA \;-\; \int_{S_{\text{rear}}} (p - p_\infty) \cos\phi_{\text{rear}} \, dA
$$

Here $\phi$ is the angle between the local outward normal and the freestream direction.

**Step 2 — Apply the pressure coefficient definition** to non-dimensionalize:

$$
C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_D = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} \int_{S_{\text{front}}} C_p \cos\phi_{\text{front}} \, dA \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} \int_{S_{\text{rear}}} C_p \cos\phi_{\text{rear}} \, dA
$$

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize the area** by the frontal area $A_f$:

$$
F_D = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} A_f \left[ \int_{S_{\text{front}}} C_p \cos\phi_{\text{front}} \, d\bar{A} \;+\; \int_{S_{\text{rear}}} C_p \cos\phi_{\text{rear}} \, d\bar{A} \right]
$$

where $\bar{A} = A / A_f$.

**Step 4 — Define the drag coefficient** by inspection:

$$
C_D = -\int_{S} C_p \, \cos\phi \, d\bar{A}
$$

For a notchback, this is dominated by the **negative $C_p$ acting on the rear-facing surfaces** (the trunk lid's top and the rear panel). Because the pressure on these rear surfaces is much **lower than freestream** (large negative $C_p$ in the wake), the integral of $C_p \cos\phi$ over the rear is large and negative, yielding a large positive $C_D$.

**Step 5 — Approximate the rear of a notchback as a flat base** of area $A_b$ with uniform base pressure coefficient $\bar{C}_{p,b}$:

$$
C_{D,\text{base}} \approx -\bar{C}_{p,b} \cdot \frac{A_b}{A_f}
$$

For a typical notchback with $\bar{C}_{p,b} \approx -0.45$ and $A_b / A_f \approx 0.35$:

$$
C_{D,\text{base}} \approx -(-0.45)(0.35) = 0.158
$$

This single contribution explains roughly half of the total $C_D \approx 0.32$ for a notchback sedan — the rest comes from the slanted rear window (where separation initiates) and viscous skin friction.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Rear Window Slant Angle on $C_D$ (Simplified Model)

A simple empirical correlation used in preliminary design relates the notchback drag coefficient to the rear window angle $\theta$ (measured from vertical):

$$
C_D \approx C_{D,0} + k \cdot \tan^{2}(\theta - \theta_{\text{opt}})
$$

where:
- $C_{D,0}$ = minimum achievable drag coefficient at the optimum slant angle
- $k$ = sensitivity coefficient
- $\theta_{\text{opt}}$ = optimum slant angle (typically 30° for a notchback)
- $\theta$ = actual slant angle in degrees

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**

Given a notchback sedan with:
- $C_{D,0} = 0.26$
- $k = 0.45$
- $\theta_{\text{opt}} = 30°$
- Actual slant angle $\theta = 25°$

Calculate the drag coefficient.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the angle deviation:**

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta - \theta_{\text{opt}} = 25° - 30° = -5°
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\tan(\Delta\theta)$:**

$$
\tan(-5°) = -0.0875
$$

**Step 3 — Square the value:**

$$
\tan^{2}(-5°) = (0.0875)^{2} = 0.00766
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $k$:**

$$
k \cdot \tan^{2}(\Delta\theta) = 0.45 \times 0.00766 = 0.00345
$$

**Step 5 — Add to the baseline $C_{D,0}$:**

$$
C_D = 0.26 + 0.00345 = 0.2635 \approx 0.264
$$

This shows that a 5° deviation from the optimal slant angle causes only a modest $C_D$ increase — production cars are designed slightly off-optimum to balance aesthetics and packaging.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Required to Overcome Notchback Drag at Highway Speed

Given:
- $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (ISA sea level)
- $U_\infty = 120 \text{ km/h} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$
- $C_D = 0.33$
- $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute the dynamic pressure:**

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(33.33)^{2}
$$

$$
q = (0.6125)(1110.9) = 680.4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the drag force:**

$$
F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A_f = 680.4 \times 0.33 \times 2.2
$$

$$
F_D = 680.4 \times 0.726 = 494.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power dissipated against drag:**

$$
P_D = F_D \cdot U_\infty = 494.0 \times 33.33
$$

$$
P_D = 16{,}466 \text{ W} \approx 16.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to horsepower:**

$$
P_D = \frac{16{,}466}{745.7} = 22.08 \text{ hp}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:** Of the engine's available power at 120 km/h (typically 25–35 hp for a cruising sedan), roughly **65–80%** is consumed just by air resistance in a notchback. This is why **reducing $C_D$ by 0.03** (e.g., from 0.33 to 0.30) saves approximately **1.5 kW** of continuous power demand at highway cruise.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Notchback vs Fastback vs Hatchback

| **Parameter** | **Notchback** | **Fastback** | **Hatchback** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of "boxes" | 3 (engine, cabin, boot) | 2 (engine + cabin-roof-tail) | 2 (engine + cabin) |
| Rear window angle (from vert) | 25°–35° | 40°–60° | 50°–75° |
| Typical $C_D$ | 0.30 – 0.36 | 0.25 – 0.30 | 0.28 – 0.34 |
| Boot/trunk volume | Large, isolated | None (or small) | Moderate, shared with cabin |
| Wake structure | Twin trailing vortices | Attached, narrower wake | Disturbed by rear wiper/edge |
| Pressure recovery | Poor (abrupt break) | Good (smooth curve) | Moderate |
| Styling flexibility | High (separate boot) | Low (swooping roof) | High |
| Cargo access | Via trunk lid only | Via trunk lid | Via large hatch |
| Crosswind sensitivity | Moderate | Low | Moderate-High |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Notchback drag. What is the typical range of drag coefficient $C_D$ for a production notchback sedan?**

**Model Answer:**

A **notchback** is a three-box vehicle body style (engine compartment, passenger cabin, and a distinct, separated trunk/boot) characterized by a near-vertical or steeply inclined rear window meeting a horizontal deck (trunk lid) at a **convex break point**. **Notchback drag** refers to the aerodynamic resistance force generated on such a body, dominantly by **pressure drag (form drag)** arising from **abrupt flow separation at the rear window–trunk junction** and the resultant **low-pressure turbulent wake** behind the trunk.

The typical drag coefficient for a production notchback sedan lies in the range:
$$
C_D \approx 0.30 \text{ to } 0.36
$$

This is higher than that of a fastback ($C_D \approx 0.25$–$0.30$) because of the abrupt geometric break that prevents smooth pressure recovery along the rear.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the rear window slant angle on the drag of a notchback vehicle. Mention the critical angle at which the wake structure changes.**

**Model Answer:**

The **rear window slant angle** $\theta$ (measured from vertical) is the single most influential geometric parameter on notchback drag. It governs:

1. **Location and severity of flow separation** at the rear window–trunk junction.
2. **Size and intensity of the trailing vortex pair** in the wake.
3. **Magnitude of the base pressure coefficient** $\bar{C}_{p,b}$ behind the trunk.

- At **small slant angles** ($\theta < 20°$), the rear is nearly vertical — separation occurs far upstream, the wake is large, and $C_D$ is high ($> 0.40$).
- At **moderate slant angles** ($\theta \approx 25°$–$35°$, typical production), a compromise is achieved between cabin headroom/aesthetics and aerodynamics ($C_D \approx 0.30$–$0.36$).
- At **larger slant angles** ($\theta > 40°$), the body begins to behave like a fastback with smoother flow attachment and lower $C_D$.

The **critical slant angle**, identified in the classic Ahmed Body study (1984), is:
$$
\theta_{\text{crit}} \approx 30°
$$

At this angle, the wake structure undergoes a **sudden transition** between an axisymmetric bubble-type wake and an asymmetric twin-vortex wake, causing a **discontinuous jump** in the base pressure coefficient.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

A production notchback sedan is travelling at a steady highway speed of **$V = 100$ km/h** through still air at standard sea-level conditions. The vehicle has a frontal area of **$A_f = 2.15$ m²** and a measured drag coefficient of **$C_D = 0.34$**.

**(a)** Calculate the **aerodynamic drag force** acting on the vehicle at this speed. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the **power required to overcome this aerodynamic drag**, and comment on the **percentage saving in power** if the manufacturer redesigns the rear of the car to reduce $C_D$ to **$0.30$** (assume speed, frontal area, and air density unchanged). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $V = 100$ km/h $= \dfrac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (ISA sea level)
- $A_f = 2.15$ m²
- $C_{D,1} = 0.34$ (original)
- $C_{D,2} = 0.30$ (redesigned)

**Part (a) — Drag Force Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the dynamic pressure $q$:

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(27.78)^{2}
$$

$$
q = (0.6125)(771.6) = 472.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the drag equation:

$$
F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A_f
$$

$$
F_{D,1} = 472.6 \times 0.34 \times 2.15
$$

$$
F_{D,1} = 472.6 \times 0.731 = 345.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Answer for Part (a):**
$$
\boxed{F_{D,1} \approx 345.5 \text{ N}}
$$

**Part (b) — Power and Percentage Saving:**

**Step 1:** Compute the original power:

$$
P_{D,1} = F_{D,1} \cdot V = 345.5 \times 27.78
$$

$$
P_{D,1} = 9598 \text{ W} \approx 9.60 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the redesigned drag force:

$$
F_{D,2} = 472.6 \times 0.30 \times 2.15 = 472.6 \times 0.645 = 304.8 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the redesigned power:

$$
P_{D,2} = 304.8 \times 27.78 = 8467 \text{ W} \approx 8.47 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the absolute power saving:

$$
\Delta P = P_{D,1} - P_{D,2} = 9.60 - 8.47 = 1.13 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the percentage saving:

$$
\% \text{Saving} = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{D,1}} \times 100 = \frac{1.13}{9.60} \times 100 \approx 11.77\%
$$

**Answer for Part (b):**
$$
\boxed{P_{D,1} \approx 9.60 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{D,2} \approx 8.47 \text{ kW}, \quad \text{Saving} \approx 11.77\%}
$$

**Examiner's Comments:** Note that the **fractional reduction in $C_D$** is $\Delta C_D / C_{D,1} = 0.04 / 0.34 = 11.76\%$. Since $P_D$ is **linearly proportional to $C_D$** at fixed speed, the percentage power saving equals the percentage $C_D$ reduction — a key conceptual point.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the aid of a **clear sketch**, describe the **wake structure behind a notchback vehicle body**. Explain the role of the **rear window slant angle** in determining the wake topology. (7 marks)

**(b)** A simplified notchback has a **trunk base area of $0.75$ m²** and a **frontal area of $2.10$ m²**. Wind tunnel testing at $U_\infty = 30$ m/s reveals an **average base pressure coefficient $\bar{C}_{p,b} = -0.42$**. Estimate the **base pressure drag coefficient** and the **base pressure drag force**. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Wake Structure Description:**

**Step 1:** Describe the body geometry. A notchback has three clearly demarcated boxes. The critical region is the **convex corner** where the sloped rear window meets the horizontal trunk deck. Air cannot negotiate this sharp turn, so it separates.

**Step 2:** Describe the separation process. The boundary layer detaches at the trunk leading edge. A **free shear layer** is shed into the wake. The shear layer is **Kelvin–Helmholtz unstable** and rolls up into a **pair of counter-rotating trailing vortices** — one on each side of the vehicle centerline (mirror-symmetric).

**Step 3:** Describe the wake structure. The wake consists of:
- A **central low-pressure recirculation bubble** directly behind the trunk.
- **Twin longitudinal trailing vortices** on either side of the centerline.
- A **horseshoe-shaped cross-section** in the near wake that elongates downstream.
- The wake may persist for **1.5–2.0 car lengths** before dissipating.

**Step 4:** Discuss the slant angle effect. As $\theta$ increases from 0° toward 60°:
- For $\theta < 30°$: Wake is **axisymmetric**, dominated by a single recirculation bubble.
- For $\theta > 30°$: Wake becomes **asymmetric**, twin trailing vortices dominate, base pressure **recovers** (becomes less negative).
- The **transition is abrupt** (a "bifurcation") at $\theta_{\text{crit}} \approx 30°$, a hallmark finding of the Ahmed Body study (1984).

**Part (b) — Base Pressure Drag Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the area ratio:

$$
\frac{A_b}{A_f} = \frac{0.75}{2.10} = 0.3571
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the base pressure drag coefficient formula:

$$
C_{D,\text{base}} = -\bar{C}_{p,b} \cdot \frac{A_b}{A_f}
$$

$$
C_{D,\text{base}} = -(-0.42)(0.3571) = 0.42 \times 0.3571 = 0.150
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the dynamic pressure:

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(30)^{2} = (0.6125)(900) = 551.25 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the base pressure drag force:

$$
F_{D,\text{base}} = q \cdot C_{D,\text{base}} \cdot A_f
$$

$$
F_{D,\text{base}} = 551.25 \times 0.150 \times 2.10
$$

$$
F_{D,\text{base}} = 551.25 \times 0.315 = 173.6 \text{ N}
$$

**Answer for Part (b):**
$$
\boxed{C_{D,\text{base}} \approx 0.150, \quad F_{D,\text{base}} \approx 173.6 \text{ N}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A base drag contribution of $0.150$ is **enormous** — it represents roughly **45% of the typical total $C_D \approx 0.33$** for a notchback. This single number underscores **why the rear-end design is the single most important aerodynamic intervention point** for a notchback sedan.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $C_{p,b}$:** Students frequently forget that $\bar{C}_{p,b}$ is **negative** (since base pressure is below freestream). The minus sign in the formula $C_{D,\text{base}} = -\bar{C}_{p,b}(A_b/A_f)$ converts it to a **positive drag contribution**. Writing $C_{D,\text{base}} = +0.42 \times 0.357 = 0.15$ without the negative sign is a **common 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Unit Conversions:** Speed in km/h must be converted to m/s **before** squaring. Forgetting this factor of $(1000/3600)^{2} \approx 1/13$ will give a wildly incorrect drag force.
> 3. **Confusing pressure and viscous drag:** Notchbacks are dominantly **pressure drag** devices. Writing $C_{D,\text{skin friction}}$ as the major term loses marks.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch:** Part (a) of Question B explicitly requires a sketch. Drawing **only a generic car outline** without labeling the separation point, vortex pair, and recirculation zone will lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the slant-angle critical value:** Many students know that $\theta$ matters but cannot quote the **$\approx 30°$ critical transition angle** from the Ahmed Body study — this is a **2-mark favorite** in KTU Part A.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Notchback Drag**

- **Definition:** Notchback = 3-box sedan; notchback drag = pressure drag dominated by rear wake.
- **Typical $C_D$ Range:** **0.30 – 0.36** (production sedans).
- **Dominant Drag Mechanism:** **Pressure drag (form drag)**, NOT skin friction. Base wake contribution alone can be $\approx 0.15$ of the total $C_D$.
- **Critical Geometric Feature:** The **convex break point** where the rear window meets the trunk deck. This is where **boundary layer separation** initiates.
- **Wake Structure:** **Twin counter-rotating trailing vortices** + central recirculation bubble, persisting **1.5–2.0 car lengths** downstream.
- **Rear Window Slant Angle $\theta$:** Measured from vertical. Typical = **25°–35°**. **Critical transition at $\theta_{\text{crit}} \approx 30°$** (Ahmed Body, 1984).
- **Master Equation (must memorize):** $\boxed{F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^{2} C_D A_f}$
- **Power Equation:** $\boxed{P_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f U_\infty^{3}}$ — note the **cubic dependence on speed**.
- **Base Drag Approximation:** $\boxed{C_{D,\text{base}} = -\bar{C}_{p,b}\,(A_b/A_f)}$
- **Reduction Strategies (mention 4–5 in essays):**
  1. **Boat-tailing** (tapering the rear to reduce base area).
  2. **Increasing rear window slant angle** toward 30°–40°.
  3. **Larger fillet radius** at the rear window–trunk junction to delay separation.
  4. **Base bleed / suction** to raise base pressure.
  5. **Vortex generators** on the rear window to energize the boundary layer.
- **Comparison Anchors to Remember:**
  - Notchback $C_D \approx 0.30$–$0.36$
  - Fastback $C_D \approx 0.25$–$0.30$
  - Modern EV (Tesla Model S) $C_D \approx 0.208$
  - Ideal streamlined body $C_D \approx 0.04$
- **Industry Insight:** A **0.01 reduction in $C_D$** yields **~2–3% EV range gain** — quote this in design questions.
- **Reference Atmospheric Values (always use these unless specified):**
  - $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
  - $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s)
  - $V_{\text{test, common}} = 120$ km/h or 100 km/h
- **Keyword Index for KTU Answer Writing:**
  - "Pressure drag," "Form drag," "Base wake," "Separation point," "Trailing vortex pair," "Convex corner," "Ahmed body," "Slant angle," "Boat-tail," "Base bleed," "Rear window–trunk junction."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hatch Back Drag and Effect of Down Force on Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hatch Back Drag and Effect of Down Force on Vehicle Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hatchback Body Style — Aerodynamic Context

A **hatchback** is a passenger car body style characterised by a vertical or near-vertical **rear tailgate** that opens upward, with the cargo (boot) volume integrated into the passenger cabin (no separate boot/trunk). From an aerodynamics perspective, this **abruptly truncated rear geometry** is the single most influential design feature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Hatchback drag* refers to the aerodynamic resistance produced by hatchback passenger cars due to their short rear deck and steeply raked or near-vertical tailgate, which promotes massive boundary-layer separation and a large, low-pressure wake region behind the vehicle.

Compared to a **notchback** (three-box saloon with a distinct boot) and a **fastback** (sloping roofline that tapers smoothly into the rear), the hatchback falls in between — closer to a notchback in rear-end bluntness when the tailgate is near-vertical, and closer to a fastback when the rear glass is steeply raked.

| Body Style | Rear Geometry | Typical Cd Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Fastback** (e.g., Tesla Model S) | Smooth taper into tail | 0.22 – 0.26 |
| **Notchback / Sedan** (e.g., Honda City) | Distinct boot lip, near-vertical rear screen | 0.28 – 0.34 |
| **Hatchback** (e.g., Maruti Swift) | Vertical tailgate, abrupt cut-off | 0.30 – 0.40 |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Why is the Rear So Important?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine sticking a flat plate perpendicular to a flowing river. The water slams into the plate, splits sideways, and leaves a turbulent "shadow zone" of chaotic, low-pressure water behind it. Now tilt that plate gradually so that the water can slide smoothly along its surface — the shadow zone shrinks dramatically and the resistance you feel on the plate drops by a huge margin. The hatchback's vertical tailgate is essentially that first "flat plate", which is why it pays a heavy aerodynamic penalty.

A modern hatchback achieves its drag reduction not by magic, but by:
1. Carefully shaping the **DLO (Day Light Opening)** roofline,
2. Adding a **rear spoiler / roof spoiler** to deflect flow,
3. Chamfering the **tailgate edges** to control separation,
4. Smoothening the **underbody** with flat panels and diffusers.

## 1.3 Downforce — The "Sticky Tyre" Force

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Downforce** is the vertical component of the aerodynamic force generated by a vehicle's body, wings, and underbody that acts **downward** (i.e., toward the road), increasing the normal load on the tyres and thereby improving traction, cornering grip, braking, and high-speed stability.

In lift-coefficient notation, downforce corresponds to a **negative lift coefficient** ($C_L < 0$). A Formula 1 car generates up to **3.5 × its own weight** in downforce at top speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive analogy:** Place your flat palm outside a moving car window — you feel air pushing your hand *backward* (drag) and also slightly *upward* (lift). Now angle your hand slightly, like a paper aeroplane wing, and you will feel it pushed *up* much more strongly. Flip your hand upside-down and the air now presses it *down* — that is literally what an inverted wing (or rear wing) does to a car. The harder the car pushes the air up, the harder the air pushes the car down.

## 1.4 The Fundamental Trade-off

> [!WARNING]
> The cardinal rule of vehicle aero-design:
> **More downforce ⇒ Almost always more drag.**
> Designing a vehicle is therefore a constant balancing act between *grip* (downforce) and *efficiency* (low drag).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Hatchback Drag

The total aerodynamic drag of a hatchback ($D_{total}$) has four classical components:

$$D_{total} = D_{pressure} + D_{viscous} + D_{induced} + D_{interference}$$

For a hatchback at typical road speeds (40–120 km/h), the **pressure drag** dominates and accounts for **55–65%** of total drag. This is precisely why the hatchback's rear geometry is so critical.

### 2.1.1 Pressure Drag & the Wake

When the boundary layer on the rear screen cannot negotiate the abrupt turn at the tailgate top, it **separates**. This separation creates a large recirculating wake immediately behind the car where pressure is significantly below atmospheric (i.e., **sub-atmospheric base pressure** $C_{pb}$).

The base-pressure (pressure-drag) coefficient is:

$$C_{D,base} = \dfrac{C_{pb,front} - C_{pb,rear}}{1}$$

For a hatchback:
- $C_{pb,front}$ (front bumper stagnation) ≈ **+0.8 to +0.9**
- $C_{pb,rear}$ (tailgate base) ≈ **−0.30 to −0.45**

This large rear-base suction is the dominant contributor to $C_D$.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Aerodynamic Improvement Levers

1. **Roof spoiler / kick-up lip** — Deflects high-energy flow downward, re-energising the boundary layer so it stays attached further along the tailgate.
2. **Tailgate edge chamfering** — A small ~5° outward lip at the upper trailing edge shifts separation aft and weakens the upper wake vortex.
3. **Side base-cup (boat-tail) curvature** — Narrows the wake laterally, reducing $C_{D,base}$.
4. **Diffuser at underbody exit** — Harnesses the low-pressure underbody region to suck air smoothly out, reducing rear-base depression.
5. **Smooth underbody with flat panels (FBD — Flat-Bottom Design)** — Eliminates underbody turbulence that feeds the wake.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-tested equations** for this topic. Memorise these before attempting any numerical.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,C_{D}$ | $D$ [N], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $V$ [m/s], $A$ [m²] |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Downforce | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,C_{L}$ | $L < 0$ for downforce |
| 3 | Power to overcome drag | $P_{D} = D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{3}\,A\,C_{D}$ | $P$ [W] |
| 4 | Power absorbed by downforce drag | $P_{DF} = D_{DF} \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{3}\,A\,C_{D,induced}$ | W |
| 5 | Drag–Downforce Ratio | $\eta_{L/D} = \dfrac{\vert C_{L}\vert}{C_{D}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Maximum cornering acceleration | $a_{y,max} = \mu \cdot g + \dfrac{L}{m}$ | m/s² |
| 7 | Maximum braking deceleration | $a_{x,max} = \mu \cdot g + \dfrac{L}{m}$ | m/s² |
| 8 | Rolling resistance force | $F_{r} = C_{r} \cdot m \cdot g$ | N, $C_{r}\approx 0.015$ for car tyres |
| 9 | Total resistive force | $F_{tot} = F_{r} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A C_{D}$ | N |
| 10 | Top-speed equilibrium | $V_{max} :\; F_{engine} = F_{r} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A C_{D}$ | m/s |
| 11 | Equivalent $C_D$ for rolling | $C_{D,r} = \dfrac{2 C_{r} m g}{\rho A V^{2}}$ | — |
| 12 | Base-pressure drag coeff. | $C_{D,b} = C_{pb,f} - C_{pb,r}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical symbols** (avoid confusion in the exam):
> - $C_D$ = Drag coefficient (always positive)
> - $C_L$ = Lift coefficient (negative for downforce)
> - $\rho$ = Air density **≈ 1.225 kg/m³** (ISA sea-level)
> - $A$ = **Frontal projected area** in m² (NOT plan area)

## 2.3 Downforce Sources on a Road Vehicle

| Source | Where? | Typical $C_L$ contribution | Effect on $C_D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front wing / splitter | Front | −0.3 to −0.6 | +0.02 to +0.05 |
| Flat underbody | Below | −0.4 to −1.0 | +0.01 to +0.03 (if done wrong) |
| Rear wing | Rear | −0.5 to −1.5 | +0.05 to +0.20 |
| Rear diffuser | Below rear | −0.3 to −0.8 | −0.01 to −0.04 (reduces drag!) |
| Body shape (passive) | Whole car | −0.1 to −0.4 | included in overall $C_D$ |

## 2.4 Effect of Downforce on Vehicle Performance — Real Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In passenger cars (SUVs, sports saloons), downforce improves:
> - **High-speed stability** (resists lift-induced wandering)
> - **Crosswind resistance**
> - **Wet-weather grip** at highway speeds
>
> In race cars (F1, WEC, DTM), downforce enables cornering accelerations of **3–5 g**, allowing cornering speeds unimaginable on road tyres alone.

A well-engineered race car achieves $L/D$ efficiency of around **3:1 to 5:1** (F1 cars ~4:1 in high-downforce trim). Road cars rarely exceed **1:1** because $L/D$ efficiency is sacrificed for low drag and aesthetics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Downforce Improves Maximum Cornering Acceleration

### Given:
A vehicle of mass $m$, weight $\mu m g$ friction limit on each tyre, generating a downforce $L$ at speed $V$.

### To Prove:
$a_{y,max} = \mu g + \dfrac{L}{m}$

### Derivation:

The vertical (normal) reaction on the four tyres is the sum of weight plus downforce:

$$N = m g + L$$

By Coulomb's law of friction, the maximum lateral frictional force available is:

$$F_{lat,max} = \mu N = \mu (m g + L)$$

Newton's second law in the lateral direction gives the maximum lateral acceleration:

$$a_{y,max} = \dfrac{F_{lat,max}}{m} = \dfrac{\mu (m g + L)}{m}$$

$$\boxed{a_{y,max} = \mu g + \dfrac{L}{m}}$$

Now, $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A\,C_L$ with $C_L < 0$. Let $L_{down} = \vert L\vert$, so $L = -L_{down}$:

$$a_{y,max} = \mu g + \dfrac{L_{down}}{m} = \mu g + \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A \vert C_L\vert}{m}$$

This shows that **downforce contribution scales with $V^{2}$** — at low speeds downforce is negligible, but at race speeds it can dominate the grip budget.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

### Problem Statement:
> A hatchback of mass $m = 1200$ kg has frontal area $A = 2.1$ m² and a fitted rear wing that produces $C_L = -0.45$ and adds $C_{D,wing} = 0.08$ to the car's base drag coefficient. The car's base drag coefficient (without wing) is $C_{D,base} = 0.34$. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. The tyre-road friction coefficient $\mu = 0.85$. Calculate, at $V = 60$ m/s (216 km/h):
> (i) Total drag force with and without the wing.
> (ii) Total downforce with the wing.
> (iii) Maximum cornering acceleration at this speed with and without the wing.
> (iv) Percentage loss in top speed if the engine delivers constant power.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Drag without the wing**

$$D_{1} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(60)^{2}(2.1)(0.34)$$

$$D_{1} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 2.1 \times 0.34$$

$$\boxed{D_{1} = 1572.36 \;\text{N}}$$

**Step 2: Drag with the wing**

The wing adds drag, so total $C_D = 0.34 + 0.08 = 0.42$.

$$D_{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(60)^{2}(2.1)(0.42)$$

$$D_{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 2.1 \times 0.42$$

$$\boxed{D_{2} = 1942.62 \;\text{N}}$$

**Step 3: Downforce with the wing**

$$L_{down} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A \vert C_{L}\vert$$

$$L_{down} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 \times 2.1 \times 0.45$$

$$\boxed{L_{down} = 2080.65 \;\text{N}}$$

**Step 4: Maximum cornering acceleration**

Without wing ($L_{down} = 0$):

$$a_{1} = \mu g + 0 = 0.85 \times 9.81 = 8.34 \;\text{m/s}^{2}$$

With wing:

$$a_{2} = 8.34 + \dfrac{2080.65}{1200} = 8.34 + 1.734 = 10.07 \;\text{m/s}^{2}$$

**Percentage gain:**

$$\Delta a\,\% = \dfrac{10.07 - 8.34}{8.34} \times 100 = \boxed{20.7\,\%}$$

**Step 5: Top speed impact (qualitative)**
Since $P = D \cdot V$, for constant engine power the higher drag of the wing will lower $V_{max}$. The student should solve $V_{max}^3 = 2P_{eng}/(\rho A C_{D})$ and compare.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Summary

| Performance Metric | Without Wing | With Wing | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerodynamic drag $D$ | 1572 N | 1943 N | ↑ **WORSE** (higher) |
| Downforce $L_{down}$ | 0 N | 2081 N | ↑ **BETTER** (higher) |
| Cornering $a_{y,max}$ | 8.34 m/s² | 10.07 m/s² | ↑ **BETTER** |
| Braking $a_{x,max}$ | 8.34 m/s² | 10.07 m/s² | ↑ **BETTER** |
| Top speed $V_{max}$ | Higher | Lower | ↓ **WORSE** |
| Fuel economy at cruise | Better | Worse | ↓ **WORSE** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The core engineering insight:** Downforce buys **grip at the cost of top speed and fuel economy**. This is why road cars deliberately minimise downforce, while race cars deliberately maximise it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Field Around a Hatchback (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Clean Upstream Flow] --> nodeB[Front Bumper Stagnation Point]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Boundary Layer Growth Over Bonnet]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Roofline Acceleration]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Rear Screen Boundary Layer]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Separation Point on Tailgate}
    nodeF --> nodeG[Upper Wake Vortex]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Lower Underbody Wake]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Merged Wake Region - Low Cp Base]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Pressure Drag Force on Hatchback]
    nodeK[Roof Spoiler] -. Modulates .-> nodeF
    nodeL[Rear Diffuser] -. Modulates .-> nodeH
    nodeM[Underbody Panels] -. Modulates .-> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Force Balance on a Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vehicle CG] --> nodeB[Weight mg - downward]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Aerodynamic Downforce L - downward]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Aerodynamic Drag D - rearward]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Rolling Resistance Fr - rearward]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Net Normal Load on Tyres]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Maximum Friction Force mu x N]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Lateral Acceleration Capability]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Power Required P = D x V]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Top Speed and Fuel Economy]
```

## 4.3 Downforce Generation Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Downforce Generation] --> nodeB[Body Shape Passive Downforce]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Active Aero Wings]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Underbody Venturi]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Inverted Airfoil Curvature of Bonnet and Roof]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Front Wing Splitter]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Rear Wing Element]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Flat Floor with Expanding Diffuser]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Low Pressure Below Car Sucks Vehicle Down]
```

## 4.4 Trade-off Triangle (KTU Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Downforce L] --> nodeB[Grip - Better Cornering]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Drag - Worse]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Top Speed - Worse]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Fuel Economy - Worse]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Braking Stability - Better]
    nodeB --> nodeG[High Speed Stability - Better]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why does a hatchback generally have a higher drag coefficient than a fastback?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hatchback has a near-vertical or steeply raked tailgate that terminates the roofline abruptly. This geometry forces the boundary layer flowing over the rear screen to **separate prematurely** at the upper tailgate edge, creating a **large low-pressure wake region** behind the car. The resulting large rear-base suction contributes strongly to **pressure drag**, which dominates total drag.

In contrast, a fastback has a smoothly tapering roofline that allows the boundary layer to remain attached further along the rear surface, producing a **smaller wake** and hence lower drag coefficient (typically 0.22–0.26 vs. 0.30–0.40 for a hatchback). **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define downforce. Why is it beneficial at high speeds even though it increases drag?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Downforce** is the vertical downward component of aerodynamic force generated by a moving vehicle's body shape, wings, and underbody. It is represented by a negative lift coefficient ($C_L < 0$).

Although downforce production increases drag (and hence power consumption), it is beneficial at high speeds because:
1. It **adds to the normal load** on the tyres, increasing the maximum available frictional force for cornering and braking by $F = \mu (mg + L)$.
2. It **suppresses lift-induced instability** (wandering, crosswind sensitivity).
3. In race cars it **enables cornering accelerations of 3–5 g**, achieving lap times impossible with mechanical grip alone.

The benefit outweighs the drag penalty in performance-focused applications. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students forget to use **frontal projected area $A$** (not plan area or wetted area) in $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A C_D$.
> 2. Mixing up **$C_L$ sign convention** — downforce means $C_L$ is *negative* but force $L$ is *positive downward*.
> 3. Using **km/h instead of m/s** in the formula $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A C_D$ without conversion — silent 3-mark deduction.
> 4. Writing $P = D \cdot V$ without noting this is **power required to overcome drag only**, not net propulsive power.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`**

A small hatchback has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 1100$ kg
- Frontal area $A = 2.0$ m²
- Base drag coefficient $C_D = 0.36$
- Tyre-road friction coefficient $\mu = 0.9$

The owner proposes to add a rear wing that produces $C_L = -0.50$ and adds $\Delta C_D = 0.10$ to the total drag coefficient. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(a)** Compute the drag force, downforce, and maximum cornering acceleration **at 80 km/h** for the car (i) without the wing and (ii) with the wing. Comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the engine power required to cruise at 80 km/h and 120 km/h, both with and without the wing. Discuss the effect on fuel economy. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Drag, Downforce, and Cornering Acceleration**

Step 1: Convert speed to SI: $V_1 = 80$ km/h $= 80/3.6 = 22.22$ m/s.

Step 2: Drag without wing:

$$D_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(22.22)^2(2.0)(0.36)$$

$$D_1 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 493.83 \times 2.0 \times 0.36 = \boxed{217.7 \;\text{N}}$$

Step 3: Drag with wing: $C_D = 0.36 + 0.10 = 0.46$

$$D_2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 493.83 \times 2.0 \times 0.46 = \boxed{278.2 \;\text{N}}$$

Step 4: Downforce with wing:

$$L_{down} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 493.83 \times 2.0 \times 0.50 = \boxed{302.5 \;\text{N}}$$

Step 5: Cornering accelerations:

Without wing:

$$a_{1} = \mu g = 0.9 \times 9.81 = 8.83 \;\text{m/s}^{2}$$

With wing:

$$a_{2} = 8.83 + \dfrac{302.5}{1100} = 8.83 + 0.275 = \boxed{9.10 \;\text{m/s}^{2}}$$

Step 6: Trade-off comment: The wing increases drag by 27.8% and only improves cornering acceleration by ~3% at 80 km/h. Downforce benefit scales with $V^2$, so the wing is **inefficient at low speeds** but useful at high speeds. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**Part (b) — Power Required and Fuel Economy**

At 80 km/h ($V_1 = 22.22$ m/s):

$$P_{1,no\,wing} = D_1 \cdot V_1 = 217.7 \times 22.22 = \boxed{4838 \;\text{W} = 4.84 \;\text{kW}}$$

$$P_{1,wing} = 278.2 \times 22.22 = \boxed{6182 \;\text{W} = 6.18 \;\text{kW}}$$

At 120 km/h ($V_2 = 33.33$ m/s):

$$D_{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^2 \times 2.0 \times 0.36 = 489.7 \;\text{N}$$

$$D_{4} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^2 \times 2.0 \times 0.46 = 625.7 \;\text{N}$$

$$L_{down,2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^2 \times 2.0 \times 0.50 = 680.1 \;\text{N}$$

$$P_{2,no\,wing} = 489.7 \times 33.33 = \boxed{16.32 \;\text{kW}}$$

$$P_{2,wing} = 625.7 \times 33.33 = \boxed{20.86 \;\text{kW}}$$

**Fuel economy discussion:** Adding the wing **increases power required by 27.8%** at both speeds (constant because $C_D$ ratio is independent of $V$), directly reducing fuel economy. At high speed, the absolute penalty is larger (4.5 kW vs 1.3 kW) because power scales with $V^3$. The wing is therefore **only justifiable** in performance-driving contexts. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **aerodynamic flow over a hatchback**. Identify the regions of flow separation, pressure recovery, and wake formation. Explain why the rear-end geometry of a hatchback is the dominant parameter controlling its drag coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sports hatchback is travelling at $V = 160$ km/h. Its frontal area is $A = 2.2$ m², drag coefficient $C_D = 0.32$, mass $m = 1400$ kg. A rear wing is fitted producing a downforce coefficient $C_L = -0.6$ with an additional drag coefficient of $\Delta C_D = 0.09$. Calculate:
- (i) Total downforce on the car at this speed.
- (ii) Total drag force and the power required to overcome it.
- (iii) The percentage increase in effective normal load on the tyres. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a)

The flow over a hatchback proceeds as follows:
1. Air approaches the front bumper, decelerates and stagnates at the upper grille area ($C_p \approx +0.85$).
2. Flow accelerates over the bonnet curvature and along the A-pillars.
3. Air moves up the windscreen and across the roof.
4. The boundary layer grows thicker along the roof and rear screen.
5. At the **upper trailing edge of the tailgate**, the boundary layer separates due to the adverse pressure gradient of the near-vertical tailgate.
6. A large **separated wake region** forms behind the car, characterised by low (sub-atmospheric) pressure ($C_{pb} \approx -0.35$).
7. A pair of **C-pillar vortices** shed from the rear side windows further energises the wake.

The rear-end geometry controls $C_D$ because **pressure drag = (front stagnation pressure − rear base pressure) × area**. The hatchback's abrupt rear cut-off causes the rear base pressure to be very low (large suction), and this rear suction contributes the largest single fraction of total drag. Any modification (spoiler, edge chamfer, boat-tailing) that delays separation reduces the wake size and directly lowers $C_D$. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b)

Speed conversion: $V = 160/3.6 = 44.44$ m/s

**(i) Total downforce:**

$$L_{down} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A \vert C_L \vert = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (44.44)^2 \times 2.2 \times 0.6$$

$$L_{down} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1975.3 \times 2.2 \times 0.6 = \boxed{1597 \;\text{N}}$$

**(ii) Drag and power:**

Total $C_D = 0.32 + 0.09 = 0.41$

$$D = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (44.44)^2 \times 2.2 \times 0.41 = 1091.5 \;\text{N}$$

$$P = D \cdot V = 1091.5 \times 44.44 = \boxed{48{,}506 \;\text{W} \approx 48.5 \;\text{kW}}$$

**(iii) Percentage increase in normal load:**

$$N_{baseline} = mg = 1400 \times 9.81 = 13{,}734 \;\text{N}$$

$$\Delta N\,\% = \dfrac{L_{down}}{mg} \times 100 = \dfrac{1597}{13734} \times 100 = \boxed{11.6\,\%}$$

**(7 marks complete)**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things you MUST remember before the exam:**

- [ ] **Hatchback drag** = dominant contributor is **pressure drag from the rear wake** (≈ 60% of total).
- [ ] Hatchback $C_D$ typically **0.30–0.40**, higher than notchback/sedan, much higher than fastback.
- [ ] Rear-end design levers: **roof spoiler, tailgate edge chamfer, boat-tail, underbody diffuser**.
- [ ] **Downforce** = aerodynamic force pushing the car toward the road; corresponds to **negative $C_L$**.
- [ ] Master formula: $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_D$ and $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_L$.
- [ ] Downforce benefits **scale with $V^2$** — negligible at low speeds, dominant at race speeds.
- [ ] Downforce **always** increases drag; the trade-off is the central design challenge.
- [ ] Maximum cornering/braking: $a_{max} = \mu g + L_{down}/m$.
- [ ] Power to overcome drag: $P = D \cdot V$ (and hence scales with $V^3$).
- [ ] F1 cars generate **~3.5× their weight** in downforce at top speed.
- [ ] Use **frontal projected area $A$**, not plan area or wetted area.
- [ ] Air density at sea level: $\rho = \mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$; always convert km/h to m/s.
- [ ] Underbody **diffusers** are the rare aero device that adds downforce *and* reduces drag.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vortices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vortices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vortex (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *vortex* is a region within a fluid in which the flow revolves (rotates) about an imaginary straight or curved axis. Mathematically, it is a continuous distribution of **circulation** $\Gamma$ around a central core, and it is characterized kinematically by a non-zero **vorticity** field $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{V}$.

In *Vehicle Aerodynamics*, vortices are the rotating fluid structures that are shed from the body's curved surfaces, edges, appendages, and cavities. They govern a significant portion of the **drag**, **lift**, and **downforce** budgets of road cars, race cars, trucks, and aircraft.

The two foundational families that every KTU student must master are:

| Type | Other Names | Vorticity | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Free Vortex** | Irrotational / Potential vortex | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = 0$ everywhere (except at the axis) | Tangential velocity $v_\theta$ *decreases* with $r$ |
| **Forced Vortex** | Rotational / Solid-body rotation | $\boldsymbol{\omega} \neq 0$ (constant) | Tangential velocity $v_\theta$ *increases* with $r$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Stirring a cup of tea" 🍵**
> - When you **stir** the tea, the *fluid near the spoon* rotates bodily with the spoon ⇒ **Forced Vortex** (solid-body rotation, like a rotating CD).
> - When you **stop stirring**, the *fluid far from the cup center* continues to spin, but faster near the center and slower at the wall ⇒ **Free Vortex** (irrotational swirl).
> - In a real cup, both coexist in a single structure: this is the **Rankine Combined Vortex**.

**Vehicle application intuition:**
- A sports car's **front wing end-plate** sheds a tip vortex.
- A **side-mirror** of a passenger car generates a pair of trailing vortices that dominate the **mid-body drag**.
- A **truck cab** roof generates longitudinal (horse-shoe) vortices at the base of the A-pillar.
- An aircraft **wing-tip** sheds a concentrated trailing vortex — the most dangerous and aerodynamically significant vortex in transportation.

## 1.3 Core Physical Quantities

- **Circulation** $\Gamma$ — measure of the *rotational strength* of a vortex line. **Units: m²/s**.
- **Vorticity** $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ — *local* measure of rotation (per unit area). **Units: 1/s** (or rad/s).
- **Core radius** $a$ — radius at which tangential velocity is maximum in a Rankine vortex. **Units: m**.
- **Tangential velocity** $v_\theta$ — velocity component perpendicular to the radius vector. **Units: m/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU exams:** A free vortex has **zero vorticity** yet possesses **non-zero circulation** — circulation is an *integral* property, vorticity is a *point* property.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Vortex Formation

A vortex in vehicle aerodynamics is born through the following chain:

1. **Boundary-layer separation** occurs at a sharp geometric discontinuity (edge, cavity, wing-tip, A-pillar).
2. The separated shear layer **rolls up** upon itself due to a self-induced velocity field (Kelvin–Helmholtz instability).
3. The rolled-up sheet becomes a **concentrated vortex core** with circulation $\Gamma$ fixed by the Kutta–Joukowski condition at the trailing edge.
4. The vortex convects downstream, exerting induced drag and downwash on the vehicle surfaces it passes over.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vortices matter in vehicle design:** A single wing-tip vortex on a Formula-1 rear wing can account for **40–60 %** of the total downforce but also produces **induced drag**. Designers therefore balance vortex strength against parasitic drag using **end-plates, winglets, and vortex generators**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation that has appeared, or can be expected to appear, in the KTU 2024 ESE for the topic *Vortices*. All symbols follow the **2024 Scheme notation convention**.

### 2.2.1 Kinematic Definitions

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Circulation | $\Gamma = \oint_{C} \mathbf{V} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$ | Line integral of velocity around a closed contour $C$ |
| 2 | Vorticity (vector) | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{V}$ | Curl of velocity field |
| 3 | Vorticity (2-D, z-comp.) | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}$ | Used for planar vortex flows |
| 4 | Stokes' theorem | $\Gamma = \iint_{A} \boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot d\mathbf{A}$ | Links circulation to enclosed vorticity |
| 5 | Stream function (2-D) | $v_x = \partial\psi/\partial y,\ \ v_y = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Continuity automatically satisfied |

### 2.2.2 Free Vortex (Irrotational) — *valid for $r > 0$ outside the core*

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 6 | Tangential velocity | $v_\theta = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2 \pi r}$ | Hyperbolic decay |
| 7 | Constant of motion | $v_\theta \, r = \text{const.} = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi}$ | Often called $C$ |
| 8 | Radial velocity | $v_r = 0$ | Axisymmetric, no inflow |
| 9 | Stream function | $\psi = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\ln r$ | Laplacian of $\psi = 0$ |
| 10 | Velocity potential | $\phi = \dfrac{\Gamma}{2\pi}\theta$ | Exists only because flow is irrotational |
| 11 | Pressure field | $p(r) = p_\infty - \dfrac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{8 \pi^{2} r^{2}}$ | From Bernoulli |
| 12 | Pressure gradient | $\dfrac{dp}{dr} = \dfrac{\rho v_\theta^{2}}{r} = \dfrac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{4 \pi^{2} r^{3}}$ | Radial, points outward |

### 2.2.3 Forced Vortex (Solid-Body Rotation)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 13 | Tangential velocity | $v_\theta = \omega r$ | Linear with $r$ |
| 14 | Radial velocity | $v_r = 0$ | Pure rotation |
| 15 | Vorticity | $\omega_z = 2\,\Omega$ (constant) | $\Omega$ = angular velocity |
| 16 | Pressure field | $p(r) = p_\infty + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho\,\Omega^{2} r^{2}$ | Parabolic rise outward |
| 17 | Pressure gradient | $\dfrac{dp}{dr} = \rho\,\Omega^{2} r$ | Centripetal balance |
| 18 | Stream function | $\psi = -\dfrac{1}{2}\Omega r^{2}$ | Quadratic in $r$ |

### 2.2.4 Rankine Combined Vortex

| # | Region | Tangential velocity | Pressure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 19 | $r \le a$ (core) | $v_\theta = \Omega r$ | $p = p_a + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}(r^{2}-a^{2})$ |
| 20 | $r \ge a$ (outer) | $v_\theta = \Omega a^{2}/r$ | $p = p_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}a^{4}/r^{2}$ |
| 21 | At $r = a$ | $v_{\theta,\max} = \Omega a$ | Maximum velocity |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vortex generators (VGs):** tiny vanes placed on a car bonnet or aircraft wing to *deliberately trip* the boundary layer, energising it and delaying separation. KTU commonly asks the *role* of VGs.
- **End-plates on wings:** suppress span-wise flow and *weaken* the tip vortex, reducing induced drag.
- **Diffusers on race cars:** operate by creating a low-pressure region under the floor using controlled longitudinal vortices; the *strength* of these vortices is what makes the floor "suck".
- **Wake of bluff bodies:** road vehicles shed Kármán-like vortex streets that create aero-acoustic noise — a key NVH concern in EV design.
- **Cross-wind stability:** a sudden gust separates the leeward A-pillar flow, generating a transient vortex that determines the yaw moment of a van or truck.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step below is fully expanded. The notation follows: $r$ = radial coordinate from vortex axis, $\theta$ = azimuthal angle, $v_\theta$ = tangential velocity, $v_r$ = radial velocity, $\rho$ = fluid density, $p$ = static pressure, $p_\infty$ = far-field pressure.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Tangential Velocity of a Free Vortex

**Starting point:** The circulation $\Gamma$ is defined around any closed contour $C$ enclosing the vortex axis:

$$
\Gamma = \oint_{C} \mathbf{V} \cdot d\mathbf{l}
$$

For a *circular* contour of radius $r$ centred on the vortex axis, the velocity is purely tangential, $v_r = 0$, and the line element $d\mathbf{l} = r\,d\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$. Hence:

$$
\Gamma = \oint_{C} v_\theta (r\,d\theta)
$$

Since the flow is axisymmetric, $v_\theta$ is *constant* along the contour and may be taken outside the integral:

$$
\Gamma = v_\theta \int_{0}^{2\pi} r\,d\theta = v_\theta \, r \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta
$$

Evaluating the angular integral:

$$
\Gamma = v_\theta \, r \,(2\pi)
$$

Solving for the tangential velocity:

$$
\boxed{\,v_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\,}
$$

This is the canonical free-vortex result and is the single most-tested equation in the Vortices module.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Pressure Field of a Free Vortex

**Starting point:** Apply the incompressible Bernoulli equation between a point at radius $r$ and a far-field point at $r \to \infty$ where $v_\theta \to 0$ and $p \to p_\infty$:

$$
p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (0)^{2} = p(r) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,v_\theta^{2}(r)
$$

Substitute the expression $v_\theta = \Gamma / (2\pi r)$ obtained above:

$$
p_\infty = p(r) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left(\frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}\right)^{2}
$$

Expand the squared term:

$$
p_\infty = p(r) + \frac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{8\pi^{2} r^{2}}
$$

Isolate the local pressure:

$$
\boxed{\,p(r) = p_\infty - \frac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{8\pi^{2} r^{2}}\,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The pressure at the vortex axis is *lowest* and rises monotonically outward as $1/r^{2}$. The radial pressure gradient that results is exactly the centripetal force required to deflect each fluid element along its circular path:

$$
\frac{dp}{dr} = \frac{\rho v_\theta^{2}}{r} = \frac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{4\pi^{2} r^{3}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation C — Tangential Velocity and Pressure of a Forced Vortex

**Starting point:** Solid-body rotation about the z-axis with constant angular velocity $\Omega$. In cylindrical coordinates the velocity vector is $\mathbf{V} = \Omega r\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$; therefore the tangential speed grows linearly with $r$:

$$
\boxed{\,v_\theta = \Omega r\,}
$$

The vorticity is the curl of $\mathbf{V}$:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}
$$

Substitute $v_\theta = \Omega r$ and $v_r = 0$:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(r \cdot \Omega r) - 0 = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(\Omega r^{2}) = \frac{1}{r}(2\Omega r)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\omega_z = 2\Omega \quad \text{(uniform, non-zero)}\,}
$$

**Pressure derivation:** Apply the radial component of the Euler equation (steady, axisymmetric, no radial velocity):

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{\rho v_\theta^{2}}{r} = \frac{\rho (\Omega r)^{2}}{r} = \rho \Omega^{2} r
$$

Integrate from a generic radius $r$ to the core boundary $a$, where $p = p_a$:

$$
\int_{p_a}^{p(r)} dp = \int_{a}^{r} \rho \Omega^{2} r'\,dr'
$$

$$
p(r) - p_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}(r^{2} - a^{2})
$$

Solving explicitly:

$$
\boxed{\,p(r) = p_a + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}(r^{2} - a^{2})\,}
$$

If we anchor the pressure to the far-field value $p_\infty$ at infinity:

$$
p(r) = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2} r^{2} \quad \text{(for a pure forced vortex, no far-field anchor)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forced-vortex pitfall:** Bernoulli's equation in its standard *incompressible* form **does NOT apply** to a forced vortex, because the flow is rotational. A modified form with the $\int \Omega^{2}r\,dr$ term must be used.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Rankine Combined Vortex at the Core Boundary

**Concept:** Match the inner forced vortex ($v_\theta = \Omega r$) and the outer free vortex ($v_\theta = \Omega a^{2}/r$) at the *core radius* $r = a$ by ensuring continuity of $v_\theta$.

- Inner at $r=a$: $v_\theta^{\text{in}} = \Omega a$
- Outer at $r=a$: $v_\theta^{\text{out}} = \Omega a^{2}/a = \Omega a$ ✓

The *maximum* tangential velocity therefore occurs at $r = a$:

$$
\boxed{\,v_{\theta,\max} = \Omega a\,}
$$

The circulation of a Rankine vortex is computed from the outer field:

$$
\Gamma = 2\pi r \cdot v_\theta = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{\Omega a^{2}}{r} = 2\pi \Omega a^{2}
$$

Hence the angular velocity relates to circulation as:

$$
\boxed{\,\Omega = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi a^{2}}\,}
$$

## 3.5 Derivative Continuity Check (Free → Forced at the Core)

Inside the core ($r \le a$), the pressure from the forced vortex anchored to $p_a$ at $r = a$ is:

$$
p_{\text{in}}(r) = p_a + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}(r^{2} - a^{2})
$$

Outside ($r \ge a$), Bernoulli gives:

$$
p_{\text{out}}(r) = p_\infty - \frac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{8\pi^{2} r^{2}} = p_\infty - \frac{\rho (\Omega a)^{2}}{2 r^{2}}\cdot 2 \quad \text{(after substitution)}
$$

Matching $p_{\text{in}}(a) = p_{\text{out}}(a)$ and using $v_{\theta,\max} = \Omega a$:

$$
p_a = p_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}a^{2}
$$

This anchor ensures the entire Rankine profile is single-valued and is the form KTU examiners expect in long derivations.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Free vs. Forced Vortex Side-by-Side

| Property | Free Vortex (Irrotational) | Forced Vortex (Rotational) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vorticity $\omega_z$ | $0$ | $2\Omega$ (constant) |
| Velocity profile $v_\theta(r)$ | $\Gamma/(2\pi r)$ — *hyperbolic decay* | $\Omega r$ — *linear growth* |
| Pressure profile $p(r)$ | $p_\infty - \rho\Gamma^{2}/(8\pi^{2}r^{2})$ — *rises outward* | $p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\Omega^{2}r^{2}$ — *parabolic rise* |
| Stream function $\psi(r)$ | $(\Gamma/2\pi)\ln r$ | $-\tfrac{1}{2}\Omega r^{2}$ |
| Velocity potential $\phi$ | Exists: $(\Gamma/2\pi)\theta$ | Does **not** exist (rotational) |
| Energy at large $r$ | Tends to zero | Tends to infinity |
| Energy at small $r$ | Tends to infinity | Finite at $r=0$ |
| Bernoulli applicable? | Yes | No (rotational flow) |
| Physical example | Drain of a bathtub, far-field of a wing-tip | Fluid rotating with a stirrer, inside a tornado core |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block diagrams are used to visualise the *functional flow architecture* of vortex formation around a vehicle and the velocity profile shapes that KTU examiners repeatedly test.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Vortex Formation Chain Around a Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Flow: Freestream V_infty] --> B[Vehicle Body Surface]
    B --> C{Sharp Edge or Appendage?}
    C -- No --> D[Attached Flow Region]
    C -- Yes --> E[Boundary-Layer Separation]
    E --> F[Shear Layer Roll-Up]
    F --> G[Concentrated Vortex Core]
    G --> H{Vortex Location}
    H --> H1[Wing-Tip Vortex]
    H --> H2[A-Pillar Vortex]
    H --> H3[Wheel-Wake Vortex]
    H --> H4[Boat-Tail Longitudinal Vortex]
    H1 --> I[Induced Drag / Downforce]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Aerodynamic Performance Outcome]
    D --> J
    J --> K{Vehicle Designers' Choice}
    K -- Suppress --> L[Vortex End-Plates / Fillets]
    K -- Exploit --> M[Vortex Generators / Diffusers]
    L --> N[Optimised Flow Field]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Velocity Profile Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FreeVortex["Free Vortex Zone (r greater than a)"]
        FV1[Tangential velocity v_theta]
        FV2[Profile: 1 over r]
        FV3[Decays hyperbolically]
    end
    subgraph ForcedVortex["Forced Vortex Zone (r less than or equal to a)"]
        F1[Tangential velocity v_theta]
        F2[Profile: omega times r]
        F3[Grows linearly]
    end
    subgraph RankineCore["Rankine Combined Vortex (Matching at r equals a)"]
        R1[Continuity of v_theta at r equals a]
        R2[Maximum v_theta at r equals a]
        R3[Single-valued pressure field]
    end
    FreeVortex --> RankineCore
    ForcedVortex --> RankineCore
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pressure Distribution in a Vortex

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Far-Field Pressure p_infinity] --> P1{Region Check}
    P1 -- Free Vortex r greater than a --> P2[p equals p_infinity minus rho Gamma squared over 8 pi squared r squared]
    P1 -- Forced Vortex r less than or equal to a --> P3[p equals p_a plus 1 over 2 times rho times omega squared times r squared minus a squared]
    P2 --> P4[Low Pressure at Core, Rises as 1 over r squared]
    P3 --> P5[Low Pressure at r equals 0, Rises as r squared]
    P4 --> P6[Centripetal Force Balance Verified]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Engineering Outcome: Downforce or Suction]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Vortex in a Vehicle Diffuser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Freestream Air Approaches Floor] --> CON[Converging-Diverging Channel]
    CON --> V1[Vortex Pair Generated at Front Strakes]
    V1 --> EN[Boundary-Layer Energy Increased]
    EN --> DIFF[Diffuser Expansion]
    DIFF --> LP[Low Static Pressure under Floor]
    LP --> DF[Vehicle Downforce F_z]
    DF --> PERF[Improved Cornering Performance]
    V1 --> ROLL[Rotating Flow: omega greater than zero]
    ROLL --> MIX[Momentum Transfer to Near-Wall Flow]
    MIX --> SEP[Delayed Separation at Trailing Edge]
    SEP --> PERF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagrams:** The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to *qualitatively* link the mathematical structure of a vortex (free, forced, or Rankine) to its **aerodynamic effect** on a vehicle. The diagrams above are the *functional flow architecture* that an examiner will look for in the answer sheet — not a free-hand sketch, but a *cause → effect* chain.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark distribution reminder (KTU 2024 ESE):** Module 3 carries *one full 14-mark question* in Part B and at least *one 3-mark question* in Part A. The questions below mirror this exact pattern with the **internal choice** that the university follows.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a free vortex and a forced vortex. Give one engineering example of each from vehicle aerodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Free Vortex | Forced Vortex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vorticity | Zero (irrotational) | Non-zero (rotational) |
| Velocity law | $v_\theta \propto 1/r$ | $v_\theta \propto r$ |
| Example | Far-field of an aircraft wing-tip vortex | Fluid rotating with a rotating wheel-hub fairing |
| Bernoulli | Applicable | Not applicable |

*(1 mark – definitions, 1 mark – velocity laws, 1 mark – examples.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define circulation and vorticity. How are they related by Stokes' theorem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Circulation** $\Gamma$ is the line integral of velocity around a closed curve: $\Gamma = \oint \mathbf{V} \cdot d\mathbf{l}$. It is a *global* property with units m²/s. *(1 mark)*
- **Vorticity** $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{V}$ is the *local* curl of the velocity field, with units 1/s. *(1 mark)*
- **Stokes' theorem** links the two: $\Gamma = \iint_{A} \boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot d\mathbf{A}$. The circulation around a closed loop equals the flux of vorticity through any surface bounded by that loop. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B for Module 3 always provides an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Starting from the definition of circulation, derive the tangential velocity distribution $v_\theta(r)$ for a **free vortex** and the corresponding **pressure field** $p(r)$. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a **forced vortex** rotating with angular velocity $\Omega = 30\ \text{rad/s}$, evaluate:
   (i) the tangential velocity at $r = 0.4\ \text{m}$;
   (ii) the pressure difference between $r = 0.4\ \text{m}$ and the axis, taking $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define circulation (1 mark):**
$$
\Gamma = \oint_{C} \mathbf{V} \cdot d\mathbf{l}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose a circular contour of radius $r$ centred on the vortex axis (1 mark):** By axisymmetry, $v_r = 0$ and $v_\theta$ is constant on the contour, with $d\mathbf{l} = r\,d\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (2 marks):**
$$
\Gamma = v_\theta \int_{0}^{2\pi} r\,d\theta = 2\pi r\,v_\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_\theta$ (1 mark):**
$$
v_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bernoulli between radius $r$ and infinity (1 mark):**
$$
p_\infty = p(r) + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_\theta^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify (1 mark):**
$$
p(r) = p_\infty - \frac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{8\pi^{2} r^{2}}
$$

**Assumptions (to be stated):** steady, incompressible, inviscid, irrotational (except at axis), axisymmetric flow. *(included in marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\Omega = 30\ \text{rad/s}$, $r = 0.4\ \text{m}$, $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$.

**(i) Tangential velocity (3 marks):**
$$
v_\theta = \Omega r = 30 \times 0.4 = \boxed{12\ \text{m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Pressure difference (4 marks):**
$$
\Delta p = p(r) - p(0) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \Omega^{2} r^{2}
$$

Substitute the values:
$$
\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (30)^{2} \times (0.4)^{2}
$$

$$
\Delta p = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 \times 0.16
$$

$$
\Delta p = \boxed{88.2\ \text{Pa}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Describe the **Rankine combined vortex** with the aid of a velocity profile sketch. Derive the relationships between the angular velocity $\Omega$, the core radius $a$, and the circulation $\Gamma$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A trailing vortex shed from the wing-tip of a race-car rear wing has circulation $\Gamma = 4\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ and core radius $a = 0.05\ \text{m}$. Calculate:
   (i) the maximum tangential velocity in the vortex;
   (ii) the tangential velocity at $r = 0.5\ \text{m}$ from the axis;
   (iii) the pressure drop (relative to far-field) at the core boundary. Take $\rho = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Describe the Rankine vortex (2 marks):**
- Inner core ($r \le a$): solid-body rotation, $v_\theta = \Omega r$, $\omega_z = 2\Omega$ (constant, non-zero).
- Outer region ($r \ge a$): free vortex, $v_\theta = \Omega a^{2}/r$, $\omega_z = 0$ (irrotational).
- Maximum velocity occurs at the core boundary $r = a$.

**Step 2 — Continuity of velocity at $r = a$ (2 marks):**
$$
\Omega a = \frac{\Omega a^{2}}{a} = \Omega a \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Derive $\Gamma = 2\pi \Omega a^{2}$ (2 marks):** Using the outer-field formula:
$$
\Gamma = 2\pi r \cdot v_\theta = 2\pi r \cdot \frac{\Omega a^{2}}{r} = 2\pi \Omega a^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert (1 mark):**
$$
\boxed{\,\Omega = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi a^{2}}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\Gamma = 4\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$, $a = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $\rho = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$.

**(i) Maximum tangential velocity at $r = a$ (2 marks):**
$$
v_{\theta,\max} = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi a} = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 0.05} = \frac{4}{0.1\pi} = \boxed{12.73\ \text{m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Tangential velocity at $r = 0.5\ \text{m}$ (2 marks):** Since $r > a$, use the free-vortex formula:
$$
v_\theta = \frac{\Gamma}{2\pi r} = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 0.5} = \frac{4}{\pi} = \boxed{1.273\ \text{m/s}}
$$

**(iii) Pressure drop at the core boundary $r = a$ (3 marks):** For the free-vortex outer field:
$$
\Delta p = p_\infty - p(a) = \frac{\rho \Gamma^{2}}{8\pi^{2} a^{2}}
$$

Substitute the values:
$$
\Delta p = \frac{1.2 \times (4)^{2}}{8\pi^{2} (0.05)^{2}} = \frac{1.2 \times 16}{8 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0025}
$$

$$
\Delta p = \frac{19.2}{0.19739} = \boxed{97.27\ \text{Pa}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (read carefully!)**
> 1. **Confusing $\Gamma$ (circulation) with $\omega$ (vorticity).** They are related by Stokes' theorem, but they are *not* the same. Marks lost here are *fatal* in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (steady, incompressible, inviscid, irrotational) before applying Bernoulli to a free vortex. Examiners explicitly award marks for this line.
> 3. **Using Bernoulli for a forced vortex.** It is *rotational* — Bernoulli does not apply. Use the radial Euler equation instead.
> 4. **Mixing core and outer regions of a Rankine vortex.** Always check whether the given $r$ is *less than, equal to, or greater than* $a$ before writing $v_\theta$.
> 5. **Unit slips in $\Gamma$.** Circulation is **m²/s**, not m/s. Vorticity is **1/s**, not rad/s in the KTU convention. Keep units explicit.
> 6. **Skipping the matching step** at $r = a$ in Rankine-vortex derivations. The continuity of $v_\theta$ and $p$ at the core is *what defines* the Rankine vortex.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Vortex** = rotating flow region characterised by non-zero circulation.
- [x] **Free vortex**: $v_\theta = \Gamma/(2\pi r)$; **vorticity = 0**; pressure rises as $1/r^{2}$; Bernoulli valid.
- [x] **Forced vortex**: $v_\theta = \Omega r$; **vorticity = $2\Omega$**; pressure rises as $r^{2}$; Bernoulli **not** valid.
- [x] **Rankine combined vortex** = forced inside core, free outside core; max $v_\theta$ at $r = a$.
- [x] **Circulation** $\Gamma$ (m²/s) is *integral*; **vorticity** $\omega$ (1/s) is *local*; linked by **Stokes' theorem**: $\Gamma = \iint \omega\, dA$.
- [x] Stream function for a free vortex: $\psi = (\Gamma/2\pi)\ln r$.
- [x] Stream function for a forced vortex: $\psi = -\tfrac{1}{2}\Omega r^{2}$.
- [x] Velocity potential exists **only for irrotational (free) flow**: $\phi = (\Gamma/2\pi)\theta$.
- [x] In **vehicle aerodynamics**, vortices govern: wing-tip loss, A-pillar separation, base wake, diffuser suction.
- [x] **Vortex generators** *exploit* vortices to delay separation; **end-plates** *suppress* tip vortices to reduce induced drag.
- [x] Standard exam traps: (1) using Bernoulli for a forced vortex; (2) forgetting the $r \le a$ vs. $r \ge a$ split in Rankine; (3) unit confusion between $\Gamma$ and $\omega$.
- [x] Always **state assumptions** before applying Bernoulli: steady, incompressible, inviscid, irrotational, axisymmetric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerodynamic Testing - Wind Tunnels for Automotive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aerodynamic Testing - Wind Tunnels for Automotive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aerodynamic Wind Tunnel (Automotive):** A controlled-environment experimental facility in which a stationary full-scale or scale model of a road vehicle is subjected to a regulated, uniform, and quantifiable airflow to measure aerodynamic forces (drag, lift, side force), moments (pitching, yawing, rolling), surface pressure distributions, and flow-field behavior, under precisely simulated boundary conditions representing the vehicle's interaction with the road and free-stream atmosphere.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (OEAUT723 – Module 4), automotive wind tunnels are classified as **ground-simulating, low-speed, subsonic** facilities operating typically between **Re = 0.5 × 10⁶ to 5 × 10⁶** based on vehicle length, with freestream velocities of **30 m/s to 70 m/s** (≈ 108 km/h to 252 km/h).

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|------|----------------|
| **Test Section** | The working region where the model is placed and the air is conditioned for testing. |
| **Contraction Ratio** | Ratio of the area at the entrance of the contraction to the area at the test section ($CR = A_1/A_2$). |
| **Nozzle** | Converging passage that accelerates flow to the test section. |
| **Diffuser** | Diverging passage that decelerates the flow and recovers static pressure. |
| **Boundary Layer Simulation (BLS)** | Methods to simulate the relative motion between tire and road (moving belt, suction, etc.). |
| **Turbulence Intensity (Tu)** | Ratio of RMS fluctuating velocity to mean velocity, $Tu = u'/U_{\infty} \times 100\%$. |
| **Blockage** | Distortion of the streamlines caused by the model's presence in the test section. |
| **6-Component Balance** | Force-measuring device that simultaneously resolves drag, lift, side force, and three moments. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Indoor Highway for Air"**
> Imagine trying to test how efficiently a fish (your car) cuts through water, but you don't have an ocean. So you build a long swimming pool, install a giant fan at one end, a giant vacuum at the other, and let the water stream past your stationary fish. You attach spring scales to the fish's nose, tail, and sides to measure how hard the water pushes. That's exactly what an automotive wind tunnel is — a giant, scientifically instrumented "air river" where the **car stands still and the air moves**.

The clever bit is that cars don't fly — they roll on a road. So a true wind tunnel must also **fake the road rolling under the car** while keeping the car still. That's why automotive tunnels have a special **moving belt** under the wheels, or boundary layer suction, to mimic the relative road motion.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

- Standard air density at test conditions: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** (ISA Sea Level)
- Kinematic viscosity of air: **ν = 1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**
- Dynamic pressure reference: **q∞ = ½ ρ V²∞** (used to non-dimensionalize all forces)
- Reference area for cars: **frontal projected area A = H × W** (height × width)
- Reference length: **wheelbase L** (most common) or overall length.
- Typical tunnel contraction ratios: **CR ≈ 4 : 1 to 9 : 1** (automotive tunnels prefer 6:1)
- Acceptable turbulence intensity for automotive testing: **Tu < 0.1 %** in modern climatic tunnels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike aircraft wind tunnels, automotive wind tunnels **MUST simulate the ground boundary layer**. Failure to simulate road motion can introduce errors of **up to 20–30 % in measured drag coefficient (C_D)** for sedan-type vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A subsonic, closed-return, automotive wind tunnel operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Intake / Bellmouth Entry** — Atmospheric air is drawn smoothly into the tunnel through a honeycomb-straightener mesh and screens. The bellmouth shape accelerates the air gradually to avoid flow separation.
2. **Settling Chamber / Contraction** — Air is conditioned (laminarized) by **honeycombs** (break up large swirls) and **fine wire-mesh screens** (damp turbulence). The cross-section reduces via a contraction cone, accelerating the flow and increasing uniformity.
3. **Nozzle** — Final acceleration of the flow to the designed test-section velocity, typically with a contraction ratio between **6:1 and 9:1**.
4. **Test Section** — The model (or full vehicle) is placed here. The floor may be a **5-belt system (moving ground plane)** or a stationary floor with **boundary layer suction**. Forces are measured using an underfloor **6-component force balance**.
5. **Diffuser** — Gradual expansion recovers static pressure and reduces flow velocity, reducing the fan's power requirement.
6. **Corner Turning Vanes** (in closed-return tunnels) — Redirect the flow smoothly through 180° to return it to the fan.
7. **Drive Fan / Fan Section** — High-power, variable-pitch axial fan (typically **1.5 MW to 4 MW** in modern automotive tunnels) drives the closed loop.
8. **Return Leg / Exit Silencer** — Returns conditioned air back to the inlet.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas are categorized for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) quick reference.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_D = \dfrac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} A}$ | Drag coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_L = \dfrac{F_L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} A}$ | Lift coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_M = \dfrac{M}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} A L}$ | Moment coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_P = \dfrac{p - p_{\infty}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}$ | Pressure coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Re_L = \dfrac{V_{\infty} L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number based on length | dimensionless |
| 6 | $q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2}$ | Dynamic pressure | Pa |
| 7 | $Tu = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{u'^{2}}}}{U_{\infty}} \times 100\%$ | Turbulence intensity | % |
| 8 | $CR = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Contraction ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta p_{screen} = K \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$ | Pressure loss across screen | Pa |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{block} = \dfrac{\text{Blocked Area}}{A_{ts}} \times 100\%$ | Blockage ratio | % |
| 11 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{\Delta p_{total} \cdot Q}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Fan power | W |
| 12 | $Q = V_{ts} \cdot A_{ts}$ | Volume flow rate | m³/s |

### 2.3 Why the Contraction Ratio Matters

The contraction ratio determines the **quality** of the test-section flow. A higher CR produces:
- Higher and more uniform **velocity** at the test section.
- Lower **turbulence intensity** Tu.
- Better **flow angularity** (less swirl and divergence).

By the **1-D continuity and Bernoulli** analysis, the velocity gain in a contraction is:
$$ \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1 - \Gamma^2 (1 - (A_1/A_2)^2)}} $$

where $\Gamma$ is the screen pressure-loss coefficient. For typical values of $A_1/A_2 = 6$, velocity ratios of **2.4 to 2.7** are commonly achieved.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Passenger Cars** | C_D reduction, fuel economy (every 0.01 in C_D ≈ 0.5 % MPG gain on highway). |
| **Formula-1 & Motorsport** | Front/rear wing tuning, downforce optimization, wake management. |
| **Trucks & Heavy Vehicles** | C_D reduction via trailer skirts, boat-tails, gap fairings (mandatory in EU). |
| **EV (Electric Vehicles)** | Range extension (1 unit of C_D ≈ 5–8 km extra range). |
| **Hyperloop / High-Speed Rail** | Slipstream and pressure-wave studies. |
| **Cycles & Bicycles** | Rider posture and TT helmet optimization. |

## 2.5 Special Considerations for Automotive Wind Tunnels

> [!NOTE]
> **Why automotive tunnels differ from aircraft tunnels:**
>
> 1. **Ground simulation is mandatory** (aircraft tunnels do not need this).
> 2. **Yaw angle mechanism** — vehicles experience crosswinds; tunnels have **rotating turntables** to vary yaw from **-20° to +20°** in 1° increments.
> 3. **Larger test sections** (full-scale testing): cross-sections of **10 m × 5 m to 25 m × 10 m**.
> 4. **Higher blockage tolerance** — solid blockage correction formulae are applied.
> 5. **Boundary layer control** — special floor management to avoid upstream boundary layer contamination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drag Coefficient from Wind Tunnel Data

**Given:** A passenger car model is tested in a wind tunnel. The following readings are taken:
- Free-stream velocity in test section: $V_{\infty} = 40$ m/s
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Measured drag force: $F_D = 280$ N
- Frontal area: $A = 2.2$ m²

**Find:** Drag coefficient $C_D$.

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure:**

The dynamic pressure in the freestream is the kinetic energy per unit volume of the moving air:
$$ q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{\infty}^{2} $$

Substituting the given values:
$$ q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (40)^{2} $$

$$ q_{\infty} = 0.6125 \times 1600 $$

$$ q_{\infty} = 980 \; \text{Pa} $$

**Step 2 — Express the drag force non-dimensionally:**

The drag force is given by:
$$ F_D = C_D \cdot q_{\infty} \cdot A $$

Rearranging for $C_D$:
$$ C_D = \dfrac{F_D}{q_{\infty} A} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$ C_D = \dfrac{280}{980 \times 2.2} $$

$$ C_D = \dfrac{280}{2156} $$

$$ C_D \approx 0.130 $$

**Result:** The drag coefficient is **C_D ≈ 0.13**, which corresponds to an exceptionally well-streamlined modern sedan (e.g., Tesla Model S, Mercedes EQS, or Toyota Prius in this range).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Contraction Ratio and Velocity Gain (with Screens)
