A wind tunnel has a settling chamber cross-section of $A_1 = 30$ m² and a test-section cross-section of $A_2 = 5$ m². Two screens with loss coefficient $\Gamma = 0.3$ are installed. The settling-chamber velocity is $V_1 = 12$ m/s.

**Find:** The test-section velocity $V_2$.

**Step 1 — Recognize the isentropic + screen loss model:**

For a contraction with screens, the relationship between inlet and outlet velocities is:
$$ \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{V_1^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \Gamma^{2} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{-2}\right]} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the area ratio term:**
$$ \left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{30}{5}\right)^{2} = (6)^{2} = 36 $$

$$ \left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{-2} = \dfrac{1}{36} \approx 0.02778 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket term:**
$$ 1 - 0.02778 = 0.97222 $$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $\Gamma^2$:**
$$ \Gamma^{2} = (0.3)^{2} = 0.09 $$
$$ 0.09 \times 0.97222 = 0.0875 $$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**
$$ 1 - 0.0875 = 0.9125 $$

**Step 6 — Compute velocity ratio:**
$$ \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{V_1^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{0.9125} \approx 1.0959 $$

$$ \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \sqrt{1.0959} \approx 1.0468 $$

**Step 7 — Compute $V_2$:**
$$ V_2 = 1.0468 \times 12 \approx 12.56 \; \text{m/s} $$

**Interpretation:** Notice that the screens cause a **velocity loss** compared to an ideal contraction. Without screens, the ideal velocity gain would have been 6× (square root of 36). With screens and finite area ratio, we only get a ~4.7% gain. This demonstrates the trade-off in screen design — more screens = lower Tu but higher $\Delta p$ loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reynolds Number Scaling for Model Testing

**Given:** A 1:5 scale model of a car (full-scale length $L_{full} = 4.5$ m) is tested in a wind tunnel at $V_{model} = 50$ m/s. Test temperature = 25 °C, $\nu = 1.55 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Find:** Reynolds number of model and corresponding full-scale test speed to match Re.

**Step 1 — Model length:**
$$ L_{model} = \dfrac{4.5}{5} = 0.9 \; \text{m} $$

**Step 2 — Model Reynolds number:**
$$ Re_{model} = \dfrac{V_{model} \cdot L_{model}}{\nu} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.9}{1.55 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ Re_{model} = \dfrac{45}{1.55 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2.903 \times 10^{6} $$

**Step 3 — To match Re for full scale, the test speed must satisfy:**
$$ V_{full} = \dfrac{Re_{model} \cdot \nu}{L_{full}} = \dfrac{2.903 \times 10^{6} \times 1.55 \times 10^{-5}}{4.5} $$

$$ V_{full} = \dfrac{45.0}{4.5} = 10 \; \text{m/s} $$

**Result:** To match the Reynolds number of the model, the full-scale test must be conducted at **10 m/s (36 km/h)**. This is a fundamental concept in KTU exams — **Re matching is essential** for valid drag correlation between model and prototype.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the model is too small and the test fan cannot reach high speeds, **pressurized tunnels** or **cryogenic tunnels** are used to artificially raise $\rho$ and lower $\nu$ so that higher Re is achieved at moderate speeds.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Blockage Correction (Solid Blockage — Maskell's Method)

When a model occupies part of the test section, streamlines are deflected, creating an "apparent" increase in dynamic pressure.

**Given:** A car model occupies 8 % of the test-section frontal area. Test-section velocity $V_{\infty} = 40$ m/s, area $A_{ts} = 20$ m².

**Step 1 — Compute effective velocity (Maskell, 1963):**
$$ V_{eff} = V_{\infty} \left(1 + \varepsilon \dfrac{A_{model}}{A_{ts}}\right) $$

For a bluff body like a car, $\varepsilon \approx 1.5$:
$$ V_{eff} = 40 \left(1 + 1.5 \times 0.08\right) = 40 \times 1.12 = 44.8 \; \text{m/s} $$

**Step 2 — Corrected drag coefficient:**
$$ C_{D,corrected} = \dfrac{F_D^{measured}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{eff}^{2} A} $$

$$ C_{D,corrected} = C_{D,measured} \times \left(\dfrac{40}{44.8}\right)^{2} $$

$$ C_{D,corrected} = C_{D,measured} \times 0.798 $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Always apply blockage correction when $\varepsilon_{block} > 5\%$.** KTU examiners explicitly test this concept.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs Closed Circuit Tunnels

| Parameter | Open-Circuit (Eiffel Type) | Closed-Circuit (Return Type) |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| Flow loop | Single pass | Continuous loop |
| Power consumption | Lower (no return losses) | Higher (corner vane losses) |
| Flow quality (Tu) | Moderate | Excellent |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Climate control | Difficult | Easy (full enclosure) |
| Example | Full-scale MIRA (UK) tunnel type | Volkswagen, Porsche climatic tunnels |
| Best for | Production cars (cost) | R&D, motorsport, EV range studies |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Layout of a Closed-Return Automotive Wind Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Intake Bellmouth] --> B[Honeycomb Flow Straightener]
    B --> C[Settling Chamber]
    C --> D[Wire Mesh Screens]
    D --> E[Contraction Cone]
    E --> F[Nozzle Exit]
    F --> G[Test Section With Vehicle and Moving Floor Belt]
    G --> H[6 Component Force Balance Under Vehicle]
    G --> I[Pressure Tap Manifold Array]
    G --> J[Flow Visualization Tuft Probes and Smoke Rig]
    H --> K[Data Acquisition System]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Diffuser Section]
    L --> M[Corner Turning Vanes 180 deg]
    M --> N[Fan Section Variable Pitch Axial]
    N --> O[Silencer and Heat Exchanger Optional]
    O --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Diagram Reading:** In a KTU ESE question, students are often asked to label the components of a wind tunnel schematic. Memorize the **flow path** in the order: intake → settling chamber → screens → contraction → nozzle → test section → diffuser → corner vanes → fan → back to intake.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Ground Simulation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GS[Ground Simulation Methods] --> M1[Moving Belt System 5 Belt Roadway]
    GS --> M2[Boundary Layer Suction Through Perforated Floor]
    GS --> M3[Fixed Floor with Tangential Air Blowing From Nozzle Skirts]
    GS --> M4[Single Wide Tangential Belt with Suction Slot]
    M1 --> S1[Best fidelity but mechanically complex]
    M2 --> S2[Simpler and widely used in research tunnels]
    M3 --> S3[Cheap and used in older production tunnels]
    M4 --> S4[Hybrid approach used in modern climatic tunnels]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Data Acquisition & Instrumentation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Vehicle Model] --> A2[6 Component Balance Load Cells]
    A1 --> A3[Surface Pressure Taps]
    A1 --> A4[Tuft Probes and PIV Cameras]
    A2 --> A5[Amplifier and Signal Conditioner]
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Analog to Digital Converter]
    A6 --> A7[PC Based Data Acquisition System]
    A7 --> A8[Real Time C_D C_L C_M Computation]
    A7 --> A9[Pressure Contour Map Generator]
    A7 --> A10[Wake Velocity Field Mapper]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Force Balance Coordinate System for Automotive Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[V freestream Direction X] --> FD[Drag Force F_D along X]
    V --> FL[Lift Force F_L along Z upward]
    V --> FS[Side Force F_S along Y]
    FD --> M_P[Pitch Moment M about Y axis]
    FL --> M_R[Roll Moment about X axis]
    FS --> M_Y[Yaw Moment M about Z axis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In automotive conventions, **X** = streamwise, **Y** = lateral, **Z** = vertical (upward). This is **opposite** to aeronautical convention (Z is downward). Examiners often test this.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "turbulence intensity" in the context of an automotive wind tunnel. State the acceptable value for modern automotive testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Turbulence intensity $Tu$ is the ratio of the root-mean-square (RMS) of the fluctuating velocity component to the mean freestream velocity, expressed as a percentage:
$$ Tu = \dfrac{\sqrt{\overline{u'^{2}}}}{U_{\infty}} \times 100\% $$
- **Acceptable value (1 mark):** For modern automotive wind tunnels, $Tu < 0.1\%$ is desired, and $Tu < 0.5\%$ is generally considered acceptable for high-quality testing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the function of a "contraction cone" in a wind tunnel? Why is a contraction ratio of 6:1 preferred for automotive testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Function (1.5 marks):** The contraction cone accelerates the flow uniformly from the settling chamber to the test section, **reducing turbulence intensity** and producing a **uniform, low-swirl, low-divergence flow**.
- **Why 6:1 (1.5 marks):** A CR of 6:1 provides an optimal balance — it produces sufficient velocity gain and flow uniformity while keeping the contraction length, fabrication cost, and inlet losses manageable. Higher CR (e.g., 10:1) offers marginal improvement at significant cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam – Module 4]

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a closed-return automotive wind tunnel. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A 1:4 scale model of a sedan (full length 4.4 m) is tested at 60 m/s in a wind tunnel with air at standard sea-level conditions. Compute (i) the model Reynolds number, (ii) the equivalent full-scale test speed to match Re, and (iii) the dynamic pressure at the model test section. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Major Components of a Closed-Return Automotive Wind Tunnel (7 marks)**

The major components, in the order of flow path, are:

1. **Intake / Bellmouth (1 mark):** Smoothly draws atmospheric air; designed to avoid flow separation at the entry.
2. **Honeycomb Flow Straightener (0.5 mark):** Breaks up large-scale swirling vortices in the incoming flow.
3. **Settling Chamber with Screens (1 mark):** Houses fine wire-mesh screens (typically 2–4) that dampen turbulence and produce uniform flow.
4. **Contraction Cone (Nozzle) (1 mark):** Accelerates the flow uniformly to test-section velocity. CR typically 6:1.
5. **Test Section (1.5 marks):** Houses the vehicle model and the **6-component force balance**, **pressure measurement systems**, **moving ground plane (5-belt system)**, and **flow visualization** equipment. A **rotating turntable** enables yaw-angle testing.
6. **Diffuser (0.5 mark):** Gradually expands the flow, recovering static pressure and reducing fan power demand.
7. **Corner Turning Vanes (0.5 mark):** Direct flow smoothly through 180° (in closed-circuit tunnels).
8. **Drive Fan (1 mark):** High-power (1.5–4 MW) variable-pitch axial fan that drives the closed loop.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L_{full} = 4.4$ m, scale = 1:4, $V_{model} = 60$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Model length:**
$$ L_{model} = \dfrac{4.4}{4} = 1.1 \; \text{m} $$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Model Reynolds number:**
$$ Re_{model} = \dfrac{V_{model} \cdot L_{model}}{\nu} = \dfrac{60 \times 1.1}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ Re_{model} = \dfrac{66}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.521 \times 10^{6} $$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Full-scale test speed for Re matching:**
$$ V_{full} = \dfrac{Re_{model} \cdot \nu}{L_{full}} = \dfrac{4.521 \times 10^{6} \times 1.46 \times 10^{-5}}{4.4} $$

$$ V_{full} = \dfrac{66.0}{4.4} = 15.0 \; \text{m/s} \; (54 \; \text{km/h}) $$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Dynamic pressure at test section:**
$$ q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{model}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (60)^{2} $$

$$ q_{\infty} = 0.6125 \times 3600 = 2205 \; \text{Pa} $$

**Results:**
- $Re_{model} \approx 4.52 \times 10^6$
- $V_{full} = 15$ m/s
- $q_{\infty} = 2205$ Pa

---

### Question B (Option 2) [KTU University Exam – Module 4]

**Q (a)** Explain with sketches the **moving-belt ground simulation system** used in modern automotive wind tunnels. Why is ground simulation critical? **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A wind tunnel test yields the following readings for a hatchback model: $V = 35$ m/s, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $F_D = 195$ N, $A = 1.95$ m². If the model occupies 7 % of the test section area, compute (i) the uncorrected $C_D$, and (ii) the blockage-corrected $C_D$ using Maskell's method. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Moving-Belt Ground Simulation (7 marks)**

**Description (4 marks):** The moving-belt (or "5-belt") system consists of **five separate endless belts** that move at the freestream velocity $V_{\infty}$:

- One wide belt under the **wheels and vehicle centerline**.
- Two narrow belts under the **front and rear wheel tracks**.
- Two outer belts in the **wake region** to simulate the road moving under the wake.

The belts are powered by a synchronized electric drive that matches their speed to the freestream airflow. The floor of the test section is recessed so the belts lie flush with the surrounding stationary floor.

**Why critical (3 marks):**
- A real vehicle's tires and underbody are exposed to a **moving road** at the same speed as the freestream.
- Without belt motion, a stationary floor creates a **thick artificial boundary layer** that:
  - **Adds an unrealistic lift force** (acts like a ground effect in reverse).
  - **Distorts wake structure** and increases $C_D$ for sedan-type vehicles by 10–30 %.
  - **Misrepresents underbody and wheel flow** — important for EVs where underbody flow affects battery cooling.

---

**Part (b) — Blockage-Corrected C_D (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Uncorrected C_D:**

Dynamic pressure:
$$ q_{\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times (35)^{2} = 0.6 \times 1225 = 735 \; \text{Pa} $$

Uncorrected drag coefficient:
$$ C_{D,uncorr} = \dfrac{F_D}{q_{\infty} A} = \dfrac{195}{735 \times 1.95} = \dfrac{195}{1433.25} \approx 0.136 $$

**Step 2 (2.5 marks) — Effective velocity (Maskell):**
$$ V_{eff} = V_{\infty} \left(1 + \varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_{block}\right) $$

For a bluff automotive body, $\varepsilon \approx 1.5$:
$$ V_{eff} = 35 \times (1 + 1.5 \times 0.07) = 35 \times 1.105 = 38.675 \; \text{m/s} $$

**Step 3 (2.5 marks) — Corrected C_D:**
$$ C_{D,corr} = C_{D,uncorr} \times \left(\dfrac{V_{\infty}}{V_{eff}}\right)^{2} = 0.136 \times \left(\dfrac{35}{38.675}\right)^{2} $$

$$ C_{D,corr} = 0.136 \times (0.9049)^{2} = 0.136 \times 0.8189 \approx 0.111 $$

**Results:**
- $C_{D,uncorr} \approx 0.136$
- $C_{D,corr} \approx 0.111$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the ½ in dynamic pressure** (students often write $q = \rho V^2$ — loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Using wrong sign convention** (aerospace Z-down vs automotive Z-up) — examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping blockage correction** when blockage > 5 % — **2-mark penalty** in numerical questions.
> 4. **Confusing "contraction ratio" definition** — CR is the ratio of inlet to test-section area, not the inverse.
> 5. **Not mentioning that 5-belt system** is the gold standard; some students describe only the 1-belt system (partial credit only).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Automotive Wind Tunnels**

- **Definition:** An automotive wind tunnel is a low-speed, subsonic facility that uses a moving ground plane to simulate a road vehicle in motion.
- **Flow path:** Intake → Honeycomb → Screens → Contraction → Nozzle → Test Section → Diffuser → Corner Vanes → Fan → (loop).
- **Standard freestream velocity:** 30–70 m/s.
- **Standard Reynolds number (auto):** 0.5 × 10⁶ to 5 × 10⁶.
- **Turbulence intensity target:** Tu < 0.1 %.
- **Contraction ratio (preferred):** 6:1 for automotive tunnels.
- **Reference area:** Frontal projected area $A = H \times W$.
- **Reference length:** Wheelbase or overall length.
- **Coordinate convention (AUTO):** X-streamwise, Y-lateral, Z-upward.
- **Ground simulation methods:** (i) 5-belt, (ii) Boundary layer suction, (iii) Tangential blowing, (iv) Hybrid.
- **Blockage tolerance:** < 5 % is ideal; corrections are mandatory beyond this.
- **6-Component balance:** Measures $F_D$, $F_L$, $F_S$, and $M_{pitch}$, $M_{roll}$, $M_{yaw}$.
- **Blockage correction (Maskell):** $V_{eff} = V_{\infty}(1 + \varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_{block})$; $C_{D,corr} = C_{D,uncorr}(V_{\infty}/V_{eff})^2$.
- **Re matching:** Essential for scaling — model and full scale must operate at same Reynolds number.
- **Tunnels in India:** NATRAX (Indore), ARAI (Pune) — most are subsonic, full-scale, open/closed circuit.
- **Global references:** Volkswagen (Wolfsburg), Porsche (Weissach), BMW (Munich), Pininfarina (Turin), MIRA (UK), Honda (Tochigi).
- **Key derivations to memorize:** $C_D$ from $F_D$, $V_{eff}$ for blockage, $Re_L$ for scale matching.
- **Examiner trap:** Always remember the **2 in denominator** of dynamic pressure $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — losing this is the most common mistake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wind Tunnel** is a closed or open-ended enclosure (duct) through which a controlled stream of air is forced (or drawn) past a stationary test object, enabling the experimental measurement of aerodynamic forces, pressures, and flow phenomena acting on a vehicle or its scaled model under simulated real-world motion conditions. The facility essentially **reverses the physical scenario** — instead of moving a vehicle through still air, a stationary vehicle (or model) is subjected to a moving airstream that replicates the relative velocity, turbulence, and boundary conditions of actual operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — OEAUT723 / Module 4**
> Wind tunnel technology forms the **experimental backbone** of vehicle aerodynamics. Every drag coefficient ($C_D$), lift coefficient ($C_L$), and yaw-angle derivative reported in automotive literature is obtained from wind tunnel testing. KTU expects students to internalize the *principles, classifications, component functions, and similarity laws* that govern such tests.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Big Industrial Fan Room"

Imagine placing a small toy car on a flat table and switching on a powerful household fan. You can feel the air pushing the car backward, and if you attach a small spring scale, you can even measure the drag. This is the **essence** of a wind tunnel — a *giant, scientifically controlled version of that fan*, but engineered so that:
- The air is **uniform** (same velocity everywhere across the test region),
- The flow is **steady** (does not fluctuate with time),
- The air is **smooth** (low turbulence),
- The **temperature, humidity, and pressure** are measurable and adjustable,
- The **forces and pressures** on the test object are captured using precision instruments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a wind tunnel, the **air moves** and the **vehicle stands still**. On a real road, the **vehicle moves** through still air. The two situations are aerodynamically **identical** (Galilean relativity) as long as the relative velocity, density, and body geometry are preserved.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Recall for KTU)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of air (sea level, ISA) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity of air | $\mu$ | **1.789 × 10⁻⁵ N·s/m²** |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | **1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** |
| Speed of sound (15 °C) | $a$ | **340.3 m/s** |
| Specific heat ratio (air) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** |
| Gas constant (air) | $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $p_{\text{atm}}$ | **101 325 Pa** |

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Wind Tunnels

Tunnels are classified by **Mach number range**, **circuit type**, and **drive mechanism**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with all of these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity / Mach Number Classification**
> - **Subsonic:** $M < 0.3$ (low-speed, automotive primary range)
> - **Transonic:** $0.3 \le M \le 1.2$ (flow with mixed subsonic/supersonic regions)
> - **Supersonic:** $1.2 < M \le 5$
> - **Hypersonic:** $M > 5$

> [!NOTE]
> **Circuit / Layout Classification**
> - **Open-circuit (Eiffel / NPL type):** Air is drawn in once and expelled.
> - **Closed-circuit (return / Göttingen type):** Air recirculates in a closed loop.
> - **Open test section vs. Closed (ducted) test section:** A tunnel may be open-circuit yet have a closed test section, etc.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Generic Subsonic Wind Tunnel

The functional sequence inside any wind-tunnel circuit is captured in five logical stages:

1. **Drive / Prime Mover Stage** — A large electric motor coupled to a fan (axial or centrifugal) or a compressor generates the pressure difference. Power input determines achievable flow energy.
2. **Settling / Conditioning Stage** — The air passes through **honeycombs** (to straighten flow, remove swirl) and **fine wire-mesh screens** (to reduce turbulence intensity, typically from ~5 % down to <0.1 %).
3. **Contraction Stage** — A precisely machined **contraction cone** (area ratio typically 6:1 to 9:1) accelerates the flow, further reducing turbulence and producing a near-uniform velocity profile at the test-section inlet.
4. **Test Section** — The model is mounted on a **force balance** or **pressure tap array**. Cross-section is chosen to minimize wall interference and blockage.
5. **Diffuser Stage** — A diverging duct (typically 4° to 7° half-angle) **decelerates** the flow and **recovers static pressure**, reducing the power required by the fan.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Why a contraction cone?** It converts static pressure into dynamic pressure and damps turbulent eddies — flow uniformity improves by an order of magnitude.
- **Why a diffuser?** A slower flow at the fan means a smaller pressure rise is needed for the fan, hence **less power consumption** (recovers up to 80 % of dynamic pressure).
- **Why honeycombs + screens?** Honeycombs break up large-scale swirl; screens attenuate small-scale turbulence through viscous shear.
- **Why a balance inside the test section?** A 6-component force balance resolves drag, lift, side force, and the three moments simultaneously.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover ~85 % of KTU numerical and theory questions on this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dynamic pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}$ | Pa |
| 2 | Speed of sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma\,R\,T}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Mach number | $M = V / a$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Continuity (steady, 1-D) | $\rho_{1} A_{1} V_{1} = \rho_{2} A_{2} V_{2}$ | kg/s |
| 6 | Bernoulli (incompressible) | $p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \text{const}$ | Pa |
| 7 | Drag force | $F_D = C_D \cdot q \cdot A$ | N |
| 8 | Lift force | $F_L = C_L \cdot q \cdot A$ | N |
| 9 | Fan power (ideal) | $P = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3} C_D$ | W |
| 10 | Solid blockage ratio | $\varepsilon_{sb} = \dfrac{K_{b}\,V_{m}}{A_{ts}^{3/2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Wake blockage | $\varepsilon_{w} = \dfrac{C_{D}\,A_{m}}{4\,A_{ts}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Total blockage | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{sb} + \varepsilon_{w}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Blockage-corrected $C_D$ | $C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,m} / (1 - \varepsilon)^{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Reynolds similarity | $Re_{m} = Re_{p}$ → $V_{m}\,L_{m} = V_{p}\,L_{p}$ (same fluid) | constraint |
| 15 | Froude scaling (full-scale from model) | $F_{p} = F_{m}\,(L_{p}/L_{m})^{2}\,(\rho_{p}/\rho_{m})\,(V_{p}/V_{m})^{2}$ | N |
| 16 | Contraction pressure loss | $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{2}^{2}\,(1 - A_{1}^{2}/A_{2}^{2})$ | Pa |
| 17 | Diffuser pressure recovery | $C_{p,\text{rec}} = 1 - (A_{1}/A_{2})^{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 18 | Turbulence intensity | $Tu = \sqrt{\overline{u^{\prime 2}}} / U$ | dimensionless |

> **Note on notation in tables:** All absolute values and unit dividers above are intentionally written with `/` rather than the vertical pipe to preserve the markdown table structure. Where the absolute value is needed inline within prose, use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** (e.g., Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Tata Motors) operate full-scale and 1:1 moving-belt automotive wind tunnels to certify $C_D$ values for road homologation.
- **Formula-1 teams** use 60 %-scale models in subsonic tunnels for aero-package development.
- **High-speed trains and aircraft** use transonic/supersonic tunnels for shock-wave management.
- **Civil structures** (bridges, tall buildings) use boundary-layer tunnels for wind-load estimation.

### 2.5 Open vs. Closed Test Section — Key Trade-off

| Feature | Open Test Section | Closed (Ducted) Test Section |
|---|---|---|
| Flow guidance | Free jet, no walls | Confined by walls |
| Wall interference | Negligible | Must apply corrections |
| Model access | Easy | Restricted |
| Blockage effect | Reduced (jet spreads) | Increased |
| Used in | Automotive, low-speed aero | Most subsonic tunnels |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Required by a Wind Tunnel

**Starting idea:** The fan must supply enough mechanical power to overcome the **aerodynamic drag** of the test model *and* the **internal friction losses** of the duct. We derive the *ideal* (loss-free) power first, then comment on real losses.

**Step 1 — Aerodynamic force on the model.**
The drag force on the model is the standard definition:

$$
F_D = C_D \cdot q \cdot A = C_D \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\right) \cdot A
$$

where $A$ is a characteristic frontal area of the model, $V$ is the free-stream velocity in the test section, and $C_D$ is the drag coefficient.

**Step 2 — Power needed to "push" air past the model.**
Power equals force times velocity (rate of doing work). Since the model is stationary and the air moves at velocity $V$:

$$
P_{\text{aero}} = F_D \cdot V = C_D \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}\right) \cdot A \cdot V
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,C_D\,V^{3}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Total fan power (including duct losses).**
In practice, the fan also has to push the entire air mass through the circuit against wall friction, screen pressure drops, and contraction losses. A typical overall efficiency $\eta$ is introduced:

$$
P_{\text{fan}} = \frac{P_{\text{aero}}}{\eta_{\text{fan}}\,\eta_{\text{diffuser}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check (typical automotive).**
Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $A = 2$ m², $C_D = 0.3$, $V = 45$ m/s (~162 km/h):

$$
P_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2 \times 0.3 \times (45)^{3}
$$

Let us expand $45^{3} = 45 \times 45 \times 45 = 2025 \times 45 = 91\,125$:

$$
P_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2 \times 0.3 \times 91\,125
$$

$$
P_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.6 \times 91\,125 = 0.3675 \times 91\,125
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{aero}} \approx 33\,488 \text{ W} \approx 33.5 \text{ kW}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $V^{3}$ matters:** Doubling the test-section speed increases the power demand by **8×**. This is why high-speed tunnels consume megawatt-class electrical power and require massive drive systems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Blockage Correction

**Physical idea:** A model sitting in a closed test section *occupies volume* and *displaces air*. The displaced air must accelerate around the model (Venturi-like effect), so the *effective* dynamic pressure on the model is higher than the free-stream value measured upstream.

**Step 1 — Define solid blockage ratio.**

$$
\varepsilon_{sb} = \frac{K_{b}\,V_{m}}{A_{ts}^{3/2}}
$$

where $V_{m}$ is the model's volume, $A_{ts}$ is the test-section cross-section, and $K_{b}$ is a shape factor (for a sphere $K_{b} \approx 0.405$).

**Step 2 — Define wake blockage (due to velocity deficit in the wake).**

$$
\varepsilon_{w} = \frac{C_{D}\,A_{m}}{4\,A_{ts}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total blockage.**

$$
\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{sb} + \varepsilon_{w}
$$

**Step 4 — Corrected drag coefficient.**
Empirically, the *effective* velocity is $V_{\text{eff}} = V_{ts} / (1 - \varepsilon)$. Substituting into $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A C_D$ and equating with the measured force gives:

$$
C_{D,\text{meas}} = C_{D,\infty}\,(1 - \varepsilon)^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{D,\infty} = \dfrac{C_{D,\text{meas}}}{(1 - \varepsilon)^{2}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always confirm $\varepsilon < 0.05$ (i.e., blockage below 5 %) before trusting uncorrected data. Automotive tests use $A_m / A_{ts} < 0.01$ to keep blockage negligible.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reynolds Number Scaling (Model → Prototype)

**Goal:** Find the wind-tunnel speed $V_m$ for a 1:$n$ scale model so that it experiences the *same* Reynolds number as the full-scale vehicle at speed $V_p$.

**Step 1 — Equate Reynolds numbers.**

$$
\frac{\rho_m\,V_m\,L_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_p\,V_p\,L_p}{\mu_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Use same air** ($\rho_m = \rho_p$, $\mu_m = \mu_p$):

$$
V_m\,L_m = V_p\,L_p
$$

**Step 3 — Insert scale ratio** $L_m = L_p / n$:

$$
V_m \cdot \frac{L_p}{n} = V_p \cdot L_p
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_m = n \cdot V_p\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Force scaling** (using $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A C_D$):

$$
\frac{F_{D,m}}{F_{D,p}} = \left(\frac{V_m}{V_p}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_p} = n^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^{2}} = 1
$$

If $C_D$ is preserved by Reynolds similarity, then $F_{D,m} = F_{D,p}$ — *the model sees the same drag force as the prototype*. This is the **great advantage of Reynolds-scaled automotive testing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subsonic rule of thumb:** Because real cars operate at $Re \sim 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$ and most subsonic tunnels are limited to $Re \sim 10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$ for full-scale models, **moving ground belts and boundary-layer suction** are used to artificially raise the effective Reynolds number on the model.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Subsonic Tunnel Configurations

| Tunnel Type | Test Section | Contraction Ratio | Speed Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Eiffel (open-circuit, open jet)** | Open | 5:1 to 6:1 | 10–60 m/s | Educational, low-speed aero |
| **NPL (open-circuit, closed jet)** | Closed | 7:1 | 15–80 m/s | General-purpose |
| **Göttingen (closed-circuit)** | Closed (often slotted) | 9:1 to 12:1 | 5–100 m/s | High-quality research, automotive |
| **Automotive full-scale closed** | Closed with moving belt | 7:1 | up to 60 m/s | OEM certification |
| **Vertical spin tunnel** | Closed | 6:1 | up to 40 m/s | Parachutes, rotating bodies |

---

### 3.5 Sample Numerical Problem — Worked Solution

> **Problem:** A 1:5 scale model of a car is tested in a subsonic wind tunnel at 25 m/s. The prototype travels at 90 km/h (i.e., 25 m/s). The measured drag on the model is 80 N. Frontal area of prototype is 2.2 m². Compute:
> (i) The drag coefficient, (ii) The drag on the prototype at 120 km/h.

**Solution:**

(i) Frontal area of model: $A_m = A_p / n^{2} = 2.2 / 25 = 0.088$ m².

Dynamic pressure: $q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (25)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 = 382.8$ Pa.

$$
C_D = \frac{F_{D,m}}{q\,A_m} = \frac{80}{382.8 \times 0.088} = \frac{80}{33.69} \approx 2.37
$$

This is unusually high — the $n=5$ scale suggests a Reynolds mismatch; we will use it as a *given* $C_D$ for the prototype.

(ii) At 120 km/h = 33.33 m/s:

$$
F_{D,p} = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A_p = 2.37 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^{2} \times 2.2
$$

Compute $(33.33)^{2} = 1110.9$:

$$
F_{D,p} = 2.37 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1110.9 \times 2.2
$$

$$
F_{D,p} = 2.37 \times 1495.5 \approx 3564 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_{D,p} \approx 3.56 \text{ kN}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Many students use $V_m = 25$ m/s for both (i) and (ii). The prototype speed is *different* in (ii), so the dynamic pressure must be **recalculated** at the new speed. Also, do *not* assume the same drag force; $C_D$ is preserved only if Reynolds similarity holds.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Circuit Subsonic Wind Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Drive Motor and Fan] --> A2[Flow Straightener Honeycomb]
    A2 --> A3[Anti Turbulence Screens]
    A3 --> A4[Contraction Cone]
    A4 --> A5[Test Section with Force Balance and Model]
    A5 --> A6[Diffuser]
    A6 --> A7[Drive Motor and Fan]
    A5 --> A8[Instrumentation Block Pressure Scans Force Balance Flow Vis]
    A8 --> A9[Data Acquisition and Computer]
```

**Reading the loop:** The fan pressurises air that flows sequentially through the **honeycomb → screens → contraction → test section → diffuser** and back to the fan. The **instrumentation block** taps signals from the test section (pressure tappings, balance output, Pitot-static probe) and routes them to the data-acquisition system.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Wind Tunnel Testing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Define Test Objective] --> B2[Select Scale Ratio and Similarity Parameters]
    B2 --> B3[Choose Tunnel Type and Test Section Size]
    B3 --> B4[Manufacture Instrumented Model]
    B4 --> B5[Calibrate Force Balance and Pressure Transducers]
    B5 --> B6[Install Model in Test Section]
    B6 --> B7[Set Tunnel Speed and Wait for Flow Stabilisation]
    B7 --> B8[Acquire Raw Force and Pressure Data]
    B8 --> B9[Apply Blockage and Wall Corrections]
    B9 --> B10[Compute Aerodynamic Coefficients CD CL CM]
    B10 --> B11[Report Uncertainty Analysis and Final Results]
```

**Reading the workflow:** This is a *procedural* diagram. It shows the **engineering decision chain** from defining a test objective all the way through to publishing final coefficients with uncertainty bounds — a structure that mirrors KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment rubrics.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Wind Tunnel Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Wind Tunnels] --> C2[Subsonic M less than 0.3]
    C1 --> C3[Transonic 0.3 to 1.2]
    C1 --> C4[Supersonic 1.2 to 5]
    C1 --> C5[Hypersonic M greater than 5]
    C2 --> C21[Eiffel Open Open Jet]
    C2 --> C22[NPL Open Closed Jet]
    C2 --> C23[Gottingen Closed Return Type]
    C3 --> C31[Slotted Wall Test Section]
    C3 --> C32[Perforated Wall Test Section]
    C4 --> C41[Blow Down Type]
    C4 --> C42[Indraft Type]
    C4 --> C43[Intermittent Type]
    C5 --> C51[Shock Tube]
    C5 --> C52[Plasma Arc Tunnel]
    C5 --> C53[Hot Shot Tunnel]
```

**Reading the tree:** This taxonomic chart maps the **speed-regime classification** to **structural variants** expected in KTU 2024 theory questions on wind-tunnel technology.

---

### 4.4 Schematic of a 6-Component Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Vehicle Model Mounted on Strut] --> D2[Upper Cross Beam with Strain Gauges]
    D2 --> D3[Lower Cross Beam with Strain Gauges]
    D3 --> D4[Side Beams for Side Force and Yaw Moment]
    D4 --> D5[Front and Rear Beams for Drag and Pitch Moment]
    D5 --> D6[Base Support Column with Lift and Roll Moment Channels]
    D6 --> D7[Signal Conditioner and DAQ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why six components?** A moving vehicle experiences three forces (drag, lift, side) and three moments (pitch, roll, yaw). A **six-component balance** resolves all of them simultaneously using cleverly arranged strain-gauge beams, so that the bending of one beam does not contaminate the reading of another.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between an open-circuit and a closed-circuit wind tunnel. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each in the context of vehicle testing.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (key points):**

| Aspect | Open-Circuit (Eiffel/NPL) | Closed-Circuit (Göttingen) |
|---|---|---|
| Air path | Drawn in fresh, expelled | Recirculated continuously |
| Power consumption | Higher (no pressure recovery) | Lower (diffuser recovers pressure) |
| Flow quality | Moderate, sensitive to ambient | High, well-controlled |
| Installation cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best for | Educational, low-budget tests | OEM-grade certification |

> **Advantage/Disadvantage examples:** Open-circuit is *cheap and simple* but *noisy and energy-inefficient*; closed-circuit is *energy-efficient and quiet* but *expensive and large-footprint*.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q2. What is the blockage effect in a closed-test-section wind tunnel? Write the expression for solid blockage ratio and state the typical acceptable limit.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Blockage effect:** The presence of the model in a confined test section displaces air and accelerates the flow around it, producing an *effective* dynamic pressure higher than the free-stream value. This causes the measured drag to be **higher than the free-flight value**.
- **Expression:** $\varepsilon_{sb} = K_{b}\,V_{m} / A_{ts}^{3/2}$
- **Acceptable limit:** $\varepsilon < 0.05$ (5 %).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **subsonic closed-circuit wind tunnel**. List the functions of the **contraction cone, test section, diffuser, honeycomb, and screens**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 1:4 scale model of a car is tested in a subsonic wind tunnel at 60 m/s. The measured drag on the model is 120 N. The frontal area of the prototype is 2.4 m², and the car is to travel at 144 km/h. Calculate: **(i)** the drag coefficient of the model, **(ii)** the drag force on the prototype, **(iii)** the power required to overcome drag at prototype speed. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (block diagram already shown in Section 4.1):**

- **Drive motor and fan** generate a pressure rise to drive air through the loop.
- **Honeycomb** straightens flow by breaking up large swirl vortices into smaller, more uniform cells.
- **Screens (fine wire mesh)** attenuate residual turbulence, reducing intensity from ~5 % to <0.1 %.
- **Contraction cone** accelerates the flow (by reducing cross-section per continuity) and converts static to dynamic pressure, improving velocity uniformity at the test section.
- **Test section** houses the model on a **force balance**; cross-section is constant so that velocity is nominally constant.
- **Diffuser** decelerates the flow, recovering static pressure and reducing the load on the fan (saves power).
- Air returns to the fan inlet, completing the **closed loop**.

> *Award full 7 marks if all six components are named, their physics explained, and the flow direction is correctly described.*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Scale ratio: 1:4 → $L_m = L_p / 4$
- Test speed: $V_m = 60$ m/s; $F_{D,m} = 120$ N
- Frontal area prototype: $A_p = 2.4$ m²; prototype speed = 144 km/h = 40 m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Frontal area of model:**

$$
A_m = \frac{A_p}{n^{2}} = \frac{2.4}{16} = 0.15 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic pressure at test speed:**

$$
q_m = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (60)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3600 = 2205 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — (i) Drag coefficient of the model:**

$$
C_D = \frac{F_{D,m}}{q_m A_m} = \frac{120}{2205 \times 0.15} = \frac{120}{330.75} \approx 0.363
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_D \approx 0.36\,}
$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Drag force on prototype at 144 km/h = 40 m/s:**

Dynamic pressure at prototype:

$$
q_p = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (40)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1600 = 980 \text{ Pa}
$$

Drag force (assuming $C_D$ unchanged — Reynolds similarity):

$$
F_{D,p} = C_D \cdot q_p \cdot A_p = 0.363 \times 980 \times 2.4
$$

$$
F_{D,p} = 0.363 \times 2352 \approx 853.8 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_{D,p} \approx 854 \text{ N}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Power at prototype speed:**

$$
P_p = F_{D,p} \cdot V_p = 853.8 \times 40 \approx 34\,152 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_p \approx 34.15 \text{ kW}\,}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Classify wind tunnels based on **speed range (Mach number)**. Explain the construction and working of a **blow-down supersonic wind tunnel** with a neat sketch. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define the **blockage effect**. Derive the expression for the **blockage-corrected drag coefficient** for a model tested in a closed test section. A model of frontal area 0.08 m² and drag coefficient 0.45 is tested in a square test section of side 1.6 m. Compute the **wake blockage ratio** and the **corrected drag coefficient**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mach Number Classification:**

| Range | Tunnel Type | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| $M < 0.3$ | Subsonic | Continuous flow, atmospheric pressure |
| $0.3 \le M \le 1.2$ | Transonic | Perforated / slotted walls to mitigate choking |
| $1.2 < M \le 5$ | Supersonic | Convergent-divergent (de Laval) nozzle |
| $M > 5$ | Hypersonic | Heated reservoirs, shock tubes |

**Blow-down Supersonic Wind Tunnel — Working:**

- Air is stored in a **high-pressure reservoir** (typically 5–40 atm).
- A **quick-opening valve** releases air into a **convergent-divergent (Laval) nozzle**.
- The nozzle accelerates the flow to supersonic speed; the test Mach number is set by the *area ratio* $A_{exit} / A_{throat}$ and reservoir pressure.
- After the test section, the air is exhausted into a **vacuum sphere** or to atmosphere.
- Run time is limited by reservoir capacity (typically 5 s to a few minutes).

> *Award full 7 marks for: classification table (3 marks) + blow-down working (3 marks) + diagram description (1 mark).*

**(b) Blockage Effect — Definition and Derivation:**

*Blockage effect* is the artificial increase in measured aerodynamic force caused by the model displacing air inside a confined test section, leading to locally higher velocity and dynamic pressure than the free-stream value.

**Derivation:**

When the model is placed, the effective flow area available to the airstream is reduced. By continuity, the velocity must increase:

$$
V_{\text{eff}} = \frac{V_{ts}}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

The measured drag is therefore based on $V_{\text{eff}}$:

$$
F_{D,\text{meas}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,V_{\text{eff}}^{2}\,A_{m}\,C_{D,\infty} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,\frac{V_{ts}^{2}}{(1-\varepsilon)^{2}}\,A_{m}\,C_{D,\infty}
$$

But the experimenter computes $C_D$ assuming $V = V_{ts}$:

$$
C_{D,\text{meas}} = C_{D,\infty}\,(1 - \varepsilon)^{-2}
$$

Solving for the free-air coefficient:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{D,\infty} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)^{2}\,}
$$

**Wake Blockage Calculation:**

$$
\varepsilon_{w} = \frac{C_{D}\,A_{m}}{4\,A_{ts}}
$$

Test section area: $A_{ts} = 1.6 \times 1.6 = 2.56$ m².

$$
\varepsilon_{w} = \frac{0.45 \times 0.08}{4 \times 2.56} = \frac{0.036}{10.24} \approx 0.00352
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{w} \approx 3.52 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.35 \%\,}
$$

Assuming solid blockage is small (typical for streamlined automotive models), $\varepsilon \approx \varepsilon_{w}$:

$$
C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} \cdot (1 - 0.00352)^{2} = 0.45 \times (0.99648)^{2}
$$

$$
C_{D,\text{corr}} = 0.45 \times 0.99297 \approx 0.4468
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{D,\text{corr}} \approx 0.447\,}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on wind-tunnel questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting pressure recovery in the diffuser.** Many students assume the fan must supply full $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$. Always *state* the diffuser recovery factor (typically 60–80 %) and reduce the fan power accordingly.
> 2. **Not converting km/h to m/s.** KTU problems frequently give prototype speed in km/h. A 144 km/h value used as 144 m/s will balloon the drag by a factor of ~13.
> 3. **Mixing up the scale ratio direction.** A *1:4 scale model* has $L_m = L_p / 4$ and $A_m = A_p / 16$. Inverting the ratio is a **3-mark killer** in numericals.
> 4. **Ignoring blockage corrections.** In a 14-mark question, neglecting blockage (when explicitly asked) leads to a full 7-mark loss on sub-part (b).
> 5. **Confusing the Mach number classification boundary.** Transonic tunnels can handle $M \le 1.2$ because of mixed subsonic/supersonic flow regions, *not* $M < 1$ (that would be sonic, not transonic).
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always state **N, kW, m², dimensionless**, etc. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before every revision.**

- [ ] Wind tunnel reverses the relative motion — air moves, model is fixed.
- [ ] Mach number classification: Subsonic (<0.3), Transonic (0.3–1.2), Supersonic (1.2–5), Hypersonic (>5).
- [ ] Circuit classification: Open (Eiffel/NPL) vs. Closed (Göttingen).
- [ ] **Key components in order:** Fan → Honeycomb → Screens → Contraction → Test Section → Diffuser → back to fan.
- [ ] **Contraction cone** → accelerates flow, improves uniformity, contraction ratio 6:1 to 9:1.
- [ ] **Diffuser** → decelerates flow, recovers pressure, saves fan power.
- [ ] **Honeycomb** → removes swirl; **Screens** → reduce turbulence.
- [ ] **Force balance** → 3-component or 6-component for full vehicle force/moment measurement.
- [ ] **Dynamic pressure:** $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$.
- [ ] **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$.
- [ ] **Blockage correction:** $C_{D,\text{corr}} = C_{D,\text{meas}} (1 - \varepsilon)^{2}$.
- [ ] **Wake blockage:** $\varepsilon_{w} = C_{D} A_{m} / (4 A_{ts})$.
- [ ] **Acceptable blockage limit:** $\varepsilon < 0.05$.
- [ ] **Reynolds similarity:** Same fluid → $V_m L_m = V_p L_p$ → $V_m = n V_p$ for 1:$n$ scale.
- [ ] **Force scaling:** $F_p / F_m = (V_p / V_m)^{2} (A_p / A_m)$.
- [ ] **Power scaling:** $P \propto V^{3}$ — doubling speed needs 8× power.
- [ ] **Automotive tunnels** use **moving ground belts** and **boundary-layer suction** to simulate the real road.
- [ ] **Open test section** → no wall corrections; **Closed test section** → requires blockage correction.
- [ ] Always include units: N, kW, m², Pa, dimensionless.
- [ ] **Typical $C_D$** for modern cars: 0.25–0.35; for trucks: 0.6–0.8.

---

*End of Module 4 — Principles of Wind Tunnel Technology | OEAUT723 — Vehicle Aerodynamics | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types and Components of Wind Tunnel - Limitation of Simulation - Stress with Scale Models - Full Scale Wind Tunnels - Measurement Techniques - Equipment and Transducers - Road Testing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Tunnel — Formal KTU Definition

A **Wind Tunnel** is a precisely engineered fluid-mechanics laboratory device that artificially generates a controlled, uniform stream of air (or other working gas) over a stationary or rotating test article so that aerodynamic phenomena — forces, moments, pressures, flow separation, and turbulence — can be measured, visualized, and quantified under rigorously repeatable, ground-based conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (KTU 2024):** A wind tunnel is the *primary experimental instrument* of vehicle aerodynamics. It converts a real, moving vehicle on a real road into a stationary vehicle in a controlled air-jet, while preserving the most important dimensionless similarity parameters (Reynolds number, Mach number, geometric fidelity).

For automotive and ground-vehicle work, the most frequently used class is the **low-speed, low-turbulence, full-scale or large-scale closed-circuit tunnel**, typically operating in the velocity band **15 m/s to 70 m/s** (≈ 54 km/h to 252 km/h).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Reverse Highway"

Imagine standing on a bridge and watching trucks zoom past you. The air is being pushed across your face. Now flip the picture: instead of moving trucks past a still observer, **place the truck on a giant treadmill and blow a precisely shaped wind across its nose**. The aerodynamic result is *almost identical*. That is the genius of the wind tunnel — *the test article is held still, while the air (the moving "highway of air-molecules") is dragged past it*.

A second analogy: think of a musical instrument. To hear the true sound of a violin, you play it in an acoustically clean room (anechoic chamber). To hear the true *aerodynamic voice* of a car, you test it in a tunnel whose flow has been scrubbed of swirl, eddies, and turbulence by honeycombs, screens, and a contraction — exactly the "acoustic cleanliness" of an anechoic chamber, but for air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why must we use wind tunnels at all if we have CFD?**
> Because CFD assumes a turbulence model; the tunnel measures **truth**. The two are **complementary**, never substitutes. Industry practice: CFD during design → wind tunnel for verification → road test for final certification.

## 1.3 Classification of Wind Tunnels — The Master Taxonomy

Wind tunnels for vehicle aerodynamics are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

### (A) By Circuit Topology
- **Open-Circuit (Open-Return / Eiffel Type):** Air enters, passes once through the test section, and is exhausted to atmosphere. Simple, cheap, but inlet flow is "dirty" (turbulent, non-uniform).
- **Closed-Circuit (Closed-Return / Göttingen Type):** Air circulates continuously inside an annular duct. Cleanest possible flow, lower power, but more expensive. **Preferred for automotive work.**

### (B) By Speed / Mach Number Regime
- **Low-Subsonic** ($M < 0.3$): The automotive workhorse. No compressibility.
- **High-Subsonic** ($0.3 < M < 0.85$): Transonic onset; local supersonic pockets appear on upper vehicle surfaces.
- **Transonic** ($0.85 < M < 1.2$): Requires slotted or perforated walls to suppress blockage.
- **Supersonic** ($M > 1.2$) and **Hypersonic** ($M > 5$): Not used for road vehicles.

### (C) By Test-Section Geometry
- **Open-Jet (Free-Jet):** Test section is an unconfined jet into a large hall. Excellent for road-vehicle testing because there is no wall boundary layer to interfere with the car's wake.
- **Closed-Throat (Wall-Jet):** Test section walls completely enclose the model. Higher blockage penalty.
- **Slotted-Wall:** Compromise — walls have transverse slots to relieve blockage.

### (D) By Application / Specialty
- **Full-Scale Vehicle Tunnels:** A single real car fits inside.
- **Climatic Wind Tunnels:** Combined with refrigeration/altitude chambers.
- **Acoustic Wind Tunnels:** Anechoic lining for wind-noise measurement.
- **Climatic-Aeroacoustic Tunnels:** Combined aero + thermal + noise (e.g., FKFS Stuttgart, GM Aero Lab).
- **Spin / Yaw Tunnels:** Turntable allows yaw-angle sweeps.
- **Climb / Soak Tunnels:** For engine cooling studies.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite opener:** *"Classify wind tunnels used for automotive testing with examples."* The answer must list at least *three* of the above categories and pair each with one specific facility name (e.g., Volkswagen's Climatic Wind Tunnel in Wolfsburg).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Closed-Circuit Subsonic Wind Tunnel

The flow path of a Göttingen-type tunnel follows a strict sequence. Let us trace the air from the fan back to the fan:

1. **Drive Section / Fan Room** — Contains the axial-flow or centrifugal fan driven by an electric motor (typically **150 kW to 4 MW** for automotive tunnels). Fan diameter may be **3 m to 6 m**.
2. **First Corner (with Corner Vanes)** — Turns flow 90°. **Corner vanes** (airfoil-shaped turning vanes, often 8–12 of them stacked vertically) prevent large-scale secondary flows and ensure uniform downstream velocity profile.
3. **Diffuser** — Expands flow area, converting kinetic energy into static pressure and *recovering pressure*. Diffuser half-angle kept **≤ 5°–7°** to avoid flow separation.
4. **Settling Chamber / Honeycomb Section** — Houses **flow-straightening honeycombs** (thin-walled cells, typically 6 mm–25 mm cell size, 75 mm–150 mm depth). Honeycombs remove swirl and lateral velocity components.
5. **Screens / Turbulence Reduction Screens** — 2 to 5 fine mesh screens (typically 20, 40, 60 mesh count). Each screen progressively reduces the **turbulence intensity** $Tu$ from ≈ 5% to **< 0.1%**.
6. **Contraction Cone** — The flow's "nozzle." Converges from large cross-section to the small test section. Contraction ratio $C_R = A_{in} / A_{test}$ is typically **6:1 to 10:1**. A higher ratio gives a more uniform, lower-turbulence flow.
7. **Test Section** — The measurement volume. Typically **10 m to 20 m long**, **3 m to 6 m wide**. Equipped with a **rolling road / 5-belt system** (for ground simulation), turntable (for yaw), and a **force balance** beneath the model.
8. **Second Diffuser** — Decelerates flow and recovers pressure before next corner.
9. **Second Corner** — Final 90° turn back to the fan inlet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four "Quality Indices" of a Wind Tunnel:**
> 1. **Turbulence Intensity** $Tu = \sqrt{\overline{u'^2}} / U_\infty < 0.1\%$ (good automotive tunnel).
> 2. **Flow Angularity** $\vert \alpha \vert < \pm 0.2°$ deviation from mean direction.
> 3. **Velocity Uniformity** $\Delta U / U_\infty < \pm 0.5\%$.
> 4. **Blockage Ratio** $A_{model} / A_{test} < 5\%$–10% (otherwise correction required).

## 2.2 Limitations of Simulation — Why Full Reality Cannot Be Reached

Despite enormous engineering effort, a wind tunnel is only a **model** of reality. Five fundamental limitations exist:

### Limitation 1 — Reynolds Number Mismatch
The most severe limitation. Real cars cruise at $Re \approx 1.5 \times 10^6$ to $5 \times 10^6$ based on car length. A 1:4 scale model at the same wind speed has $Re \approx 4 \times 10^5$ to $1.25 \times 10^6$. Boundary-layer transition location, separation behaviour, and wake structure all change with $Re$.

### Limitation 2 — Blockage Effect
The model occupies a finite fraction of the test section. Streamlines are squeezed around it, **artificially accelerating** the flow and **inflating forces** by typically **5% to 15%** at 10% blockage.

### Limitation 3 — Wall Interference
In closed-throat tunnels, the model-induced pressure field reflects off the walls and re-impinges on the model — a phenomenon absent on the open road. **Slotted walls** or **open-jet tunnels** mitigate this.

### Limitation 4 — Support / Sting Interference
The strut holding the model has its own drag, wake, and downwash. **Raked struts** or **blade-strut (strut-mounted inside the rear wing)** solutions exist.

### Limitation 5 — Ground Simulation Failure
Without a **moving ground belt**, the stationary floor of the test section forces the boundary layer to *thicken beneath the car*, artificially increasing drag. This is solved by the **5-belt rolling road** (one belt under each wheel + one central belt).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse *blockage* with *wall interference*. **Blockage** = solid blockage due to displaced volume. **Wall interference** = pressure-reflection effect. Both inflate forces but through *different physics* and *different corrections*.

## 2.3 Stress with Scale Models — The Reynolds Number Headache

The governing dimensionless group for incompressible vehicle flow is the **Reynolds number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, U_\infty \, L}{\mu} = \frac{U_\infty \, L}{\nu}$$

If we shrink the model by factor $k = L_{model} / L_{full}$, then at the same air and same test-section velocity:

$$Re_{model} = Re_{full} \cdot k$$

To restore full-scale $Re$, three engineering options are pursued:

### Option A — Pressurized Wind Tunnels
Increase $\rho$ by pressurizing the tunnel to **3 to 5 atm**. Since $Re \propto \rho$, a 4× pressure gives **4× the Reynolds number**. Example: **DNW (Deutsch-Nederlandse Windkanal)** — the world's largest pressurized tunnel, operating to **10 bar**.

### Option B — Cryogenic Wind Tunnels
Cool the gas to **−150 °C to −196 °C** (LN₂-cooled nitrogen). Lower $T$ → lower $\nu$ → higher $Re$. Example: **ONERA's F1 cryogenic tunnel** at Modane.

### Option C — Heavy-Gas Wind Tunnels
Replace air with a denser gas (e.g., **Freon, SF₆**). Since $Re \propto 1/\nu$, and dense gases have low kinematic viscosity, $Re$ rises. Example: **NLR's LSDT tunnel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not simply blow harder (higher $U$)?** Because doubling $U$ doubles $Re$ but **quadruples** power ($P \propto U^3$). Tunnel fans and power bills become prohibitive. Pressure and cryogenics are cheaper than fan power.

## 2.4 Full-Scale Wind Tunnels

A **full-scale wind tunnel** is one in which the entire, unmodified production vehicle fits inside the test section, on a real ground plane with simulated boundary layer, a yaw turntable, and a **moving-belt rolling road**. The world's leading facilities include:

| Facility | Test Section (L × W × H, m) | Max Speed (km/h) | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volkswagen, Wolfsburg** | 19 × 10 × 5 | 250 | Climatic, aero-acoustic combined |
| **Pininfarina, Turin** | 15 × 9 × 5 | 250 | Single-belt rolling road |
| **GM Aerodynamics Lab, Warren** | 18 × 8 × 5 | 220 | 5-belt rolling road |
| **FKFS Stuttgart** | 13 × 7 × 5 | 240 | Excellent flow quality ($Tu < 0.03\%$) |
| **MIRA, UK** | 16 × 8 × 5 | 240 | Climatic chamber |
| **Toyota Higashi-Fuji** | 25 × 12 × 6 | 270 | World's largest full-scale automotive tunnel |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — The Master "Cheat Code" Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single highest-yield reference for Module 4. Memorize every row.**

| # | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho U_\infty L / \mu = U_\infty L / \nu$ | dimensionless | Ratio of inertia to viscous forces |
| 2 | Mach Number | $M = U_\infty / a$ | dimensionless | Ratio of flow speed to sound speed |
| 3 | Drag Coefficient | $C_D = 2 F_D / (\rho U_\infty^2 A)$ | dimensionless | Normalized drag force |
| 4 | Lift Coefficient | $C_L = 2 F_L / (\rho U_\infty^2 A)$ | dimensionless | Normalized lift force |
| 5 | Side-Force Coefficient | $C_S = 2 F_S / (\rho U_\infty^2 A)$ | dimensionless | Normalized lateral force |
| 6 | Yaw Moment Coefficient | $C_N = 2 M_N / (\rho U_\infty^2 A L)$ | dimensionless | Normalized yawing moment |
| 7 | Pitching Moment Coefficient | $C_M = 2 M_M / (\rho U_\infty^2 A L)$ | dimensionless | Normalized pitch moment |
| 8 | Rolling Moment Coefficient | $C_L' = 2 M_L / (\rho U_\infty^2 A L)$ | dimensionless | Normalized roll moment |
| 9 | Dynamic Pressure | $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2$ | Pa | Pressure scale of all aero forces |
| 10 | Blockage Ratio | $BR = A_{model} / A_{test}$ | dimensionless | Wall-effect strength indicator |
| 11 | Turbulence Intensity | $Tu = u'_{rms} / U_\infty$ | dimensionless | Flow cleanliness metric |
| 12 | Contraction Ratio | $C_R = A_{inlet} / A_{test}$ | dimensionless | Flow uniformity multiplier |
| 13 | Power Required (fan) | $P = q_\infty \cdot Q \cdot \eta_{fan}$ | W | Energy cost of tunnel operation |
| 14 | Boundary-Layer Thickness | $\delta \approx 0.37 x / Re_x^{1/5}$ | m | Approximate flat-plate BL thickness |
| 15 | Skin-Friction Coefficient | $C_f \approx 0.074 Re_L^{-1/5}$ | dimensionless | Flat-plate local average $C_f$ |
| 16 | Strouhal Number (vortex shedding) | $St = f L / U_\infty$ | dimensionless | Frequency scaling |
| 17 | Froude Number | $Fr = U_\infty / \sqrt{g L}$ | dimensionless | Inertia/gravity ratio (less relevant for road vehicles) |
| 18 | Eötvös Number | $Eo = (\rho - \rho_a) g L^2 / \sigma$ | dimensionless | Buoyancy/capillary ratio (relevant to raindrops on windshield) |
| 19 | Pitot-Static Velocity | $U = \sqrt{2 (p_0 - p) / \rho}$ | m/s | From Bernoulli |
| 20 | Hot-Wire Calibration (King's Law) | $E^2 = A + B U^n$ | V² | Voltage-velocity relation of hot-wire anemometer |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize #1, #3, #4, #6, and #9 perfectly.** Every KTU question uses at least one of these. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` so that the table renders correctly.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dynamic Pressure as the Scale of Aerodynamic Force

We start from Newton's second law for a control volume of air with density $\rho$ flowing at velocity $U_\infty$ past a frontal area $A$.

**Step 1.** The mass flow rate through area $A$ per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, U_\infty \, A$$

**Step 2.** Each unit mass carries momentum equal to $U_\infty$. So the momentum flux through $A$ is:

$$\dot{M} = \dot{m} \, U_\infty = \rho \, U_\infty^2 \, A$$

**Step 3.** Any aerodynamic force (drag, lift, side-force) must, by dimensional reasoning, be **proportional to** this momentum flux — there is no other combination of $\rho$, $U_\infty$, and $A$ that has units of force. Therefore:

$$F \propto \rho \, U_\infty^2 \, A$$

**Step 4.** Define the dimensionless coefficient $C_F$ as the **proportionality constant**:

$$F = C_F \cdot \rho \, U_\infty^2 \, A$$

**Step 5.** It is conventional to absorb the factor of $1/2$ for symmetry with the kinetic-energy definition, giving the **dynamic pressure** $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2$:

$$F = C_F \cdot 2 q_\infty \, A = C_F \cdot \rho \, U_\infty^2 \, A$$

Thus any of $C_D$, $C_L$, $C_S$ is defined with the same convention:

$$\boxed{C_D = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho \, U_\infty^2 \, A} = \frac{F_D}{q_\infty \, A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the factor of 2 inserted?** Because $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2$ has the form of kinetic energy per unit volume. The factor of 2 keeps the standard formula aesthetically symmetric with $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number Scaling Across Scales

Suppose we test a full-scale vehicle of length $L_{full}$ at speed $U_{full}$, and a scale model of length $L_{model} = L_{full}/k$ at speed $U_{model}$ in the same air.

**Step 1.** Write the two Reynolds numbers:

$$Re_{full} = \frac{U_{full} \, L_{full}}{\nu}, \qquad Re_{model} = \frac{U_{model} \, L_{model}}{\nu}$$

**Step 2.** Form the ratio to enforce **dynamic similarity** $Re_{model} = Re_{full}$:

$$\frac{U_{model} \, L_{model}}{U_{full} \, L_{full}} = 1$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $L_{model} = L_{full}/k$:

$$\frac{U_{model}}{U_{full}} \cdot \frac{1}{k} = 1$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the required model velocity:

$$\boxed{U_{model} = k \cdot U_{full}}$$

**Step 5.** Read the result: to match full-scale $Re$ on a $1/k$ scale model, **you must blow $k$ times faster**. For $k = 4$ (a quarter-scale model), the tunnel must run at **4×** the full-scale vehicle speed — usually impossible.

**Numerical illustration.** Full-scale cruise at $U_{full} = 25$ m/s, $L_{full} = 4.5$ m. Quarter-scale model requires $U_{model} = 100$ m/s = **360 km/h**. Most automotive tunnels cannot sustain this. Hence the *need* for pressurized/cryogenic/heavy-gas techniques.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Return Tunnel Pressure-Rise Budget

A useful engineering check: a wind tunnel must overcome all **flow losses** $\Delta p$ in the loop, plus the test-section dynamic pressure rise. The fan pressure rise $\Delta p_{fan}$ satisfies:

$$\Delta p_{fan} = \Delta p_{losses} + \Delta p_{test}$$

The losses are the **sum** of:

- Corner losses (each corner): $\approx 0.2 \cdot q_\infty$
- Diffuser recovery: $+ 0.6 \, q_\infty$ (a recovery, so it *reduces* fan load)
- Contraction loss: $\approx 0.05 \cdot q_\infty$
- Screen losses: $\approx 0.1 \cdot q_\infty$ per screen
- Honeycomb loss: $\approx 0.2 \cdot q_\infty$
- Wall friction: $\approx 4 f (L/D) q_\infty$

For a 4-screen tunnel with 2 corners, 2 diffusers:

$$\Delta p_{fan} \approx 4 \cdot q_\infty \approx 2 \rho \, U_\infty^2$$

**Step 1.** Test-section dynamic pressure $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_\infty^2$. For $U_\infty = 50$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³:

$$q_\infty = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 50^2 = 1531.25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2.** Fan pressure-rise requirement:

$$\Delta p_{fan} \approx 4 \cdot 1531.25 = 6125 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3.** Power consumed by the fan $P = \Delta p \cdot Q / \eta$ where $Q = U_\infty A_{test}$. For $A_{test} = 10 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} = 40 \text{ m}^2$, $\eta = 0.85$:

$$Q = 50 \cdot 40 = 2000 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$P = \frac{6125 \cdot 2000}{0.85} \approx 14.4 \text{ MW}$$

This shows why **automotive wind tunnels are major industrial infrastructure** — power bills alone are millions of euros per year.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tunnel Selection Decision Tree

| Vehicle Class | Recommended Tunnel Type | Test-Section Size | Reynolds Match Needed? | Special Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formula 1 car | Closed-circuit, subsonic, open-jet | Small (2.5 × 3 m) | Yes — critical | 6-component balance, PIV |
| Passenger car (sedan) | Closed-circuit, subsonic, closed-throat | Medium (4 × 3 m) | Moderate | 5-belt rolling road |
| Heavy truck | Closed-circuit, large | Large (8 × 5 m) | Less critical | High-load balance |
| Motorcycle | Open-jet | Small (2 × 2 m) | Yes | Rider manikin + breathing simulator |
| Solar car | Open-jet, low-speed | Small | Yes | Optical access for flow viz |
| High-speed train | Closed-circuit, transonic-capable | Very large (10 × 6 m) | High | Long test section |
