# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Differentiate between open-circuit and closed-circuit wind tunnels. State one advantage of each for automotive testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Circuit | Closed-Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Air path | Passes once, exhausted to atmosphere | Continuously recirculated |
| Flow quality | Lower (intake turbulence) | Higher (settling chamber + screens) |
| Power consumption | Higher (single-pass fan) | Lower (recovered pressure) |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher |
| Climate control | Harder | Easier |

**Open-circuit advantage:** Simple construction, easier to install; ideal for *low-budget* testing or when the *test article is large* (trucks, buses).
**Closed-circuit advantage:** Superior flow quality with very low turbulence intensity ($Tu < 0.1\%$), and **full climate control** (essential for cold-start or hot-weather tests).

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark marking scheme (typical):** Definition (1 mark) + 2 distinguishing points (1 mark each).

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define blockage ratio. Why must it be limited to below 10% in automotive wind tunnels?**

**Model Answer:**
Blockage ratio $BR = A_{model} / A_{test}$, where $A_{model}$ is the model's frontal area and $A_{test}$ is the test-section cross-section. It quantifies the fraction of the test-section area physically occupied by the model.

**Reasons to limit $BR < 10\%$:**
1. Higher blockage **artificially accelerates** the oncoming streamlines around the model, inflating drag by up to **15%–20%** at $BR = 10\%$.
2. Solid blockage alters the effective freestream velocity used in coefficient calculations.
3. **Wall interference** grows non-linearly with $BR$ — at $BR > 10\%$, classical blockage corrections (Maskell, Hackett) lose accuracy.
4. Above 15% blockage, the flow may separate from the contraction lip, contaminating the entire test.

(Definition 1 mark + 2 reasons with brief explanation 2 marks = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice Q1]

**Q: (a)** Describe the major components of a closed-circuit subsonic wind tunnel used for vehicle testing. Explain the function of **honeycombs**, **screens**, and the **contraction cone** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1:5 scale model of a passenger car (full-scale length 4.5 m, full-scale cruise speed 30 m/s) is tested in a wind tunnel using air at standard conditions. Calculate:
- (i) The model length.
- (ii) The required wind-tunnel speed to match the full-scale Reynolds number.
- (iii) The ratio of dynamic pressures at model and full scale, and hence the ratio of measured-to-actual drag force on the model.
- (iv) Two practical difficulties in achieving this match. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A modern closed-circuit subsonic automotive wind tunnel comprises the following **major components** in flow order:

**1. Drive system / Fan.** An axial-flow or centrifugal fan (often carbon-fibre blades for low inertia) driven by an electric motor of 150 kW to 4 MW. Speed is typically controlled by a VFD (variable-frequency drive).

**2. Corner vanes.** Airfoil-shaped turning vanes at each 90° bend. They prevent large-scale secondary flows and Dean vortices that would otherwise contaminate downstream flow uniformity.

**3. Diffuser.** Slowly expands flow cross-section, recovering static pressure. Half-angle kept ≤ 5° to prevent separation.

**4. Honeycombs.** Thin-walled, hexagonal or square cells (typical cell size 6–25 mm, depth 75–150 mm). They remove **swirl and lateral velocity components** because the cells only permit flow along the cell axis. Honeycombs convert disordered rotational motion into parallel streamlines.

**5. Screens.** Fine wire mesh (20–80 mesh count), 2 to 5 in series. Each screen **damps turbulent fluctuations** and reduces turbulence intensity by a factor of ≈ 3. A typical 4-screen stack drops $Tu$ from 5% to below 0.1%. They also act as flow equalizers.

**6. Contraction cone.** The "nozzle." Reduces flow area from a large inlet to the small test section. Contraction ratio $C_R = A_{inlet}/A_{test}$ is typically 6:1 to 10:1. The cone **converts** upstream low-velocity high-pressure flow into downstream high-velocity uniform flow; by continuity and Bernoulli, the velocity accelerates but **spatial non-uniformities shrink**.

**7. Test section.** The working volume. 10–20 m long, 3–6 m wide, equipped with rolling road, turntable, force balance, and access for probes.

**8. Force balance (under the model).** A 6-component sting balance beneath the test-section floor.

**9. Rolling road / 5-belt system.** Simulates the moving ground. Each wheel belt runs at the wind-tunnel velocity, plus a central underbody belt.

(Marking: Fan+corner+diffuser 2 marks; honeycomb 1.5 marks; screens 1.5 marks; contraction 1.5 marks; test section + accessories 0.5 mark.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Scale factor $k = L_m / L_f = 1/5$; $L_f = 4.5$ m; $U_f = 30$ m/s; air ($\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s at 15 °C).

**(i) Model length:**

$$L_m = k \cdot L_f = \frac{1}{5} \cdot 4.5 = 0.9 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Model velocity for Reynolds match:** From Derivation 3.2:

$$U_m = k \cdot U_f = \frac{1}{5} \cdot 30 = 6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Sanity check via Reynolds numbers:**

$$Re_f = \frac{30 \cdot 4.5}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.25 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Re_m = \frac{6 \cdot 0.9}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.70 \times 10^{5}$$

Wait — these are not equal. Let us recompute carefully:

$$Re_m = \frac{6 \cdot 0.9}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{5.4}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.70 \times 10^{5}$$

$$Re_f = \frac{30 \cdot 4.5}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{135}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.25 \times 10^{6}$$

These are *not* equal — because $k = 1/5$, the model at speed $U_m = k \cdot U_f$ would give $Re_m = k^2 \cdot Re_f = (1/25) \cdot Re_f$. To match $Re$, we must *further* increase $U_m$ by factor $1/k = 5$:

$$U_{m,req} = U_f / k = 30 \cdot 5 = 150 \text{ m/s}$$

Then check:

$$Re_{m,req} = \frac{150 \cdot 0.9}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.25 \times 10^{6} \checkmark$$

**Corrected Answer to (ii):** $U_m = 150$ m/s = **540 km/h**.

**(iii) Ratio of dynamic pressures and drag forces:**

Dynamic pressure ratio:

$$\frac{q_m}{q_f} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_m^2}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho U_f^2} = \frac{U_m^2}{U_f^2} = \left(\frac{1}{k}\right)^2 = k^{-2} = 25$$

So the model test runs at **25×** the dynamic pressure. Frontal-area ratio:

$$\frac{A_m}{A_f} = k^2 = \frac{1}{25}$$

For identical $C_D$ (Reynolds-matched), measured drag force:

$$\frac{F_{D,m}}{F_{D,f}} = \frac{C_D \, q_m \, A_m}{C_D \, q_f \, A_f} = \frac{q_m}{q_f} \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_f} = 25 \cdot \frac{1}{25} = 1$$

**Beautifully, the measured force on the model equals the force on the full-scale car.** This is the *point* of Reynolds matching.

**(iv) Two practical difficulties in achieving the match:**

1. **Tunnel speed too high.** Required $U_m = 150$ m/s is beyond most subsonic automotive tunnels (max ≈ 70 m/s). Operating at this speed induces **compressibility effects** and may exceed the maximum power of the fan.

2. **Power consumption.** Fan power scales as $U^3$:

$$\frac{P_m}{P_f} = \left(\frac{U_m}{U_f}\right)^3 = 5^3 = 125$$

The tunnel consumes 125× more power than at the design speed. The fan, motor, and duct heating cannot sustain this.

**Hence the real-world solution is pressurized or cryogenic tunnels** (see Section 2.3).

(Part b marking: (i) 1 mark, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks = 7 marks.)

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Internal Choice Q2]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **limitations of wind-tunnel simulation** of road vehicles. Discuss in detail **blockage**, **wall interference**, and **ground-boundary-layer simulation** issues. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **6-component strain-gauge force balance** used to measure aerodynamic forces and moments on a vehicle model. A balance is calibrated to give $F_x = 12.5$ N (raw output) when a known drag of 50 N is applied. Calculate the **calibration constant** and the **measured drag** if the raw output during a test is 8.75 N. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Wind tunnels are powerful but imperfect simulators of the open road. The following five limitations are critical for vehicle testing:

**1. Reynolds-number mismatch.** Real road conditions: $Re \approx 10^6$ to $10^7$ based on vehicle length. Scale-model tests give much lower $Re$, altering boundary-layer transition and wake behaviour. Solutions: pressurized/cryogenic/heavy-gas tunnels.

**2. Blockage.** The model displaces air that would otherwise flow straight through the test section. Streamlines are squeezed, locally accelerating the flow. This **artificially raises** the measured drag by 5%–15% (Maskell correction: $C_{D,corrected} = C_{D,measured} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)$ where $\epsilon$ is the solid-blockage fraction).

**3. Wall interference.** The walls confine the flow and reflect pressure perturbations back onto the model. In contrast, on the open road the wake expands freely. Closed-throat tunnels exaggerate lift and pitching-moment coefficients. Open-jet or slotted-wall tunnels mitigate but do not eliminate this.

**4. Support / sting interference.** The strut that holds the model has its own drag, downwash, and wake that contaminate the measurement. Solutions: blade-mounted rear-wing supports, raked sting struts, reflection-plane techniques.

**5. Ground-boundary-layer simulation.** The test-section floor develops its own boundary layer, which can grow thick enough to **flood the underbody** of the car, increasing drag. Solution: **5-belt rolling road** — four belts under each wheel plus a central belt, all moving at $U_\infty$. Without it, road-load drag is overestimated by 5%–10%.

(Each limitation: 1.2 marks + integration/relevance 1 mark.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block-diagram description (from Section 4.4):** The aerodynamic loads on the model pass through an adapter plate and sting strut into the **balance flexures**. The flexures are instrumented with **strain gauges** arranged in **Wheatstone-bridge** configurations. Each bridge produces an analog voltage proportional to strain on one flexure. The signal is amplified, digitized by a 16-bit ADC, and fed into a **matrix-inversion algorithm** that resolves the six strain signals into the six rigid-body components:

$$F_x, \, F_y, \, F_z, \, M_x, \, M_y, \, M_z$$

The cross-coupling matrix is established during **static calibration** using dead-weights and lever-arm loading.

**Numerical problem:**

**Calibration constant** $K$ is defined by:

$$F_{D,actual} = K \cdot V_{raw}$$

Given: $V_{raw} = 12.5$ N (in balance-output units, equivalent to mV after calibration scaling) produces $F_{D} = 50$ N:

$$K = \frac{F_{D,actual}}{V_{raw}} = \frac{50 \text{ N}}{12.5 \text{ N}} = 4.0$$

So **the calibration constant is 4.0** (force per unit raw output).

**Measured drag** when $V_{raw} = 8.75$ N:

$$F_{D,measured} = K \cdot V_{raw} = 4.0 \cdot 8.75 = 35.0 \text{ N}$$

(Part b marking: Diagram description 3 marks; calibration equation 1 mark; K calculation 1.5 marks; measured-drag calculation 1.5 marks = 7 marks.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners dock marks on Module 4 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing blockage with wall interference.** Both inflate drag but through *different physics*. Solid blockage = displaced volume; wall interference = pressure reflection. Examiners *will* dock 2 marks if you merge them.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the dynamic-pressure formula.** The standard is $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2$, **not** $\rho U^2$. A common slip costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Writing $U_m = k U_f$ to "match" Reynolds number** when actually the requirement is $U_m = U_f/k$ for a $1/k$-scale model. Direction of the inequality is *critical* — examiners specifically test this trap.
>
> 4. **Stating that an open-circuit tunnel has better flow quality than closed-circuit.** The opposite is true. 1 mark lost.
>
> 5. **In a 6-component balance question, omitting the matrix inversion** step. Students often stop at "strain gauges measure strain," missing that the *six* outputs must be **decoupled** through the calibration matrix to obtain individual forces and moments.
>
> 6. **Using $A_{frontal}$ in $C_D$ but $A_{plan}$ in $C_L$.** This is a classic error. Automotive convention uses **frontal area** $A_f$ for *all three* force coefficients $C_D, C_L, C_S$.
>
> 7. **Forgetting that road testing suffers from uncontrollable wind variability**, not the tunnel. Many students write "tunnel has variability, road is steady" — the *opposite* is closer to the truth for low-turbulence tunnels.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Last 60 seconds before the exam):**

- [ ] Wind tunnel = **stationary model, moving air**; CFD = **moving model in stationary air** mathematically. Tunnels measure **truth**, CFD measures **model truth**.
- [ ] **Closed-circuit** = best flow quality + climate control; **open-circuit** = simpler, cheaper.
- [ ] **Honeycombs** remove swirl (lateral velocity); **screens** reduce turbulence intensity; **contraction cone** accelerates flow and shrinks non-uniformities by factor $\sqrt{C_R}$.
- [ ] **Blockage ratio** $BR < 10\%$; corrections: Maskell, Hackett, boundary-blockage (Allen).
- [ ] **Reynolds matching** at 1:5 scale needs $U_m = 5 U_{full}$, hence **pressurized** (DNW, 10 bar), **cryogenic** (ONERA F1, 80 K), or **heavy-gas** (NLR, SF₆) tunnels.
- [ ] **Full-scale automotive tunnels** operate 15–70 m/s; **test-section quality targets** $Tu < 0.1\%$, $\Delta U / U < \pm 0.5\%$, $\alpha < \pm 0.2°$.
- [ ] **5-belt rolling road** simulates ground motion; without it, drag is **overestimated** by 5–10%.
- [ ] **Force measurement** uses 6-component balance (Fx, Fy, Fz, Mx, My, Mz); calibration via dead-weights and matrix inversion.
- [ ] **Pressure measurement** uses piezoresistive or piezoelectric transducers; **velocity** via Pitot-static (Bernoulli) or hot-wire (King's Law).
- [ ] **Flow visualization:** smoke, tufts, oil-film, PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry).
- [ ] **Road testing:** coastdown (deceleration → $C_D$), constant-speed fuel consumption, GPS+anemometer for $C_D$-vs-yaw mapping.
- [ ] **Universal formula family:** $C_X = 2 F_X / (\rho U^2 A)$, $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2$, $Re = U L / \nu$.
- [ ] **Wind tunnel advantages over road:** controlled environment, repeatable, no driver variability. **Road testing advantages:** true $Re$, true boundary layer, true yaw variability, full-scale.
- [ ] **KTU-favoured facts to memorize verbatim:** Volkswagen Wolfsburg, Pininfarina Turin, FKFS Stuttgart, MIRA, Toyota Higashi-Fuji — these are the "famous five" full-scale automotive tunnels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Methods and CFD - Aerodynamic Study of F1 Racing Cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Numerical Methods and CFD - Aerodynamic Study of F1 Racing Cars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Numerical Methods** in vehicle aerodynamics refer to the set of mathematical algorithms used to convert the continuous governing partial differential equations of fluid flow (Navier–Stokes equations) into a finite, solvable system of algebraic equations. **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is the engineering discipline that uses these numerical methods together with high-speed computing to simulate and analyze fluid flow phenomena around geometric bodies.

In the context of Formula 1 racing, CFD has emerged as the **primary virtual engineering tool** for analyzing airflow over highly complex geometries such as front and rear wings, diffusers, brake ducts, sidepods, the floor, and the suspension components. It complements and partially replaces expensive wind-tunnel testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> "Numerical methods involve the discretization of governing fluid equations using techniques like Finite Difference Method (FDM), Finite Volume Method (FVM), or Finite Element Method (FEM), which are then solved iteratively to obtain pressure, velocity, and turbulence quantities around a vehicle body."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Wind Tunnel"

Imagine you have a **giant 3D chessboard** where each square (cell) is a tiny measuring station placed at a fixed point in space around an F1 car. You blow virtual air over the car, and at every time-step you record the pressure, velocity, and temperature at every chess square.

- **The car** = the obstacle in the grid.
- **The chessboard** = the **computational mesh** (millions of cells).
- **The recording instrument** = the **governing equations** (Navier–Stokes) applied at each cell.
- **The simulated wind** = the boundary conditions and inlet velocity.

Instead of measuring one point with a physical probe, you are simultaneously measuring **everywhere** — that is CFD. It is essentially a **virtual, parallel, ultra-fine wind tunnel** that runs on a supercomputer.

## 1.3 Why F1 Cars Specifically Demand CFD

Formula 1 cars operate in the **ground-effect regime** with extreme aerodynamic sensitivity. Small geometric changes (a 2 mm gurney flap adjustment) can alter downforce by hundreds of newtons. CFD allows:

- **Rapid design iteration** — testing hundreds of geometries per day.
- **Flow visualization** — viewing streamlines, vortices, and separation regions.
- **Performance estimation** — predicting **C_D** (drag coefficient), **C_L** (lift coefficient, which is *negative* for downforce), and **L/D ratio**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key reference values used universally in F1 CFD studies:
> - **Air density, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** (ISA sea-level standard).
> - **Kinematic viscosity, ν = 1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**.
> - **Dynamic viscosity, μ = 1.789 × 10⁻⁵ kg/(m·s)**.
> - **Speed of sound, a = 340.3 m/s** at 15 °C.
> - **FIA regulatory velocity in CFD testing, V_ref = 50 m/s (≈180 km/h)** for correlation.
> - **Reference area, A_ref** for F1 cars ≈ 1.5 m².

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical CFD Approach Used in F1

To make the otherwise unsolvable Navier–Stokes equations tractable, F1 teams use a layered modeling strategy:

### Step 1 — Governing Equations (Continuous)
The full incompressible Navier–Stokes (INS) equations describe **mass conservation** and **momentum conservation** for every fluid particle.

### Step 2 — Turbulence Modeling (Reynolds-Averaged)
Because F1 flow is **fully turbulent** (Re > 10⁶), direct simulation is impossible. Engineers apply **Reynolds-Averaging** to obtain the **RANS (Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes)** equations, which introduce an additional unknown: the **Reynolds Stress Tensor**.

### Step 3 — Closure via Turbulence Models
To close the RANS system, additional transport equations are solved for turbulent quantities. Common models in F1 CFD:

- **k–ε model** — robust, used for external flow far from walls.
- **k–ω SST model** — industry standard for F1; handles adverse pressure gradients on wings and diffusers exceptionally well.
- **LES / DDES** — Large-Eddy Simulation variants used for detailed vortex resolution, e.g., wing-tip vortices and tire wake.

### Step 4 — Discretization
The continuous PDEs are converted to algebraic equations on a mesh. F1 teams almost exclusively use the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** because it conserves mass, momentum, and energy on each cell.

### Step 5 — Solver Execution
Iterative solvers (SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, PISO, or coupled solvers) march towards a converged steady-state (or advance in time for unsteady DES simulations).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why average?** It removes the cost of resolving every turbulent eddy, reducing computation by **orders of magnitude**.
- **Why FVM?** F1 geometries are curved and complex; FVM handles arbitrary polyhedral cells naturally.
- **Why SST?** Wings, diffusers, and the underfloor create strong **adverse pressure gradients** causing separation; SST predicts this separation onset correctly.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions (F1-Specific)

| Boundary | Type | Setting |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet | Velocity inlet | **V = 50 m/s** (FIA mandated), turbulent intensity ≈ 0.5 % |
| Outlet | Pressure outlet | p = 0 (gauge) |
| Ground | Moving wall | V_wall = V_∞, no-slip |
| Car body | No-slip wall | V_wall = 0 |
| Wheels | Rotating wall | ω translated to tangential velocity |
| Symmetry plane | Symmetry | Used on half-car to save cells |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these equations — they form the **backbone** of Module 4 numerical problems.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Continuity:} \quad & \nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Momentum (INS):} \quad & \rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V}\cdot\nabla\mathbf{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{V} + \mathbf{f} \\[4pt]
\text{RANS Momentum:} \quad & \rho \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \rho \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \tau_{ij}}{\partial x_j} \\[4pt]
\text{Reynolds Stress:} \quad & \tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} \\[4pt]
\text{Boussinesq Hypothesis:} \quad & -\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \nu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) - \tfrac{2}{3} k \delta_{ij} \\[4pt]
\text{Turbulent kinetic energy:} \quad & k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'} \\[4pt]
\text{Drag force:} \quad & F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 \, C_D \, A_{\text{ref}} \\[4pt]
\text{Downforce (negative lift):} \quad & F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 \, C_L \, A_{\text{ref}} \\[4pt]
\text{Pressure coefficient:} \quad & C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2} \\[4pt]
\text{Y+ target (k–ω SST):} \quad & y^+ = \frac{\rho u_\tau y_w}{\mu} \approx 1 \\[4pt]
\text{Grid Independence Index:} \quad & GCI = \frac{F_s \vert \epsilon \vert}{r^p - 1} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic tip:** Always pair the **dynamic pressure (½ρV²)** with any aerodynamic coefficient. It is the *currency* in which all aerodynamic forces are quoted.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

This numerical framework is not academic — every modern F1 team (Mercedes-AMG, Red Bull, Ferrari, McLaren) runs **> 100 million CPU-hours per season** on CFD. The FIA's 2022 regulations even explicitly **permit** CFD usage up to a defined computational allowance (TAF - Token Allocation Factor) to democratize the technology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RANS Momentum Equation from Instantaneous Navier–Stokes

This is the **single most important derivation** in this module.

**Step 1 — Start with the instantaneous incompressible momentum equation:**

$$
\rho \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + \rho u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} + f_i
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Reynolds Decomposition.** Every instantaneous variable is split into a mean (overline) and a fluctuating (prime) component:

$$
u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i', \quad p = \overline{p} + p'
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the decomposed variables back into the momentum equation.** Since the instantaneous equation is *linear* in time derivative and viscosity term, the decomposition substitutes directly. However, the **non-linear convective term** $u_j u_i$ produces the cross-product terms.

**Step 4 — Expand the non-linear convective term:**

$$
u_j u_i = (\overline{u_j} + u_j')(\overline{u_i} + u_i') = \overline{u_j}\,\overline{u_i} + \overline{u_j}\,u_i' + u_j'\,\overline{u_i} + u_j' u_i'
$$

**Step 5 — Time-average the entire equation.** By definition of Reynolds averaging:

- $\overline{u_i} = \overline{u_i}$ (mean is unchanged).
- $\overline{u_i'} = 0$ (mean of fluctuation is zero).
- $\overline{\overline{u_j}\,\overline{u_i}} = \overline{u_j}\,\overline{u_i}$.
- $\overline{u_j'\,u_i'} \neq 0$ — this is the **Reynolds stress**.

**Step 6 — Collect the surviving terms:**

$$
\rho \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \rho \overline{u_j}\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \rho \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j} + \overline{f_i}
$$

**Step 7 — Define the Reynolds Stress Tensor:**

$$
\tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}
$$

This is a **symmetric second-order tensor** with 6 independent components (3 normal + 3 shear). It has now added **6 new unknowns**, making the system mathematically unclosed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closure requires a turbulence model.** This is the entire purpose of models such as k–ε and k–ω SST — to provide transport equations for quantities that allow us to estimate $\tau_{ij}$.

## 3.2 Discretization of the Generic Transport Equation (FVM Form)

The general scalar transport equation solved by every FVM-based CFD code is:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}\phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi
$$

**Step 1 — Integrate over an arbitrary control volume (CV) $\Omega$ bounded by surface $\partial \Omega$:**

$$
\int_\Omega \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t}\, d\Omega + \int_\Omega \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}\phi)\, d\Omega = \int_\Omega \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi)\, d\Omega + \int_\Omega S_\phi\, d\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Divergence Theorem to convert volume integrals of divergence to surface integrals:**

$$
\int_\Omega \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t}\, d\Omega + \oint_{\partial \Omega} \rho \mathbf{V}\phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \oint_{\partial \Omega} \Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} + \int_\Omega S_\phi\, d\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Discretize the surface integrals.** Let each CV face have area A_f, and approximate the surface flux as the value at the face center:

$$
\oint_{\partial \Omega} \rho \mathbf{V}\phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} \approx \sum_f (\rho \mathbf{V}\phi)_f \cdot \mathbf{A}_f
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the face value $(\rho \mathbf{V}\phi)_f$ using an interpolation scheme** (upwind, central differencing, or blended). For F1 external aerodynamics, **second-order upwind** is the standard.

**Step 5 — Final discrete algebraic form per cell P:**

$$
a_P \phi_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b_P
$$

where $a_P$ is the central coefficient, the sum runs over all neighbor cells, and $b_P$ contains source terms. This linear system is solved iteratively using **TDMA** (line-by-line) or algebraic multigrid.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Estimating Downforce on an F1 Front Wing

**Given:** F1 front wing reference area $A_{\text{ref}} = 0.4\ \text{m}^2$, $V_\infty = 80\ \text{m/s}$ (≈ 288 km/h), CFD-predicted $C_L = -1.8$ (negative = downforce), $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure:**

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 80^2 = 3920\ \text{N/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute downforce:**

$$
F_L = q \cdot C_L \cdot A_{\text{ref}} = 3920 \times (-1.8) \times 0.4 = -2822.4\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:**

The negative sign indicates **downward** force. The magnitude, **2822 N ≈ 288 kgf**, is the load pressing the front tires into the asphalt, allowing the F1 car to corner at > 5g.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked to compute aerodynamic force, **always** state the sign convention explicitly. Downforce = negative lift. Marks are lost when students return positive lift without interpreting it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End CFD Workflow for an F1 Car

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Geometry IGES / STEP] --> B[Surface Clean-up Blending Spline Fit]
    B --> C[Enclosure Domain Generation]
    C --> D[Surface Mesh Triangulation]
    D --> E[Volume Mesh Generation]
    E --> F[Prism Layer Inflation near walls]
    F --> G[Boundary Condition Setup]
    G --> H[Choose Solver Settings k-omega SST]
    H --> I[Run Steady RANS Solver]
    I --> J[Convergence Monitoring]
    J --> K{Converged?}
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[Post Processing]
    L --> M[Cp Contours Streamlines Vectors]
    M --> N[Cd and CL Force Calculation]
    N --> O[Compare with Wind Tunnel Data]
    O --> P[Design Iteration Decision]
    P -- Modify --> B
    P -- Accept --> Q[Final F1 Aerodynamic Package]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CFD Code

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        I1[Geometry]
        I2[Mesh]
        I3[Boundary Conditions]
    end
    subgraph PreProcessor
        P1[Import and Repair]
        P2[Scale and Domain]
        P3[Cell Assignment]
    end
    subgraph Solver
        S1[Initialize Field]
        S2[Compute Gradients]
        S3[Assemble Fluxes]
        S4[Linear System Solver]
        S5[Update Variables]
        S6[Check Convergence]
    end
    subgraph PostProcessor
        PO1[Cp and Cf Plots]
        PO2[Streamlines and Vectors]
        PO3[Force Coefficients]
    end
    I1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> S1
    I2 --> P3
    I3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    S6 -- Iterate --> S2
    S6 -- Converged --> PO1
    S6 -- Converged --> PO2
    S6 -- Converged --> PO3
```

## 4.3 Mesh Quality Requirements for F1 CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mesh Quality Assessment] --> N1{Skewness Less Than 0.85}
    M --> N2{Aspect Ratio Less Than 5 in core region}
    M --> N3{Y+ Equals 1 on walls}
    M --> N4{Orthogonal Quality Greater Than 0.15}
    N1 -- Pass --> OK[Mesh Suitable]
    N1 -- Fail --> FIX1[Remesh locally]
    N2 -- Pass --> OK
    N2 -- Fail --> FIX2[Re-draw Mesh]
    N3 -- Pass --> OK
    N3 -- Fail --> FIX3[Adjust First Layer Height]
    N4 -- Pass --> OK
    N4 -- Fail --> FIX4[Re-project Nodes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Block Diagrams:**
> - Diagram 4.1 traces the **entire CFD cycle** from CAD import to aerodynamic conclusion. Note the feedback loop indicating iterative design refinement.
> - Diagram 4.2 decomposes the **internal solver architecture** — this mirrors how commercial codes (ANSYS Fluent, Star-CCM+, OpenFOAM) are structurally organized.
> - Diagram 4.3 maps the **mesh-quality gates** an F1 aerodynamicist checks before trusting any numerical result.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question 1
**Define the Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) equations and explain why they are used in Formula 1 CFD instead of the full unsteady Navier–Stokes equations.**

**Model Answer:**
The RANS equations are obtained by decomposing the instantaneous Navier–Stokes velocity and pressure fields into a time-averaged component and a fluctuating component, then time-averaging the resulting equations. They introduce the Reynolds stress tensor $-\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ which represents the momentum flux due to turbulent eddies.

In Formula 1 CFD, the instantaneous Reynolds number is of the order $10^6$, meaning the flow is fully turbulent with a vast range of eddy length scales (Kolmogorov microscales are $\sim 10\ \mu\text{m}$). Resolving all scales (DNS) is computationally impossible. RANS provides a tractable alternative by **modeling** — rather than resolving — the turbulence, reducing computation time by 3–4 orders of magnitude. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question 2
**State the Boussinesq hypothesis used in two-equation turbulence models and write the resulting relation between Reynolds stress and mean velocity gradient.**

**Model Answer:**
The Boussinesq hypothesis states that turbulent momentum transfer can be modeled analogously to molecular viscosity, using an **eddy viscosity** $\nu_t$:

$$
-\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \nu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) - \tfrac{2}{3}k \delta_{ij}
$$

where $\nu_t$ is the turbulent (eddy) viscosity, $k$ is the turbulent kinetic energy, and $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question Paper – Module 4]**
> *Answer the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the RANS momentum equation starting from the instantaneous incompressible Navier–Stokes equation. Clearly state the Reynolds decomposition and explain the origin of the Reynolds stress tensor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an F1 rear-wing element, CFD simulation at $V_\infty = 75\ \text{m/s}$ gives $C_L = -1.45$, $C_D = 0.18$, and $A_{\text{ref}} = 0.35\ \text{m}^2$. Calculate:
   - (i) The aerodynamic downforce produced.
   - (ii) The aerodynamic drag.
   - (iii) The L/D efficiency ratio.
   - Take $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The expected answer must include:

- Step 1: Writing instantaneous momentum equation. **(1 mark)**
- Step 2: Reynolds decomposition $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$. **(1 mark)**
- Step 3: Substitution into convective term and expansion. **(2 marks)**
- Step 4: Time-averaging and elimination of fluctuation means. **(1 mark)**
- Step 5: Final RANS form and definition $\tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Downforce:**

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(75)^2 = 3445.3\ \text{N/m}^2
$$

$$
F_L = q \cdot C_L \cdot A_{\text{ref}} = (3445.3)(-1.45)(0.35) = -1748.4\ \text{N}
$$

Magnitude of downforce = **1748.4 N (downward)**. **(2.5 marks)**

**(ii) Drag:**

$$
F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A_{\text{ref}} = (3445.3)(0.18)(0.35) = 217.1\ \text{N}
$$

**Drag = 217.1 N**. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) L/D Ratio:**

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{\vert F_L \vert}{F_D} = \frac{1748.4}{217.1} = 8.05
$$

**L/D ≈ 8.05**. **(2.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete **CFD solution methodology** adopted for aerodynamic analysis of an F1 racing car. Discuss the role of (i) domain size, (ii) mesh independence study, and (iii) turbulence model selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CFD study on an F1 front-wheel wake reports a maximum $C_p$ of $-1.85$ at a stagnation point on the tire surface and a minimum $C_p$ of $-0.92$ at a wing-element suction surface, both measured at $V_\infty = 70\ \text{m/s}$, $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
   - (i) Compute the absolute pressure difference between the two points in kPa.
   - (ii) Comment on why the stagnation region $C_p$ is not exactly +1 in CFD but lower. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Discussion (7 marks):**

The expected answer must include a flow diagram covering: **Geometry → Domain → Mesh → BC → Solver → Convergence → Post-processing**. Refer to **Section 4.1 / 4.2** of these notes for the canonical block diagram.

(i) **Domain size:** F1 CFD domain should extend at least **5 car lengths** downstream, **3 widths** laterally, and **5 lengths** upstream of the car to avoid inlet/outlet boundary interference. **(2 marks)**

(ii) **Mesh independence study:** Three progressively refined meshes (e.g., 8M, 20M, 45M cells) are run; the solution is considered grid-independent when the change in $C_L$ or $C_D$ between successive meshes is **< 1 %**. The Grid Convergence Index (GCI) quantifies this. **(2.5 marks)**

(iii) **Turbulence model selection:** For F1 external flows, the **k–ω SST** model is preferred because it combines the near-wall accuracy of the k–ω model with the free-stream robustness of k–ε. **(2.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Absolute pressure difference:**

The dynamic pressure at $V_\infty = 70\ \text{m/s}$:

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(70)^2 = 3001.25\ \text{N/m}^2
$$

Pressure at stagnation point ($C_p = -1.85$):

$$
p_1 = p_\infty + C_p \cdot q = p_\infty + (-1.85)(3001.25) = p_\infty - 5552.3\ \text{Pa}
$$

Pressure at suction point ($C_p = -0.92$):

$$
p_2 = p_\infty + C_p \cdot q = p_\infty + (-0.92)(3001.25) = p_\infty - 2761.15\ \text{Pa}
$$

Absolute pressure difference:

$$
\Delta p = \vert p_1 - p_2 \vert = 5552.3 - 2761.15 = 2791.15\ \text{Pa} \approx 2.79\ \text{kPa}
$$

**(2.5 marks)**

**(ii) Comment:**

In real CFD, the stagnation $C_p$ is **never exactly +1** because:
- The flow around the F1 tire is not inviscid; viscous effects and boundary-layer development reduce the peak suction/stagnation value.
- The tire is **rotating** (slip angle ≠ 0), introducing tangential velocity components.
- Mesh resolution and turbulence modeling introduce numerical smoothing.

A $C_p$ of $-1.85$ (note: in tire wake regions with reversed flow, $C_p$ can even be negative at geometric stagnation due to wake recirculation). **(4.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $C_L$:** Always quote downforce as a *negative lift* or *positive downforce*; do not omit the sign.
> 2. **Forgetting dynamic pressure $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$:** Many students compute $F_L = C_L \cdot A$ directly — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Units mismatch:** $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s, $A$ in m² — do not mix km/h and m².
> 4. **Boussinesq hypothesis:** Students often forget the $-\tfrac{2}{3}k\delta_{ij}$ term; full marks require the complete expression.
> 5. **FVM vs FDM confusion:** KTU specifically asks for FVM in F1 applications. Writing FDM without justification loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- **CFD** in F1 = solving discretized Navier–Stokes on a mesh, iteratively, with a turbulence model.
- **Three pillars:** (1) Geometry & Mesh, (2) Governing equations + Turbulence model, (3) Solver + Post-processing.
- **RANS** = time-averaged Navier–Stokes + Reynolds stress $\tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$.
- **Closure problem** solved via **Boussinesq hypothesis** using eddy viscosity $\nu_t$.
- **Most popular turbulence model for F1:** **k–ω SST** (handles adverse pressure gradients).
- **Discretization method used in F1:** **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** — conserves flux on each cell.
- **Boundary conditions:** Inlet velocity (FIA = 50 m/s), outlet pressure = 0 gauge, ground = moving wall, car = no-slip, wheels = rotating wall.
- **Performance metrics:** $C_D$ (drag), $C_L$ (lift/negative = downforce), L/D ratio.
- **Aero force formula:** $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \, C \, A_{\text{ref}}$ — always pair dynamic pressure with coefficient and area.
- **Mesh quality gates:** Skewness < 0.85, $y^+ \approx 1$, orthogonal quality > 0.15.
- **Validation:** CFD results must be **correlated with wind-tunnel data** for confidence; grid-independence study is mandatory.
- **F1-specific features** needing careful CFD: ground-effect floor, diffuser, front-wing endplate vortices, tire wake, DRS open/closed state.
- **Sign convention:** Downforce is **negative lift**. Always interpret the sign.
- **Computational scale:** Modern F1 CFD consumes **> 100 million CPU-hours/season**, regulated by FIA's TAF.
- **FVM conservation form:** $\int_\Omega \frac{\partial(\rho\phi)}{\partial t} d\Omega + \oint_{\partial\Omega} \rho\mathbf{V}\phi\cdot d\mathbf{A} = \oint_{\partial\Omega} \Gamma_\phi \nabla\phi \cdot d\mathbf{A} + \int_\Omega S_\phi d\Omega$.
- **Pressure coefficient definition:** $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_\infty}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2}$.
- **Grid Convergence Index (GCI)** is the formal method to quantify numerical uncertainty.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sports Cars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sports Cars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sports Car Aerodynamics** is the specialised sub-discipline of vehicle aerodynamics concerned with the optimisation of the airflow around high-performance passenger cars designed primarily for superior acceleration, dynamic handling, and high maximum speeds. Unlike conventional passenger cars, where comfort and fuel economy dominate the design envelope, sports cars prioritise the dual objectives of **minimising aerodynamic drag ($C_D$)** and **maximising negative lift (downforce, $C_L$)** to enhance cornering grip, high-speed stability, and braking performance.

The governing philosophy can be mathematically expressed as the simultaneous pursuit of:

$$
C_D \rightarrow \text{minimum} \quad \text{and} \quad C_L \rightarrow \text{maximum negative (downforce)}
$$

subject to the practical constraints of cooling, visibility, ground clearance, and road-legal homologation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A sports car is officially classified by KTU (per the OEAUT723 syllabus) as a two-seater (or 2+2) high-performance automobile with an engine output typically exceeding **200 hp (150 kW)**, a 0–100 km/h capability under **6 seconds**, and a top speed in excess of **250 km/h**. These thresholds place the vehicle deep within the regime where aerodynamic forces become the dominant resistance mechanism, overtaking rolling resistance and gradient forces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an aircraft wing, but inverted and bolted to the roof of your car. That is essentially what a sports car rear wing does. While an aircraft wing lifts a plane upward, an inverted wing pushes the car *downward* onto the road.

Think of it like this:
- A **regular family sedan** is like a parachute flying through the air — lots of drag, minimal downforce, prioritising fuel economy.
- A **sports car** is like an inverted aircraft wing glued to a missile — it slices through air with minimum drag while being "sucked" toward the road for grip.
- The **underbody of a sports car** works like a vacuum cleaner — air rushes under the car at high speed, creating low pressure (via the Venturi effect) that pulls the car down (ground effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world observation:** Modern hypercars like the **Bugatti Chiron** ($C_D \approx 0.36$), **McLaren Speedtail** ($C_D \approx 0.28$), and **Koenigsegg Jesko** ($C_D \approx 0.28$ with active aero) demonstrate the extreme aerodynamic refinement possible. Notably, a Formula 1 car generates more downforce than its own weight at speeds above ~130 km/h — a feat no road car can match, but the principle is identical.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics for Sports Car Aerodynamics

| Metric | Typical Range | Significance |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Drag Coefficient ($C_D$) | 0.25 – 0.40 | Lower = better top speed & fuel economy |
| Lift Coefficient ($C_L$) | -0.5 to -2.0 (negative) | More negative = more downforce |
| Frontal Area ($A$) | 1.8 – 2.3 m² | Smaller = less drag (for same $C_D$) |
| Downforce-to-Drag Ratio ($L/D$) | 2 : 1 to 5 : 1 | Higher = more efficient aero |
| Downforce at 250 km/h | 100 – 500 kgf | Critical for high-speed cornering |
| Maximum Speed | 280 – 490 km/h | Aero drag dominates at these speeds |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the relationship between $C_D$, $C_L$, frontal area $A$, and dynamic pressure $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$. Memorise the numerical range of $C_D$ values for sports cars (≈0.28 to 0.38), as this is a common 3-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Aerodynamic Design Philosophy of Sports Cars

A sports car is governed by **three conflicting aerodynamic objectives**:

1. **Low Drag ($C_D$ ↓)** — Required for high top speed and reduced fuel consumption.
2. **High Downforce ($C_L$ ↓, i.e., more negative)** — Required for cornering grip and stability.
3. **Adequate Cooling** — Engines producing >400 hp require enormous airflow through radiators, brake ducts, and intercoolers.

These three goals are mutually antagonistic: a clean, low-drag shape usually generates little downforce, while a heavily-winged, high-downforce setup produces substantial drag. The art of sports car aerodynamics lies in **balancing these competing demands**.

### 2.2 Fundamental Operating Principles (Step-by-Step Logic)

#### Step 1: Drag Force and Its Consequences
- As velocity increases, drag force scales with the **square of velocity**.
- At **250 km/h (~69.4 m/s)**, aerodynamic drag exceeds rolling resistance by a factor of **~10**.
- Power required to overcome drag scales with the **cube of velocity** ($P_D \propto V^3$).

#### Step 2: Downforce Generation
- Generated by **inverted airfoils** (rear wings, front splitters) and **ground effect** (underbody venturis).
- Downforce scales linearly with normal load on the tyres.
- More vertical load = more friction available = higher cornering speeds possible.

#### Step 3: Aerodynamic Balance
- The position of the **centre of pressure** must align with (or be tuned relative to) the **centre of gravity**.
- Front-rear downforce distribution affects **understeer/oversteer** behaviour.
- A front-heavy downforce distribution induces **understeer** (stable but slow).
- A rear-heavy downforce distribution induces **oversteer** (fast but unstable).

#### Step 4: Ground Effect (Underbody Aerodynamics)
- A shaped underbody acts as a **Venturi channel**.
- Air accelerates beneath the car, pressure drops, and the car is "sucked" downward.
- Highly efficient — produces significant downforce with minimal drag penalty.

#### Step 5: Active Aerodynamics
- Modern sports cars use **moveable wings**, **active flaps**, and **ride-height adjustment**.
- At low speeds: wings flat (low drag).
- At high speeds / cornering: wings deployed (high downforce).
- Examples: **Ferrari 488 GTB**, **McLaren P1**, **Porsche 911 GT3 RS**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for OEAUT723 Module 4 — Sports Cars.** Master these for guaranteed marks in derivations and numericals.

| # | Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A C_D$ | Aerodynamic drag force | $F_D$ [N], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $V$ [m/s], $A$ [m²] |
| 2 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A C_L$ | Aerodynamic lift force | $F_L$ [N], $C_L$ dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_{DF} = -F_L$ | Downforce (negative lift) | $F_{DF}$ [N] |
| 4 | $P_D = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 A C_D$ | Power to overcome drag | $P_D$ [W] |
| 5 | $L/D = \tfrac{C_L}{C_D}$ (in magnitude) | Aero efficiency ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_a = \tfrac{C_L^{3/2}}{C_D}$ | Aerodynamic efficiency (cornering metric) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{max} = \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{2 P_{eng}}{\rho A C_D}}$ | Theoretical max speed | $P_{eng}$ [W] |
| 8 | $a_{lat,max} = \mu \left(g + \tfrac{F_{DF}}{m}\right)$ | Max lateral acceleration with downforce | $a_{lat}$ [m/s²], $\mu$ friction coeff. |
| 9 | $p_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = p_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$ | Bernoulli's equation (ground effect) | $p$ [Pa] |
| 10 | $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$ | Pressure drop under car | $\Delta p$ [Pa] |
| 11 | $F_{venturi} = \Delta p \cdot A_{under}$ | Ground-effect downforce | $F_{venturi}$ [N] |
| 12 | $Re = \tfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime check) | $Re$ dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Air Properties for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 1.789 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
> - Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of sports car aerodynamics have direct applications in:

- **Motorsport Engineering:** Formula 1, Le Mans Prototypes (LMP), GT3 racing.
- **Passenger Vehicle Performance:** M-Sport, AMG, M Division, RS — all use F1-derived tech.
- **Hyperloop and High-Speed Rail:** Aerodynamic drag dominates above 400 km/h.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Range Optimisation:** Even family EVs (Tesla Model S, Lucid Air) borrow low-drag shapes from sports cars.
- **Drone and UAV Design:** Same lift/drag trade-offs apply at miniature scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Maximum Speed, Engine Power, and Drag Coefficient

**Starting Premise:** At maximum velocity, the entire available engine power is consumed in overcoming aerodynamic drag (rolling resistance and gradient effects are negligible at $V_{max}$).

**Step 1: Define the equilibrium condition at maximum speed.**

At $V_{max}$, the tractive force from the engine equals the resistive aerodynamic drag force:

$$
F_{engine} = F_D
$$

**Step 2: Express power as the product of force and velocity.**

Power required to overcome drag:

$$
P_D = F_D \cdot V = \left(\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A C_D\right) \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 A C_D
$$

**Step 3: At terminal velocity, equate available engine power to drag power.**

$$
P_{engine} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{max}^3 A C_D
$$

**Step 4: Isolate $V_{max}$ algebraically.**

Divide both sides by $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_D$:

$$
V_{max}^3 = \tfrac{2 P_{engine}}{\rho A C_D}
$$

**Step 5: Take the cube root on both sides.**

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{2 P_{engine}}{\rho A C_D}}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to km/h by multiplying by 3.6.**

$$
V_{max} \, [\text{km/h}] = 3.6 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{2 P_{engine}}{\rho A C_D}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Maximum speed scales with the **cube root of power** and the **inverse cube root of $C_D$**. Doubling engine power increases $V_{max}$ by only **~26 %**, but halving $C_D$ also increases $V_{max}$ by **~26 %**. This is why reducing drag is **just as effective as adding power** for top-speed performance — a key KTU board-exam takeaway.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Downforce on Maximum Cornering Speed

**Starting Premise:** A sports car cornering on a flat curve experiences centripetal acceleration balanced by tyre friction. Adding downforce increases the normal load on the tyres and thus the available friction.

**Step 1: Write the equilibrium of centripetal force.**

$$
\tfrac{m v^2}{R} = F_{friction}
$$

where $R$ is the curve radius.

**Step 2: Apply Coulomb's friction law with normal force augmented by downforce.**

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \left(mg + F_{DF}\right)
$$

where $N$ is the total normal force on the tyres.

**Step 3: Substitute the downforce expression.**

$$
F_{DF} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A \vert C_L \vert
$$

Note: we use the magnitude of $C_L$ since downforce is negative lift.

**Step 4: Equate centripetal and friction forces.**

$$
\tfrac{m v^2}{R} = \mu \left(mg + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A \vert C_L \vert\right)
$$

**Step 5: Expand and collect terms in $v^2$.**

$$
\tfrac{m v^2}{R} = \mu m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\mu \rho v^2 A \vert C_L \vert
$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to isolate $v^2$ on one side.**

$$
\tfrac{m v^2}{R} - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu \rho v^2 A \vert C_L \vert = \mu m g
$$

**Step 7: Factor out $v^2$.**

$$
v^2 \left(\tfrac{m}{R} - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu \rho A \vert C_L \vert\right) = \mu m g
$$

**Step 8: Solve for the maximum cornering speed $v_{corner}$.**

$$
v_{corner} = \sqrt{\tfrac{\mu m g R}{m - \tfrac{1}{2}\mu \rho A R \vert C_L \vert}}
$$

**Step 9: Rewrite in the canonical form for clarity.**

$$
v_{corner} = \sqrt{\tfrac{\mu g R}{1 - \tfrac{\mu \rho A R \vert C_L \vert}{2m}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** As downforce ($\vert C_L \vert$) increases, the denominator shrinks, making the cornering speed **rise dramatically**. However, this relationship is **asymptotic** — at very high downforce, the denominator approaches zero and the car theoretically cornering infinitely fast (in practice, the tyres saturate first). This is the central design equation for wings and diffusers on race cars.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Ground Effect Downforce Using Bernoulli's Principle

**Starting Premise:** A sports car with a venturi-shaped underbody accelerates air in the throat, dropping pressure and creating suction.

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation between the upstream stagnation point and the throat.**

$$
p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_{throat} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{throat}^2
$$

**Step 2: Solve for the throat pressure $p_{throat}$.**

$$
p_{throat} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_\infty^2 - V_{throat}^2)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the pressure drop.**

$$
\Delta p = p_\infty - p_{throat} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{throat}^2 - V_\infty^2)
$$

**Step 4: Use continuity to relate $V_{throat}$ to $V_\infty$.**

For an underbody of area $A_u$ (throat) and free-stream area $A_\infty$:

$$
A_\infty V_\infty = A_u V_{throat} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{throat} = V_\infty \cdot \tfrac{A_\infty}{A_u}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the velocity relation back into $\Delta p$.**

$$
\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \left[\left(\tfrac{A_\infty}{A_u}\right)^2 - 1\right]
$$

**Step 6: Compute the downforce by multiplying pressure drop by underbody area.**

$$
F_{DF,\,ground} = \Delta p \cdot A_u = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 A_u \left[\left(\tfrac{A_\infty}{A_u}\right)^2 - 1\right]
$$

**Step 7: Simplify the geometric term by defining the area ratio $k = A_u / A_\infty$.**

$$
F_{DF,\,ground} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 A_\infty \left(\tfrac{1}{k} - k\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** For a typical sports car with $k \approx 0.6$ (40% contraction), the term $(1/k - k) \approx 1.07$. Thus the ground effect produces downforce equivalent to about a body of frontal area $A_\infty$ but with the *coefficient* $(1/k - k) \approx 1.07$. A typical F1 car has $k \approx 0.3$, yielding $(1/k - k) \approx 3.03$ — explaining why F1 ground effect is **extraordinarily potent**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Aero Components of Sports Cars

| Component | Primary Function | Downforce Mechanism | Drag Penalty | Typical Location |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Rear Wing** | High-speed stability | Inverted airfoil (pressure differential) | High (0.05 – 0.15 added to $C_D$) | Rear deck |
| **Front Splitter** | Front-axle grip, balance | High-pressure air dam + low-pressure wake | Low | Front bumper underside |
| **Diffuser** | Rear underbody downforce | Gradual expansion → pressure recovery | Very low (sometimes *reduces* $C_D$) | Rear underbody |
| **Side Skirts** | Sealed underbody | Prevent lateral air leakage into venturi | Negligible | Sill sides |
| **Vortex Generators** | Energy injection to boundary layer | Trip turbulent BL → delay separation | Negative (reduce $C_D$) | Roof, rear window edges |
| **NACA Ducts** | Brake/engine cooling | Internal ducting, low disturbance | Very low | Bonnet, rear quarters |
| **Active Rear Wing** | Variable downforce | Same as fixed wing, deployable | Variable (zero at low speed) | Rear deck |
| **Front Canards** | Front downforce | Small inverted airfoils | Moderate | Front bumper edges |
| **Rear Louvers** | Heat extraction | Internal pressure release | Negligible | Rear decklid |
| **Boat-tail** | Drag reduction | Tapering shape | **Reduces $C_D$ by 0.05+** | Rear taper |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sports Car Aerodynamic System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of aerodynamic forces, components, and vehicle dynamics for a sports car. It serves as the architectural blueprint for understanding how each subsystem interacts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Free Stream Air V infinity] --> A1[Vehicle Front Bumper]
    A1 --> A2[Front Splitter and Canard]
    A2 --> A3[Bonnet and Windscreen Flow]
    A3 --> A4[Roof and NACA Duct]
    A4 --> A5[Rear Window and Vortex Generators]
    A5 --> A6[Rear Wing Active Aero]
    A5 --> A7[Boat Tail Rear Body]
    A6 --> A8[Diffuser Outlet]
    A7 --> A8[Diffuser Outlet]
    A2 --> A9[Underbody Venturi Throat]
    A9 --> A8[Diffuser Outlet]
    A8 --> A10[Wake Region]

    subgraph FORCES[Aerodynamic Force Generation]
        F1[Drag Force F sub D] --> F3[Net Longitudinal Resistance]
        F2[Downforce F sub DF] --> F4[Vertical Load on Tyres]
    end

    A1 --> F1
    A6 --> F2
    A9 --> F2
    F3 --> F5[Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics]
    F4 --> F6[Vehicle Lateral Dynamics Cornering]
    F4 --> F7[Braking Performance]
    F5 --> F8[Performance Output]
    F6 --> F8[Performance Output]
    F7 --> F8[Performance Output]

    subgraph BALANCE[Aerodynamic Balance Control]
        B1[Front Downforce Fraction] --> B2[Understeer Oversteer Tuning]
        B3[Rear Downforce Fraction] --> B2
        B2 --> F8[Performance Output]
    end

    F2 --> B1
    F2 --> B3

    A6 -. Active Deployment .-> A6
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Sports Car Aero Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SPORTSCAR[Sports Car Aerodynamic System]
        subgraph FOREGEN[Downforce Generation]
            P1[Rear Wing]
            P2[Front Splitter]
            P3[Front Canards]

            P5[Side Skirts]
        end

        subgraph DRAGRED[Drag Reduction]
            Q1[Smooth Body Panels]

        end

        subgraph COOLSYS[Cooling Subsystem]
            R1[Front Brake Ducts]
            R2[Engine Radiator Inlet]
            R3[Intercooler Ducts]

        end

        subgraph ACTIVE[Active Aerodynamics]
            S1[DRS Active Wing]
            S2[Adaptive Front Splitter]

        end
    end

    FOREGEN --> OUT1[Vehicle Stability and Grip]
    DRAGRED --> OUT2[Top Speed and Efficiency]

    OUT3 --> PERF
    OUT4 --> PERF
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Aero Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B -- Track Dominant --> C[Maximise Downforce High Drag Acceptable]
    B -- Road Dominant --> D[Balance Drag and Downforce]

    C --> F[Fixed Large Wing]
    C --> G[Aggressive Diffuser]
    C --> H[Front Splitter with Canards]

    D --> I[Active Rear Wing]

    E --> L[Retracted Wing or None]
    E --> M[Long Boat Tail]
    E --> N[Flush Underbody Panels]

    F --> OUT[Final Aero Package]
```
