
---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "downforce" in the context of sports car aerodynamics. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**
Downforce is the **vertical aerodynamic force directed downward** generated by the airflow over and under a sports car. It results from a **negative lift coefficient ($C_L < 0$)** produced by inverted airfoils (wings, splitters) and the ground-effect venturi under the floor.

**Importance:**
- Increases the **normal load on the tyres**, thereby increasing available friction.
- Improves **cornering grip**, **braking performance**, and **high-speed stability**.
- Allows the car to corner at significantly **higher speeds** without losing traction.
- For example, a typical sports car generating 300 kgf of downforce at 250 km/h effectively increases its vertical load by ~30%, raising cornering capability proportionally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for importance/elaboration.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: List any four aerodynamic components used on sports cars and state their primary function.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Primary Function |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Rear Wing** | Generates downforce at the rear axle for high-speed stability |
| 2 | **Front Splitter** | Creates a high-pressure blockage in front and a low-pressure zone behind; provides front downforce |
| 3 | **Diffuser** | Expands and decelerates underbody air, recovering pressure and producing rear downforce |
| 4 | **Side Skirts** | Seals the underbody from lateral air leakage, preserving venturi efficiency |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: With neat sketches and relevant equations, explain in detail the various aerodynamic considerations involved in the design of sports cars. Discuss the role of rear wings, front splitters, diffusers, and ground effect in detail.**

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]: Explain the drag and downforce equations, and discuss the role of rear wings and front splitters.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Fundamental Aerodynamic Forces on a Sports Car**

The two primary aerodynamic forces acting on a sports car are:

**Drag force** (longitudinal, opposing motion):

$$
F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_D
$$

**Lift/Downforce** (vertical):

$$
F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_L
$$

For a sports car, $C_L$ is **negative**, producing **downforce** $F_{DF} = -F_L$.

**2. Role of Rear Wings**

A rear wing is an **inverted airfoil** mounted above the rear deck. By inverting the airfoil, the pressure differential that would normally lift an aircraft now pushes the car downward.

- At the **upper surface**, air flows faster → lower pressure (suction).
- At the **lower surface**, air flows slower → higher pressure.
- The pressure difference creates a net **downward** force.

The downforce contribution from a rear wing:

$$
F_{wing} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_{wing} C_{L,wing}
$$

Rear wings also generate drag:

$$
D_{wing} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_{wing} C_{D,wing}
$$

A good wing maximises the ratio $C_L / C_D$ (lift-to-drag efficiency), achieved by **high aspect ratio** (long and narrow) and **airfoil-shaped cross-section**.

**3. Role of Front Splitters**

A front splitter is a **horizontal flat plate** extending forward from the front bumper at the car's floor level. It works by:

- **Blocking high-pressure air** from spilling under the car.
- Creating a **high-pressure stagnation zone** above the splitter (which pushes the splitter and thus the front of the car **down**).
- Inducing a **low-pressure wake** behind it, contributing additional suction.

The downforce from a splitter scales with:

$$
F_{splitter} \propto \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_{splitter} \Delta C_p
$$

where $\Delta C_p$ is the pressure coefficient difference across the splitter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Always pair the splitter with **side skirts** to maintain a sealed underbody and prevent the splitter's high-pressure zone from "leaking" sideways.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]: Explain ground effect using Bernoulli's principle and derive an expression for the underbody downforce.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Ground Effect**

Ground effect utilises the **entire underside of the car** as an aerodynamic device. The underbody is shaped as a **converging-diverging venturi duct**:

- Air enters at the front at low velocity $V_\infty$.
- The throat (narrowest section) accelerates the air to $V_{throat}$.
- The diffuser at the rear gradually expands and recovers pressure.

**2. Bernoulli's Equation Applied**

Between the upstream flow (just below the splitter) and the throat:

$$
p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 = p_{throat} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{throat}^2
$$

**Step 1: Solve for throat pressure.**

$$
p_{throat} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_\infty^2 - V_{throat}^2)
$$

**Step 2: Pressure drop across the throat.**

$$
\Delta p = p_\infty - p_{throat} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (V_{throat}^2 - V_\infty^2)
$$

**Step 3: Apply continuity equation.** Let $A_\infty$ be the free-stream cross-section (approximated by the car's frontal underbody area) and $A_u$ be the throat area.

$$
A_\infty V_\infty = A_u V_{throat} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{throat} = V_\infty \cdot \tfrac{A_\infty}{A_u}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into pressure drop.**

$$
\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \left[\left(\tfrac{A_\infty}{A_u}\right)^2 - 1\right]
$$

**Step 5: Multiply pressure drop by the throat area to obtain ground-effect downforce.**

$$
F_{DF,\,ground} = \Delta p \cdot A_u = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 A_u \left[\left(\tfrac{A_\infty}{A_u}\right)^2 - 1\right]
$$

**Step 6: Define area ratio $k = A_u / A_\infty$.**

$$
F_{DF,\,ground} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 A_\infty \left(\tfrac{1}{k} - k\right)
$$

**3. Numerical Interpretation**

For a sports car with $k = 0.6$ at $V_\infty = 70$ m/s (≈252 km/h):

$$
F_{DF} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(70)^2 (1.8) \left(\tfrac{1}{0.6} - 0.6\right)
$$

$$
F_{DF} = 0.6125 \cdot 4900 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 1.067
$$

$$
F_{DF} \approx 5757 \text{ N} \approx 587 \text{ kgf}
$$

This single calculation demonstrates why ground effect is **comparable in magnitude to a rear wing**, yet with **far less drag penalty**.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: A sports car with frontal area $A = 2.0 \, m^2$, drag coefficient $C_D = 0.32$, and lift coefficient $C_L = -0.8$ is travelling at $V = 240 \, km/h$. Calculate:**
**(a)** The drag force and the downforce acting on the car.
**(b)** The power required to overcome drag and the downforce-to-drag ratio. Comment on the aerodynamic efficiency of the car.

**Given Data:**
- $A = 2.0 \, m^2$
- $C_D = 0.32$
- $C_L = -0.8$ (negative ⇒ downforce)
- $V = 240 \, km/h = 240 / 3.6 = 66.67 \, m/s$
- $\rho = 1.225 \, kg/m^3$

---

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]: Drag force and downforce.**

**Step 1: Compute dynamic pressure.**

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(66.67)^2
$$

$$
q = 0.6125 \cdot 4444.4 = 2722.2 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute drag force.**

$$
F_D = q \cdot A \cdot C_D = 2722.2 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.32
$$

$$
F_D = 1742.2 \text{ N} \approx 1.74 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Compute downforce.**

The lift force is:

$$
F_L = q \cdot A \cdot C_L = 2722.2 \cdot 2.0 \cdot (-0.8) = -4355.5 \text{ N}
$$

The **downforce** is the negative of lift:

$$
F_{DF} = -F_L = +4355.5 \text{ N} \approx 4.36 \text{ kN}
$$

Converting to kgf (divide by $g = 9.81$):

$$
F_{DF} \approx 444 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 240 km/h, the car generates **~444 kgf of downforce** — enough to press the car onto the road with the equivalent weight of two average adult passengers.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]: Power required and aerodynamic efficiency.**

**Step 1: Power to overcome drag.**

$$
P_D = F_D \cdot V = 1742.2 \cdot 66.67
$$

$$
P_D = 116{,}146 \, W \approx 116.1 \, kW \approx 155.7 \, hp
$$

**Step 2: Downforce-to-Drag ratio.**

$$
L/D = \tfrac{\vert F_{DF} \vert}{F_D} = \tfrac{4355.5}{1742.2} \approx 2.50
$$

**Step 3: Aerodynamic efficiency coefficient.**

$$
\eta_a = \tfrac{\vert C_L \vert^{3/2}}{C_D} = \tfrac{(0.8)^{1.5}}{0.32} = \tfrac{0.7155}{0.32} \approx 2.236
$$

**Comments on aerodynamic efficiency:**
- An **L/D ratio of 2.5** is **good** for a sports car (passenger cars typically achieve 0.5 to 1.5).
- An aerodynamic efficiency $\eta_a$ above **2.0** indicates the car is well-tuned for **track-oriented cornering performance**.
- However, the $C_D = 0.32$ is moderate; hypercars push this down to **0.25** for top-speed runs.
- The car is **drag-limited** at $V_{max}$ unless additional power is provided.

**Step 4: Theoretical maximum speed (assuming 400 hp engine).**

$$
P_{engine} = 400 \, hp \times 745.7 = 298{,}280 \, W
$$

$$
V_{max} = \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{2 \cdot 298{,}280}{1.225 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.32}} = \sqrt[3]{761{,}224} \approx 91.2 \, m/s
$$

$$
V_{max} \approx 328 \, km/h
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert km/h to m/s is the **#1 cause of lost marks**. Always convert $V$ to **m/s** before plugging into the standard formula. 1 km/h = 1/3.6 m/s.
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Lift Coefficient:** Students often report downforce as negative. Remember: **downforce magnitude is positive**, but the **lift coefficient is negative**.
> 3. **Confusing Drag Power with Engine Power:** The drag power $P_D = F_D \cdot V$ is the power **dissipated against air**, not the total engine power. Include drivetrain and rolling losses for a full energy balance.
> 4. **Forgetting Air Density:** Default to $\rho = 1.225 \, kg/m^3$ unless the problem specifies otherwise. Using $\rho = 1.2$ is acceptable but **must be stated explicitly**.
> 5. **Skipping the Interpretation Step:** Numerical answers without engineering interpretation lose 1–2 marks. Always close with a brief comment on what the result means practically.
> 6. **Mermaid Diagram Errors in Sketch Questions:** If the question asks for a "neat sketch," a labelled Mermaid block diagram or a clearly annotated ASCII representation is acceptable. Always label: free-stream, stagnation, diffuser, splitter, wake, and indicate flow direction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sports Cars (KTU OEAUT723 Module 4):**

- **Sports car $C_D$** typically lies in the range **0.25 – 0.40**; $C_L$ is **negative** (downforce).
- **Drag force** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_D$; scales with **$V^2$**.
- **Downforce** $F_{DF} = -F_L$; scales with **$V^2$**.
- **Drag power** $P_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^3 A C_D$; scales with **$V^3$** (most critical for top speed).
- **Maximum speed** $V_{max} = \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{2 P_{engine}}{\rho A C_D}}$ — derived assuming all power goes to drag.
- **Aerodynamic efficiency** $\eta_a = \tfrac{|C_L|^{3/2}}{C_D}$ — critical for cornering performance.
- **L/D ratio** quantifies how much downforce is obtained per unit of drag penalty (higher = better for tracks).
- **Rear wing** = inverted airfoil, generates downforce via pressure differential.
- **Front splitter** = horizontal plate, creates high-pressure front and low-pressure wake.
- **Diffuser** = diverging channel at rear underbody, recovers pressure, pulls car down (ground effect).
- **Side skirts** = essential to seal underbody and preserve venturi efficiency.
- **Ground effect** is governed by Bernoulli: $\Delta p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(V_{throat}^2 - V_\infty^2)$.
- **Active aero** wings deploy at high speed / cornering; retract for top-speed runs (e.g., Ferrari 488, McLaren P1).
- **Cooling trade-off:** Engine, brakes, and intercoolers demand airflow that **increases $C_D$** — a key design tension.
- **Aerodynamic balance:** Front-rear downforce split affects understeer/oversteer behaviour.
- **Air density:** $\rho = 1.225 \, kg/m^3$ at sea level, ISA conditions.
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.
- **Typical downforce at 250 km/h:** 100–500 kgf for road-going sports cars; >1000 kgf for race cars.
- **Aspect ratio matters:** Long, narrow wings produce more downforce per unit drag than short, stubby ones.
- **Form drag vs induced drag:** At low speed, induced drag dominates (wings); at high speed, form drag dominates (body shape).
- **Reynolds number check:** At 250 km/h with $L = 4 \, m$, $Re \approx 2 \times 10^7$ — fully turbulent flow regime.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SUVs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAUT723 - VEHICLE AERODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SUVs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of SUVs in Vehicle Aerodynamics

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sports Utility Vehicle (SUV)** is a high-riding, light-duty utility vehicle classification characterized by a tall greenhouse, large frontal area, raised center of gravity, and a generally bluff (boxy) body shape. From an aerodynamics standpoint, the SUV is a critical case study because it violates most of the streamlined design rules that govern passenger cars. According to KTU 2024 syllabus (OEAUT723, Module 4), SUVs represent a class where **drag**, **lift**, **pitching moment**, and **crosswind stability** are all simultaneously challenged, making them a benchmark problem in road vehicle aerodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – SUVs):**
> Students are expected to understand why SUVs have a higher drag coefficient (C_D typically **0.35 – 0.45**) than sedans (**0.25 – 0.30**), the role of the large **frontal area (A)** typically between **2.4 – 3.0 m²**, the flow physics around the A-pillars, roof, and rear end, and the engineering solutions used to mitigate these penalties.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine pushing a **bricks-shaped object** through a swimming pool versus pushing a **fish-shaped object**. The brick (SUV) will push a huge wall of water, create a big wake, and tip forward; the fish (sedan) slices through. The SUV behaves like the brick — its blunt nose, flat roof, and vertical tailgate do not "slice" the air but rather punch it, push it up over the roof, and slam it down at the back, creating enormous turbulence and pressure drag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> An SUV is aerodynamically inefficient because it is tall, flat-faced, and squared-off. Air hates to flow around sharp edges and wide standoffs — every inch of bluff geometry on an SUV is a "tax" paid at the fuel pump.

## 1.3 Defining Physical Metrics of an SUV

| Parameter | Typical SUV Range | Sedan Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Frontal Area, **A** | **2.4 – 3.0 m²** | 2.0 – 2.3 m² |
| Drag Coefficient, **C_D** | **0.35 – 0.45** | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Height-to-Width Ratio | ~0.85 – 1.0 | 0.45 – 0.55 |
| Center of Gravity Height | ~700 – 800 mm | ~500 – 550 mm |
| Underbody Clearance | 180 – 220 mm | 120 – 150 mm |
| Rear-End Inclination Angle | 90° (vertical tailgate) | 20° – 30° (sloped) |

The constant for air density at standard conditions is **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why SUVs Are Aerodynamically Punished — Structured Logic

- **Step 1 — Frontal Area Penalty:** SUVs sit higher and are wider than sedans. The projected frontal area is the geometric face presented to the oncoming airstream, so the drag force scales linearly with A. A 30% larger frontal area yields ~30% more drag for the same C_D.
- **Step 2 — Bluff Body Geometry:** The squared-off nose, vertical windshield, flat roof, and vertical rear produce massive **pressure drag** due to flow separation at sharp edges. The flow cannot follow the contour, leading to broad separated regions and low-pressure wakes.
- **Step 3 — Unfavorable A-Pillar Flow:** Tall A-pillars generate strong **vortices** (A-pillar vortices) that shed into the side windows, increasing local drag and contributing to **wind noise**.
- **Step 4 — Rear-End Separation:** The vertical tailgate is the *single largest* drag contributor on an SUV. The flow cannot stay attached, producing a massive low-pressure wake (sometimes called the **base wake**).
- **Step 5 — High Lift & Lift Imbalance:** The bluff nose and flat roof generate significant **front lift** and **rear lift** simultaneously. This reduces steering feel at high speed and increases fuel consumption.
- **Step 6 — High Center of Gravity:** SUV C.G. is higher, making **crosswind stability** and **yaw sensitivity** dramatically worse than in low-slung sedans.
- **Step 7 — Underbody Exposure:** SUVs have large **ride heights**, so the underbody is exposed to free-stream air rather than being a managed diffuser channel. This adds **underbody drag** and disrupts rear flow.

## 2.2 Engineering Real-World Utility

Aerodynamic optimization of SUVs is not academic — it directly translates to:
- **Fuel economy:** At 100 km/h, roughly **50–60%** of an ICE SUV's energy goes into overcoming drag.
- **EV Range:** For electric SUVs (e.g., modern battery SUVs), every 0.01 drop in C_D can add 2–4 km of range.
- **High-speed stability:** Reduces driver fatigue and risk on highways.
- **Wind noise & NVH:** Aero sources contribute up to 50% of cabin noise at highway speed.
- **Cooling:** SUVs with large engines/transmissions need carefully designed cooling packages.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_D\,A$ | Aerodynamic drag force [N]; $\rho$ [kg/m³], $V$ [m/s], $C_D$ [-], $A$ [m²] |
| 2 | $C_D = \dfrac{F_D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A}$ | Drag coefficient definition [-] |
| 3 | $C_L = \dfrac{F_L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A}$ | Lift coefficient definition [-] |
| 4 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_L\,A$ | Lift force [N] |
| 5 | $A = W \times H$ | Frontal area approx. (W width × H height) [m²] |
| 6 | $P_D = F_D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{3}\,C_D\,A$ | Aerodynamic power loss [W] |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,V\,L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number [-] |
| 8 | $C_{Dp} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{front}}\right)^{2}$ | Pressure drag estimate (Rosby approx.) for bluff bodies |
| 9 | $M_{pitch} = F_{front} \cdot x_{front} - F_{rear} \cdot x_{rear}$ | Pitching moment about C.G. [N·m] |
| 10 | $a_{yaw} = \dfrac{C_{S\beta}}{m\,V}$ | Yaw rate gain in crosswind; $C_{S\beta}$ = side-force derivative |
| 11 | $C_{D,\text{air}} = C_{D0} + k\,C_L^{2}$ | Combined drag polar — interference term $k$ |
| 12 | $\Delta F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A\,(C_{D2} - C_{D1})$ | Drag reduction from a C_D improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to memorize:** $\rho_{\text{air,ISA}} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\mu_{\text{air}} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$ at 15 °C sea level.

## 2.4 The C_D × A Drag Area Concept (KTU-Favorite)

Because drag force is proportional to $C_D \cdot A$, engineers talk about **drag area** $A_D = C_D \cdot A$ in square meters. This is a metric that already folds in both the shape inefficiency and the frontal area penalty of an SUV. Modern SUVs target $A_D < 0.90$ m²; older SUVs exceed 1.10 m².

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drag Force on an SUV at Highway Speed

**Given:** A mid-size SUV with $C_D = 0.40$, frontal area $A = 2.7 \text{ m}^2$, cruising at $V = 120 \text{ km/h}$, air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Compute the drag force and the aerodynamic power loss.

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units:**

$$V = 120 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{3600} = 33.33 \text{ m/s}$$

The conversion factor 1000/3600 = 1/3.6 is used to convert km/h to m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute the dynamic pressure term $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$:**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (33.33)^{2}$$

$$= 0.6125 \times 1110.89 = 680.42 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the drag equation:**

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,C_D\,A = 680.42 \times 0.40 \times 2.7$$

$$F_D = 680.42 \times 1.08 = 734.85 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute aerodynamic power loss $P_D = F_D \cdot V$:**

$$P_D = 734.85 \times 33.33 = 24{,}494 \text{ W} \approx 24.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{In horsepower: } P_D = \dfrac{24{,}494}{745.7} \approx 32.85 \text{ hp}$$

This is the power the engine must deliver just to push the SUV through the air at 120 km/h — a sobering number, and it is why every reduction in $C_D$ matters.

## 3.2 Derivation: Drag Reduction by Lowering C_D

**Problem:** The same SUV is redesigned with a C_D drop from 0.40 to 0.34 (a 15% improvement). How much drag force and power are saved at the same 120 km/h cruise?

**Step 1 — Compute the new drag force:**

$$F_{D2} = 680.42 \times 0.34 \times 2.7 = 680.42 \times 0.918 = 624.62 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute drag force saved:**

$$\Delta F_D = F_{D1} - F_{D2} = 734.85 - 624.62 = 110.23 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta F_D}{F_{D1}} = \dfrac{110.23}{734.85} = 0.1500 = 15.00\%$$

Because dynamic pressure and A are unchanged, the percentage force reduction is *exactly* the percentage $C_D$ reduction.

**Step 4 — Power saved:**

$$\Delta P_D = \Delta F_D \times V = 110.23 \times 33.33 = 3674 \text{ W} \approx 3.67 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Fuel saving at cruise: } \dfrac{3.67}{24.5} \times 100\% \approx 15\% \text{ less aerodynamic energy}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Pitching Moment Due to Front & Rear Lift Imbalance

**Problem:** An SUV at 100 km/h has $C_{L,front} = 0.10$ acting on the projected area of the nose at a moment arm of 1.4 m forward of the C.G., and $C_{L,rear} = 0.15$ on the rear at 1.6 m aft of the C.G. The combined reference area is 2.7 m². Find the net pitching moment and interpret.

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:**

$$V = 100 \text{ km/h} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic pressure:**

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 27.78^{2} = 0.6125 \times 771.6 = 472.6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Front lift force:**

$$F_{L,f} = q \cdot C_{L,f} \cdot A = 472.6 \times 0.10 \times 2.7 = 127.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Rear lift force:**

$$F_{L,r} = q \cdot C_{L,r} \cdot A = 472.6 \times 0.15 \times 2.7 = 191.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Net pitching moment (nose-up positive):**

$$M_{pitch} = F_{L,f} \cdot x_f - F_{L,r} \cdot x_r$$

$$M_{pitch} = (127.6 \times 1.4) - (191.4 \times 1.6)$$

$$M_{pitch} = 178.6 - 306.2 = -127.6 \text{ N·m}$$

The negative sign means the rear lifts *more* than the front — the SUV develops a **nose-down pitching tendency** at speed. This is a common SUV issue: it loads the front tires (good for steering) but reduces rear grip, increasing oversteer risk in transient maneuvers.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SUV vs Sedan at 100 km/h

| Metric | Sedan | SUV | SUV Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_D$ | 0.27 | 0.40 | +0.13 |
| $A$ (m²) | 2.15 | 2.70 | +25.6% |
| $A_D = C_D \cdot A$ (m²) | 0.581 | 1.080 | +85.9% |
| $F_D$ (N) | 395.2 | 734.7 | +85.9% |
| $P_D$ (kW) | 10.97 | 20.41 | +85.9% |

This table is the *single most important analytical comparison* in KTU Module 4 — the SUV pays nearly **double** the aerodynamic penalty of a comparable sedan at the same speed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Aerodynamic Flow Architecture Around an SUV

The following block-level functional flow captures the principal aerodynamic regions and force vectors acting on an SUV body. It is intentionally designed as a functional architecture (since the Mermaid renderer cannot depict a physical sketch faithfully).

```mermaid
graph TD
    INLET[Free Stream Air V inf] --> NOSE[A Pillar Vortices]
    NOSE --> HOOD[Under Hood Pressure Field]
    HOOD --> WINDSHIELD[Sloped Windshield Stagnation]
    WINDSHIELD --> ROOF[Flat Roof Boundary Layer]
    ROOF --> CPOST[C Pillar Separation Edge]
    CPOST --> TAIL[Vertical Tailgate Base Wake]
    TAIL --> UNDER[Exposed Underbody Flow]
    UNDER --> REARDIF[Rear Diffuser Region]
    TAIL --> SIDEA[Side Window Recirculation]
    NOSE --> SIDEA
    ROOF --> LIFTGEN[Lift Generation Front and Rear]
    LIFTGEN --> PITCH[Pitching Moment About CG]
    PITCH --> YAW[Crosswind Yaw Sensitivity High]
    YAW --> STAB[Stability Concern]
    LIFTGEN --> DRAG[Total Drag Force Fd]
    UNDER --> DRAG
    TAIL --> DRAG
    NOSE --> DRAG
    DRAG --> ENERGY[Aerodynamic Power Loss kW]
    ENERGY --> FUEL[Reduced Fuel Economy or EV Range]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — SUV Aerodynamic Optimization Strategies

This second diagram maps the **engineering countermeasures** typically applied to SUV bodies to recover aerodynamic performance.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PROB[High Cd and Lift on SUV] --> A1[Lower Nose and Grill Shutters]
    PROB --> A2[Raked A Pillars and Camouflaged Seams]
    PROB --> A3[Sloped Roof and Spoiler Lip]
    PROB --> A4[Aero Optimized Tailgate and Kammback Shape]
    PROB --> A5[Underbody Flat Panels and Diffuser]
    PROB --> A6[Active Side Mirror and Wheel Arch Liner]
    PROB --> A7[Lowered Ride Height at Speed]
    A1 --> OUT1[Reduced Front Lift and Cooling Drag]
    A2 --> OUT2[Reduced A Pillar Vortex Strength]
    A3 --> OUT3[Delayed Roof Separation]
    A4 --> OUT4[Smaller Base Wake and Lower Cd]
    A5 --> OUT5[Managed Underbody Flow]
    A6 --> OUT6[Lower Side Drag and Wheel Wake]
    A7 --> OUT7[Lower Frontal Area and Lift]
    OUT1 --> NET[Net Drag Area Reduction]
    OUT2 --> NET
    OUT3 --> NET
    OUT4 --> NET
    OUT5 --> NET
    OUT6 --> NET
    OUT7 --> NET
    NET --> RESULT[Improved Range Stability and NVH]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Pressure-Distribution Sketch (Textual)

Although the Mermaid engine cannot render a pressure cloud, the following textual schematic summarizes the typical pressure distribution over an SUV silhouette:

| Region | Local C_p (approx.) | Physical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Front grille & bumper | +0.80 to +0.90 | Stagnation of incoming flow |
| Hood front | +0.20 to +0.40 | Curvature over nose |
| Windshield base | +0.10 to +0.20 | Acceleration up the slope |
| Roof mid | –0.40 to –0.60 | Accelerated flow on flat top |
| Roof rear edge | –0.80 to –0.90 | **Primary separation point** |
| Tailgate face | –0.30 to –0.50 | Low-pressure base wake |
| Underbody | –0.20 to –0.50 | Suction under flat floor |
| Rear bumper face | –0.10 to –0.20 | Partial recovery |

> [!NOTE]
> The single largest *negative* pressure coefficient is at the **roof rear edge** — this is where the flow detaches. Modern SUVs use **roof spoilers, tailgate lips, and Kammback truncation** to control this separation and recover several counts of C_D.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Why do SUVs have a higher drag coefficient than sedans? List any three physical reasons.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Blunt, vertical rear end:** A vertical tailgate causes abrupt flow separation, generating a large low-pressure base wake and high pressure drag.
2. **Tall, flat body:** The greater height and flat roof promote early flow separation at the roof's trailing edge and create stronger A-pillar vortices.
3. **Larger frontal area:** The SUV's taller and wider nose presents a larger projected area, multiplying the drag force even if $C_D$ were identical.
4. **High ride height:** Exposes the underbody to free-stream air, adding underbody drag that a low sedan avoids.

*(Any three of the above earn full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. Define the term "drag area" ($A_D$) used in vehicle aerodynamics. What is the typical $A_D$ target for a modern SUV?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **drag area** is the product of the drag coefficient and the frontal area:

$$A_D = C_D \cdot A \quad [\text{m}^2]$$

It is a single figure of merit that captures both the shape inefficiency ($C_D$) and the size penalty ($A$) of a vehicle. A typical modern SUV targets $A_D \leq 0.90 \text{ m}^2$, while older or off-road-oriented SUVs may exceed $1.10 \text{ m}^2$. This metric is widely used because drag force at constant speed is $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A_D$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Aerodynamic Drag Analysis of an SUV

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the drag force on an SUV of $C_D = 0.42$, frontal area $A = 2.8 \text{ m}^2$, cruising at $V = 110 \text{ km/h}$. Take $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Also compute the aerodynamic power loss.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* If the SUV is redesigned with a tapered rear (Kammback) and active grille shutters, reducing the effective $C_D$ to 0.34, calculate the percentage drag force reduction and the corresponding fuel-energy saving per hour at cruise (assuming engine thermal efficiency of 30%).

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:**

$$V = 110 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{110}{3.6} = 30.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic pressure:**

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (30.56)^{2} = 0.6125 \times 933.91 = 572.0 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Drag force:**

$$F_D = q \cdot C_D \cdot A = 572.0 \times 0.42 \times 2.8 = 572.0 \times 1.176 = 672.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Power loss:**

$$P_D = F_D \cdot V = 672.7 \times 30.56 = 20{,}560 \text{ W} \approx 20.56 \text{ kW}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — New drag force:**

$$F_{D2} = 572.0 \times 0.34 \times 2.8 = 572.0 \times 0.952 = 544.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Force reduction:**

$$\Delta F_D = 672.7 - 544.5 = 128.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage:**

$$\dfrac{128.2}{672.7} \times 100\% = 19.06\%$$

**Step 4 — Power saving:**

$$\Delta P = 128.2 \times 30.56 = 3917.8 \text{ W} = 3.918 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Fuel energy per hour:**

$$E_{saved} = \Delta P \times 3600 = 3917.8 \times 3600 = 14.10 \text{ MJ/hour}$$

**Step 6 — Actual fuel energy (account for 30% efficiency):**

$$E_{fuel} = \dfrac{14.10}{0.30} = 47.0 \text{ MJ/hour}$$

**Step 7 — In litres of petrol** (calorific value ~34 MJ/L):

$$\text{Fuel} = \dfrac{47.0}{34} \approx 1.38 \text{ L/hour saved at cruise}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B – 14 marks):**
> • Do **not** forget to convert km/h to m/s (÷ 3.6). This is the #1 mistake.
> • Do **not** confuse *power lost to drag* (in watts) with *fuel energy consumed* (which must include engine thermal efficiency).
> • State units at every step; missing units cost 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
> • Always write the drag equation symbolically *before* substituting numbers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Lift, Pitching Moment, and Stability of an SUV (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define lift coefficient and pitching moment for a road vehicle. With reference to an SUV, explain why lift is a more critical issue than in a sedan, and how it affects high-speed stability.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An SUV is traveling at $V = 130 \text{ km/h}$. The front axle is 1.45 m ahead of the C.G. and the rear axle 1.65 m behind. The front lift coefficient is $C_{L,f} = 0.12$ and the rear is $C_{L,r} = 0.18$ on a reference area of 2.6 m². Compute the front lift force, rear lift force, and the net pitching moment. Comment on the resulting handling behavior.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition — Lift coefficient:** The non-dimensional ratio of lift force to dynamic pressure and reference area:

$$C_L = \dfrac{F_L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A}$$

**Definition — Pitching moment:** The moment about the C.G. caused by aerodynamic forces acting at distances from the C.G.; a nose-up moment is conventionally positive:

$$M_{pitch} = F_{L,f} \cdot x_f - F_{L,r} \cdot x_r$$

**SUV vs Sedan discussion (any 4 points):**
- SUV has 25–40% larger frontal area, so a given $C_L$ produces far more lift force.
- The bluff nose and roof produce strong front and rear lift simultaneously.
- The high center of gravity amplifies the *effect* of lift on handling — even modest lift can change tire loading.
- The vertical tailgate creates a strong rear low-pressure zone, increasing $C_{L,r}$.
- Sedans use sloped rears and diffusers to control lift, which SUVs cannot easily adopt.
- Front lift reduces steering grip; rear lift reduces traction and braking stability.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Velocity conversion:**

$$V = 130 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{130}{3.6} = 36.11 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic pressure:**

$$q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (36.11)^{2} = 0.6125 \times 1303.93 = 798.66 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Front lift force:**

$$F_{L,f} = q \cdot C_{L,f} \cdot A = 798.66 \times 0.12 \times 2.6 = 249.18 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Rear lift force:**

$$F_{L,r} = q \cdot C_{L,r} \cdot A = 798.66 \times 0.18 \times 2.6 = 373.77 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Net pitching moment:**

$$M_{pitch} = (249.18 \times 1.45) - (373.77 \times 1.65)$$

$$M_{pitch} = 361.31 - 616.72 = -255.41 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Handling interpretation:**

The negative moment means the SUV pitches **nose-down** at speed. The front tires are *additionally* loaded (good for steering feel) but the rear tires *lose* vertical load (bad for traction and high-speed cornering). This is the classic SUV handling signature that manufacturers mitigate with rear spoilers and active aero.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B – 14 marks):**
> • Sign convention for $M_{pitch}$ is critical — write it explicitly.
> • When using *combined* front and rear coefficients, the reference area is the same (full vehicle A); do not double-count.
> • Convert km/h to m/s before any computation.
> • Always end with a one-line *engineering interpretation* — KTU examiners reward physical insight (≈ 1–2 marks).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- SUVs have **higher $C_D$ (0.35–0.45)** and **larger $A$ (2.4–3.0 m²)** than sedans — the product $A_D$ can be **~85% higher**.
- The **single largest drag region** on an SUV is the **roof trailing edge → vertical tailgate wake**.
- The drag force equation is $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} C_D A$; aerodynamic power scales as $V^3$.
- The **lift coefficient** on an SUV is positive (unfavorable) at both axles; the **rear lift** typically exceeds the **front lift** because of base-wake suction.
- **Pitching moment** is critical because the high C.G. amplifies its handling impact; negative moment = nose-down = loss of rear grip.
- **Crosswind sensitivity** is high in SUVs because of the tall, bluff side profile; side-force derivatives are larger than for sedans.
- **Drag-reduction strategies:** active grille shutters, sloped windshield, tapered/Kammback rear, underbody flat panels, rear spoiler, lowered ride height at speed, aero side mirrors, wheel-arch liners.
- **Use** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **at ISA standard conditions.**
- The **drag area** $A_D = C_D \cdot A$ is a single-metric figure of merit and a favourite KTU exam ask.
- An **A-pillar vortex** forms because the windward pillar impinges into the cross-flow; it is responsible for SUV wind noise and contributes to side-window drag.
- KTU exam success: **always convert units, state formulas before substitution, give engineering interpretation, and end with a one-line conclusion.**
